quick_hits: Florida's entire population traces back to a single flock of eleven birds that wintered near Sarasota in 1981. The founding group is believed to have crossed the Gulf of Mexico from the Yucatan Peninsula. From that foothold, the species now breeds across virtually the entire state.

The pink bill and legs are impossible to miss — no other duck in Florida combines that electric pink hardware with a chestnut body and jet-black belly. Males and females are identical in plumage, which is unusual for ducks and more typical of geese and swans.

This species thinks nothing of perching in trees, on telephone lines, or along fence rails — habits that earned it an earlier name, the tree duck. The long legs that let it do this also give it an upright, almost goose-like stance on the ground.

Both parents share incubation duties equally — a trait far more common in geese and swans than in ducks. They also form lifelong pair bonds, and the oldest banded individual on record lived at least ten years and seven months.
species.range_and_origin: The Black-bellied Whistling-Duck's original breeding range ran from southern Texas and Mexico south through Central America to northern Argentina. Beginning in the late 1970s, birds began expanding northward and eastward on their own, and Florida's founding flock — thought to have crossed the Gulf of Mexico from the Yucatan — arrived near Sarasota in 1981. From those eleven birds, the species spread steadily; the Florida Breeding Bird Atlas II documented breeding records across 342 quadrangles statewide, with the central peninsula especially well occupied. FWC now treats the species as a year-round resident throughout peninsular Florida, and Manatee County sits squarely in the heart of its established range. The species is considered a self-colonizing naturalized resident and is treated as native by Florida wildlife managers.
more_information: Something about this duck defies easy categorization. It looks like a duck, but its goose-like proportions, upright posture, long pink legs, and habit of perching in trees all set it apart.

Add the fact that both sexes are identically patterned and share incubation duties equally, and you have a bird that sits somewhere between a duck and a goose in its life history — which is exactly where the whistling-ducks as a group belong evolutionarily.

Nesting is an exercise in opportunism. The preferred site is a natural tree cavity, 4 to 20 feet above the ground, but nest boxes are adopted readily, and the species has also nested in chimneys, barns, and on the ground in dense low vegetation near water.

Females sometimes practice egg-dumping — laying eggs in a neighbor's nest — so a single cavity nest may hold far more eggs than one pair could incubate alone; clutches of 50 or more eggs from multiple females have been recorded. Ducklings leave the nest within a day or two of hatching, dropping to the ground and immediately feeding themselves, though they remain with both parents for weeks.

The Florida expansion of this species is one of the more remarkable range-change stories in recent North American ornithology. From a single flock near Sarasota in 1981, now thought to have originated in the Yucatan Peninsula, the birds colonized south and central Florida steadily, with breeding first confirmed in Hardee County in 1990.

The reason for the expansion is not fully understood — most other wetland species have seen range contractions over the same period — but adaptability to urban ponds, golf courses, suburban parks, and agricultural fields has clearly helped.

blocks.label: Size and shape
blocks.text: A large, long-legged, long-necked duck that stands more upright than most — roughly the size of a Cattle Egret. Length 18.5 to 21 in (47 to 53 cm); wingspan about 30 to 37 in (76 to 94 cm). The upright, goose-like posture is the first thing you notice.

blocks.label: Plumage
blocks.text: Adults are rich chestnut-brown on the breast, back, and neck cap, with a jet-black belly and tail. The face and upper neck are pale gray, with a neat white eye-ring. Wings are dark with a bold white stripe formed by the secondary feathers — striking in flight, visible as a white band when the wings are folded.

blocks.label: Bill and legs
blocks.text: Bright pink bill and long pink legs; these are the most instantly field-identifiable features at any distance. Males and females are identical in appearance.

blocks.label: Juveniles
blocks.text: Young birds are duller and browner overall, with a gray bill and pale legs and lack the sharply defined black belly of adults. The white wing stripe is still present and useful for identification.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: Broad wings, long neck stretched forward, and long legs trailing behind the tail create a distinctive gangly silhouette unlike any other local waterfowl. The white wing patch flashes prominently, and the birds call almost continuously while airborne.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look for a large, upright duck with a pink bill and pink legs — nothing else in Florida combines those features. At rest, the chestnut breast and black belly are diagnostic; in flight, watch for the broad white wing stripe and the dangling pink legs extending beyond the short tail. Juveniles are duller but still show the white wing mark. The whistling call, heard constantly when flocks are moving, is often the first clue they are nearby.

species.diet: Predominantly plant material — seeds of grasses, smartweed, sedges, amaranth, aquatic plants, and agricultural grains including sorghum, millet, corn, and rice. In shallow water they dabble at the surface or tip up to reach submerged vegetation. Insects, snails, and other invertebrates make up less than ten percent of the diet. Foraging happens on land and in water, and often at night.
species.behavior: Highly social year-round, forming flocks that can number in the hundreds. Unusually for a duck, they spend considerable time perching in trees, on fence posts, telephone lines, and low snags above water — a habit that earned them the former name tree duck. They form lifelong pair bonds, and both parents share incubation and brood care. Most foraging occurs at night; during daylight hours flocks typically loaf along wetland edges, preen, and rest. Flight is relatively slow with shallow wingbeats, and the feet trail noticeably behind the tail — a reliable flight character. Pairs form in winter; courtship involves neck-stretching, bill-dipping, and water-flicking displays.
species.sounds: One of Florida's more audible waterbirds. The signature call is a high-pitched, multi-note whistle — often described as a clear waa-choo or a rapid series of peeping whistles — delivered on the wing and on the ground alike. Flocks in flight produce a continuous chorus that carries well and typically announces their presence before they come into sight. On the ground, birds maintain a running soft chatter of peeps and chirps among themselves.
species.ecological_role: As primarily herbivorous grazers, these ducks consume seeds and aquatic plant material in wetland and agricultural settings, influencing local plant communities and dispersing seeds. Eggs, ducklings, and adults are preyed upon by raccoons, rat snakes, opossums, and raptors, placing the species in the middle of the local food web. Their use of tree cavities can bring them into competition with Wood Ducks for nesting sites, though the two species often coexist. In turn, their readily adopted nest boxes provide a model for supporting cavity-nesting waterbirds in managed urban and suburban wetlands.
species.habitat: Shallow freshwater ponds, marshes, and wetlands bordered by trees — particularly areas with standing or dead trees suitable for cavity nesting. Also highly comfortable in human-modified landscapes: urban retention ponds, golf courses, suburban parks, agricultural fields, and flooded pastures. FWC notes they can adapt to almost any habitat in rural and urban areas as long as water and nearby tree cover are available.
species.where_to_look: Check any open pond or wet margin in the park, especially near larger trees that might provide perch sites. This species spends much of the day loafing rather than foraging, so scan the shoreline edges and the branches overhanging water. At dusk, watch for flocks lifting off and heading toward feeding areas — and listen first, since the whistling call announces them reliably. They are tame and approachable by waterfowl standards.
species.when_to_see: Year-round residents in Manatee County and present at all hours, though most active foraging occurs after dark. During daylight, they are most visible in the early morning and late afternoon when loafing along water edges or making short local flights. In Florida, ducklings are most conspicuous in August and September, the species being a relatively late nester. Numbers may swell locally in late summer and fall as post-breeding flocks congregate.

size.length: 18.5 to 21 in (47 to 53 cm); wingspan 30 to 37 in (76 to 94 cm)
size.lifespan: Typically 6 to 8 years in the wild; the oldest banded individual on record lived at least 10 years and 7 months

danger.people: Harmless. No bite of any consequence, no toxins, no aggressive behavior toward people.

interaction.guidance: Observe freely — these birds are notably tolerant of people and easy to watch at close range. Do not feed them; human food does not benefit waterfowl and can make them dependent on unnatural food sources.

invasive.notes: A self-colonizing naturalist that arrived in Florida under its own power. FWC and the Florida Breeding Bird Atlas treat it as an established resident. It has integrated into local wetland habitats and is not considered invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern (IUCN). Global population estimated at 1.1 to 2 million birds and increasing. The North American population has grown substantially since the 1950s and continues to expand northward. Not listed as threatened at the federal, state, or local level.

seasonality.peak: August to September (ducklings most visible; post-breeding flocks)
seasonality.note: Year-round resident throughout peninsular Florida, including Manatee County. Single-brooded in Florida; ducklings are conspicuous in August and September. Flocks may be larger and more visible in late summer and fall as family groups consolidate.

quick_hits: Florida's largest butterfly — and the largest in North America — the Eastern Giant Swallowtail is hard to miss when it glides through the park. Its wingspan regularly exceeds five inches, and its bold black-and-yellow pattern is unmistakable on the wing.

Its caterpillars are called 'orangedogs' because they feed on citrus leaves and can defoliate a young tree in short order. Look closely at the larva, though, and you'll see one of nature's better disguises: it mimics a bird dropping so convincingly that most predators ignore it entirely.

When a caterpillar feels threatened, it everts a bright red-orange, Y-shaped organ called the osmeterium from just behind its head. The organ mimics a snake's tongue visually, and it releases a chemical cocktail — including isobutyric acid and 2-methylbutyric acid — that smells like rancid, sour citrus and drives off ants, wasps, and other invertebrate predators.

Florida is one of the few places in the eastern United States where this butterfly can overwinter and fly year-round. Most of the continent sees only one or two brief flight seasons; here you can find adults in any month.
species.range_and_origin: The Eastern Giant Swallowtail is native to eastern North America, ranging from southern New England and the Great Lakes south through Florida, the Caribbean, and into Mexico. It is common throughout the entire state of Florida and is one of the most frequently seen large butterflies in the region. Florida and the deep South are the only parts of the eastern United States where the species can overwinter and remain active year-round. Populations once thought to extend west to South America are now recognized as a separate species, the Western Giant Swallowtail (Papilio rumiko), based on DNA evidence; the butterfly at Palma Sola is the eastern form.
more_information: There is something almost casual about the way this butterfly moves. Because of its large wingspan, it can glide surprisingly long distances on still wings between slow, powerful beats — a style of flight that sets it apart from most butterflies, which flicker and dart. When it drifts through a garden, it tends to hold your eye in a way that smaller species simply don't.

Host plant fidelity is the key to understanding where you'll find caterpillars. Females lay eggs on plants in the citrus and rue family (Rutaceae) — in Florida that means both ornamental citrus trees and native species like wild lime (Zanthoxylum fagara) and Hercules' club (Zanthoxylum clava-herculis).
The round eggs have a rough, orange-tinged coating that makes them look, fittingly, like tiny oranges.

The caterpillar's bird-dropping disguise is one of the animal kingdom's more convincing tricks. Young and mature larvae alike are patterned in glossy gray, brown, and white with a creamy saddle mark that completes the illusion at every stage. A caterpillar resting motionless on a citrus stem in full daylight is easy to walk right past — which is, of course, the point.

If the disguise fails, the caterpillar deploys a backup that is equally impressive. It everts the osmeterium — a Y-shaped, red-orange glandular organ — from a slit just behind the head. The organ floods the air with volatile acids, including isobutyric acid and 2-methylbutyric acid, producing a pungent, rancid-citrus odor that deters ants, wasps, and other invertebrate attackers.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: The largest butterfly in North America. Wingspan typically 4 to 6.5 inches (10 to 16.5 cm); occasional individuals reach nearly 7 inches. Both sexes are large, with females often running slightly larger than males.

blocks.label: Upperside
blocks.text: Wings are dark brown to black with two bold diagonal bands of yellow spots — one crossing the forewings, one sweeping across the hindwings — that meet near the body to form a rough X-shape. Hindwings have yellow-centered tails and a small blue-and-red spot near the inner margin.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: Largely yellow with black borders and blue scaling near the body — a striking contrast to the dark upperside. When perched with wings open, the yellow underside is the most visible surface. A small red patch on the ventral hindwing helps distinguish this species from the similar but much rarer Schaus' Swallowtail.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: The combination of large size, dark wings, and yellow banding is diagnostic. The leisurely, gliding flight is distinctive — broad wings held flat, long slow beats interspersed with extended glides.

blocks.label: Caterpillar
blocks.text: Brown, gray, and white with a creamy saddle mark — a convincing bird-dropping mimic at every stage. Mature larvae may also show faint eyespot-like markings. When disturbed, the orange-red forked osmeterium everts from just behind the head.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look for a very large, dark butterfly with a prominent yellow-banded X across the wings. The gliding, unhurried flight style is as much a field mark as the pattern — no other common Florida butterfly of this size moves quite like it. At rest with wings spread, the yellow underside is prominent; the small red spot near the hindwing tail is a reliable clincher. On host plants, search for the bird-dropping caterpillar on upper leaf surfaces or along stems.

species.diet: Caterpillars feed on the foliage of plants in the citrus and rue family (Rutaceae). Native Florida hosts include wild lime (Zanthoxylum fagara) and Hercules' club (Zanthoxylum clava-herculis); common rue, hop tree, and ornamental citrus species are also used. Adults drink nectar from a wide range of flowers, with lantana, azalea, goldenrod, bougainvillea, swamp milkweed, and Japanese honeysuckle among the documented sources.
species.behavior: Adults are strong, swift fliers but spend much of their time gliding rather than flapping — the large wingspan lets them cover ground efficiently with minimal effort. Males establish patrol routes through wooded edges and citrus groves in search of females; courtship and mating typically occur in the afternoon. Females lay eggs singly on the upper surface of host plant leaves. Caterpillars are nocturnal feeders, resting motionless on stems or leaf surfaces during daylight in their bird-dropping disguise. Multiple broods are produced throughout the year in Florida.
species.sounds: Essentially silent, as with all butterflies. No calls, songs, or vocalizations at any life stage; the soft rush of large wings in flight is the only sound associated with this species.
species.ecological_role: As caterpillars, Eastern Giant Swallowtails are consumers of Rutaceae foliage and in turn serve as prey for birds, parasitoid wasps and flies, spiders, and predatory insects. The elaborate bird-dropping disguise and osmeterium reflect a long evolutionary arms race with those predators. As adults, they visit a wide variety of flowers and function as pollinators. They sit squarely in the middle of Florida's garden and woodland food web, connecting native host plants both upward to insectivorous birds and downward to leaf tissue.
species.habitat: Forest margins, citrus groves, suburban gardens, parks, and open woodland edges — essentially any landscape that supports plants in the Rutaceae family. In central and south Florida the species is equally at home in native scrub and hammock edges as in ornamental plantings.
species.where_to_look: Check any flowering garden bed for nectaring adults, especially plants with larger open blooms — lantana, pentas, and azalea are reliable draws. Scan the canopy edges and open sky above the treeline for the characteristic gliding silhouette. On native Zanthoxylum shrubs or any citrus trees in the park, examine upper leaf surfaces carefully for the cryptic caterpillar. Adults often come to eye level when nectaring, making them easy to observe at close range.
species.when_to_see: Year-round in Manatee County, with adults present in every month. Multiple broods mean there is rarely a gap in the adult flight. Activity peaks during warm, sunny mornings and afternoons; on cooler winter days adults are less visible but not absent. Spring and summer tend to produce the most conspicuous numbers.

size.length: Wingspan 4 to 6.5 in (10 to 16.5 cm), occasionally to nearly 7 in (18 cm); body length roughly 1 to 1.5 in
size.lifespan: Adult lifespan typically 2 to 4 weeks; full life cycle from egg through adult takes roughly 4 to 8 weeks depending on temperature

danger.people: Completely harmless to people. No sting, no meaningful bite, no toxins. The caterpillar's osmeterium secretion is a mild chemical deterrent aimed at small invertebrates and is not a hazard to humans.

interaction.guidance: Observe as closely as you like — nectaring adults are often tolerant of a careful approach. Avoid handling butterflies, as contact can damage the microscopic wing scales. Caterpillars on host plants are safe to observe but best left in place.

invasive.notes: A native species with deep roots in Florida's ecosystems. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern (IUCN). Ranked G5 (globally secure) by NatureServe. Common and widespread throughout eastern North America; not listed as threatened at the federal or Florida state level. No special conservation protections required.

seasonality.note: Year-round resident and flier in Florida, including Manatee County. Multiple broods mean adults are present in virtually every month; cooler winter days may reduce visibility but adults can appear on warm days even in January and February.

species.behavior: Adults are notably sedentary compared with most skippers, often perching motionless on low vegetation — palm fronds, broad leaves, garden flowers — for extended periods. Flight is direct and moderately swift when disturbed but not the rapid, erratic darting typical of smaller skippers. Caterpillars construct individual tube-shaped shelters on palm fronds by rolling and silk-binding a section of leaf, retreating inside by day and feeding at night. Three to four broods are produced per year in Florida.

conservation.status: Not listed as threatened or of special concern. Common and well established throughout peninsular Florida and the West Indies; has benefited from the widespread planting of ornamental palms.

danger.people: Completely harmless. No stinger, no bite of consequence, no toxins.

species.diet: Caterpillars feed on the leaves of various palms, including saw palmetto (Serenoa repens), cabbage palm (Sabal spp.), date palm, and coconut palm. Adults drink nectar from large, tubular flowers; hibiscus is a well-documented favorite, and the species' long proboscis lets it reach deep into blossoms that shorter-tongued insects cannot access.
species.ecological_role: As a caterpillar, the Monk Skipper is a minor consumer of palm foliage and becomes prey for birds, spiders, and parasitic wasps. As an adult, it functions as a pollinator of the flowers it visits, particularly larger tubular blooms. It sits within a straightforward food web linking Florida's ubiquitous native and ornamental palms upward to insectivorous birds and invertebrate predators.
species.habitat: Subtropical woodland edges, hammocks, coastal scrub, pinelands, salt marsh edges, and suburban gardens and parks — essentially any landscape that supports palms, native or ornamental. The species has adapted readily to human-modified environments wherever palms are planted.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: A large skipper — wingspan 1 7/8 to 2 3/8 inches (about 4.8 to 6 cm). Females tend to run slightly larger than males.

blocks.label: Upperside
blocks.text: Males are dark, nearly black-brown with a gray stigma (scent patch) on the forewing and a warm orange-brown wash along the base of the leading edge. Females are somewhat paler brownish-black with a diffuse pale patch on the forewing.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: Both sexes share a rich mahogany-chestnut red on the hindwing underside — the most distinctive mark when the butterfly is perched with wings folded. The head, thorax, and abdomen show blue-gray hair-scales.

blocks.label: Resting posture
blocks.text: Wings are typically held flat and folded, not spread, giving a broad, triangular outline reminiscent of a moth at rest. This is the most reliable field character for finding a perched individual.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look for a large, dark butterfly sitting very still on a palm frond or low shrub, wings folded flat. The mahogany underwing and blue-gray furry thorax distinguish it from smaller, more uniformly brown skippers. Females show a faint pale forewing patch; males are darker overall with a gray stigma. The calm, unhurried perching style is as much a field mark as any color pattern.

interaction.guidance: Observe from as close as you like — this species is famously tolerant of approach when perched. Avoid touching or handling butterflies, as it can damage the delicate wing scales.

invasive.notes: A naturalized colonist from Cuba rather than a Florida native, but it has integrated into local ecosystems without displacing native species and is not considered invasive.

more_information: Among Florida's skippers, the Monk is unusually easy to appreciate at a standstill. Rather than the rapid, darting flight typical of many family members, adults spend long periods perched with their wings folded flat against low leaves and stems — the posture giving it a distinctly moth-like silhouette.
That patience is your opportunity: get close, and you can see the rich chestnut-brown of the underwings and the blue-gray scaling on the thorax and abdomen.

The Monk Skipper is essentially tied to palms at every stage of its early life. Females lay eggs on palm fronds, and the caterpillars construct leaf-roll shelters by cutting a section of frond and binding the edges with silk — a tidy cylinder in which they rest during the day and from which they emerge to feed at night.
Florida's abundance of both native palms (saw palmetto, cabbage palm) and ornamental species has given the species a very comfortable home far from its Cuban origins.

Adults have notably long probosces for their size, well adapted for probing deep tubular flowers. Hibiscus is a frequently cited nectar source, and in Cuba adults have been recorded visiting lantana, blue porterweed, Spanish needles, and bougainvillea. At Palma Sola, any garden bed with large, open-throated blossoms is worth checking.
quick_hits: One of the largest skippers you will see in the park — dark brown, broad-winged, and unusually calm. It tends to perch motionless on low vegetation for long stretches, making it one of the easiest butterflies here to actually study up close.
The name comes from the same source as cappuccino: Capuchin friars, whose brown robes the butterfly's rich chestnut coloring was thought to resemble.
Its caterpillars eat only palms, so wherever ornamental and native palms thrive, the Monk Skipper is likely not far behind. Florida's suburban palm gardens have suited it very well.
Originally a Cuban species, the Monk Skipper established itself in Florida from strays crossing the Florida Straits — the first confirmed breeding population appeared in 1947.
species.range_and_origin: The Monk Skipper is native to Cuba and the wider West Indies, where it remains widespread and common. Strays began reaching southern Florida in the mid-twentieth century, and an established breeding population was first confirmed in 1947. Today it is resident throughout peninsular Florida and the Keys, with the UF/IFAS Florida Butterfly Gardening database listing Manatee County among its documented counties. Farther south it flies year-round; in central Florida, including this area, adults are recorded from at least March through December.

seasonality.note: Year-round in south Florida; in central Florida (including Manatee County) recorded March through at least December across multiple broods. May appear in January and February during mild winters.

size.length: Wingspan 1 7/8 to 2 3/8 in (4.8 to 6 cm); body length roughly 3/4 to 1 in
size.lifespan: Adult lifespan of days to a few weeks, as is typical for skippers; full life cycle (egg through adult) not precisely documented for this species

species.sounds: Essentially silent, as with virtually all butterflies. Produces no song or call; the soft rustle of wing movement is the only sound associated with this species.
species.when_to_see: Present in Manatee County for most of the year, with adults recorded from at least March through December and possibly in warm winter months as well. Multiple broods mean there is rarely a long gap between flights. Adults are most active and visible during morning and afternoon hours on warm, sunny days.
species.where_to_look: Check the undersides of palm fronds and the broad leaves of shrubs throughout the park, especially near saw palmetto stands or any ornamental palm plantings. Garden beds with large tubular flowers — hibiscus in particular — are good spots to find adults nectaring. This is a butterfly that rewards slow walking rather than scanning; look for something sitting very still that you might otherwise take for a fallen leaf.
species.native_notes: Choranthus capucinus (syn. Asbolis capucinus) is native to Cuba and the Bahamas. It was first recorded as an established population in Florida in 1947, originating from strays from Cuba, and is considered introduced in Florida and the southeastern United States — not a Florida native.

species.internal_notes: Imported 2026-06-23 from iNaturalist observations (4 obs at PSBP).

quick_hits: Unlike our year-round residents like the Red-bellied Woodpecker, the Yellow-bellied Sapsucker is the only eastern woodpecker that is a true long-distance migrant — breeding in northern forests and traveling south to spend the winter with us here in Florida.

A genuine sap farmer: it drills organized, horizontal rows of small holes (sapwells) in tree bark, maintains them daily to keep the sugary sap flowing, then laps up the liquid — along with any trapped insects — with a specialized, brush-like tongue.

Those sapwells double as a neighborhood buffet. Hummingbirds, butterflies, bats, and other birds regularly stop in for a free sip. Ruby-throated Hummingbirds actually time their spring migration to follow sapsuckers northward, relying on these wells for fuel before spring flowers bloom.

Despite the name, the yellow belly is often surprisingly faint — sometimes closer to off-white or soft cream against the trunk. The single best field mark is the bold, vertical white stripe running down the side of the folded wing.
species.range_and_origin: The Yellow-bellied Sapsucker breeds across Canada, eastern Alaska, and the northeastern United States, extending south through the Appalachians. It winters in the southeastern U.S., the West Indies, and Central America — making Florida a key wintering destination. It is the most migratory woodpecker in eastern North America, with essentially no overlap between its summer and winter ranges. Most individuals depart Florida by late April.
more_information: Few birds manage their food supply as methodically as the Yellow-bellied Sapsucker. It drills two types of wells: deep, round holes tapped into the xylem to catch early-spring sap moving up the trunk, and shallower rectangular wells in the phloem that must be continuously re-drilled to keep flowing. A single bird may maintain wells in dozens of trees simultaneously, spending more than half its active hours tending them, chasing off hummingbirds, bats, and other opportunists drawn to the sticky bonanza.

The sapsucker has been documented drilling wells in more than 1,000 species of trees and woody plants. In urban South Florida, researchers found strong preference for black olive and tropical almond trees, with sapwells sometimes extending to within a meter of the ground. At Palma Sola, keep an eye on live oaks, wax myrtles, dahoon hollies, and pines — the trees most often targeted locally.

Ecologists call the Yellow-bellied Sapsucker a "double keystone" species. Its sap wells feed an entire community of animals that cannot make their own, and the nest cavities it excavates (often in aspen or birch up north) are later taken over by flying squirrels, bats, and cavity-nesting birds. Even in Florida, where it is only a winter guest, its daily foraging leaves a visible and lasting mark on the trees it visits.

blocks.label: Summary
blocks.text: A robin-sized woodpecker, boldly patterned in black and white. Both sexes have a bright red forehead cap and a long vertical white stripe running down the folded wing — the single best field mark. Males add a vivid red throat patch; females show white there instead. The underparts are whitish with a soft, understated yellow wash on the belly (often so faint it reads as off-white or pale cream — don't expect a bright yellow). The back is mottled black and white, and a black crescent marks the chest.

blocks.label: In Flight
blocks.text: Flies in the typical woodpecker undulating, bounding pattern. The broad white wing stripe flashes prominently and is often the first thing seen as the bird moves between trees.

blocks.label: Juveniles
blocks.text: Brownish overall with a finely spotted crown and the same bold white wing stripe present in adults.

blocks.label: Tip
blocks.text: Even if you miss the bird, look for the evidence: neat horizontal rows of small, weeping holes in tree bark are the sapsucker's calling card.

identification.what_to_look_for: Red forehead cap (both sexes); bold vertical white wing stripe; red throat in males, white in females; soft yellow belly wash (often very faint — closer to off-white or pale cream); black chest crescent; mottled black-and-white back. Robin-sized. Often sits motionless against a trunk for long stretches — look carefully at bark, not just moving shapes.

species.diet: Tree sap is the dietary cornerstone — specifically the sugary tree sap drawn from self-drilled sapwells. Also eats insects (especially ants) and spiders gleaned from bark, as well as berries and fruit, particularly October through February. Insects caught in the sticky sap around wells are scooped up alongside the sap itself.
species.behavior: Spends most of its time on tree trunks, hitching up and down in typical woodpecker fashion and leaning back on its stiff tail for support. Highly territorial around its sapwells, actively chasing off hummingbirds and other birds that try to steal sap. Also sallies out from branch tips to catch flying insects flycatcher-style. Generally solitary in winter; flies with an undulating, bounding woodpecker rhythm.
species.sounds: Quiet for most of its Florida winter stay. Listen for soft mewing or whining notes — a cat-like "mew" — when disturbed. Also produces an irregular, stuttered drumming pattern (sometimes likened to Morse code) that becomes more frequent as spring departure approaches. Will drum on metal surfaces like signs and chimney flashing, amplifying the sound considerably.
species.ecological_role: A keystone species even in its winter range. Its sapwells provide sugary food for hummingbirds, bats, and other birds that cannot drill their own. Insect predation helps regulate bark-dwelling arthropod populations. Intensive feeding can occasionally girdle small trees, creating snags that in turn become habitat for cavity-nesting species.
species.plant_connections: Forages in and around various trees and shrubs.

size.lifespan: Typically 6–7 years in the wild; record is 7 years, 9 months.

species.habitat: In winter, uses a broad range of wooded habitats — deciduous forests, mixed woodlands, parks, suburban trees, orchards, and even palm groves. Less selective than on its boreal breeding grounds; found in any setting with mature trees large enough to support sapwells.
species.where_to_look: Scan tree trunks slowly — this bird blends into bark and often sits motionless. Focus on medium to large hardwoods: live oaks, wax myrtles, dahoon hollies, and pines are locally favored. The surest approach is to find the sapwells first — look for orderly horizontal rows of small weeping holes in bark — then wait nearby for the owner to return.
species.when_to_see: Present at Palma Sola from October through April, with peak activity in the heart of winter (December–February). Diurnal; most active during morning hours while tending sapwells. Nearly silent in December and January; listen for mewing calls and irregular drumming in February and March as individuals prepare to migrate north, with stragglers possible into April.

seasonality.note: Breeds far to the north; winters in Florida, typically October through April.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

invasive.notes: Not native to Florida — it is a winter visitor that breeds far to the north — but it is a natural, long-established part of Florida's winter avifauna. It is not invasive and is protected under the federal Migratory Bird Treaty Act.

conservation.status: Least Concern (IUCN). Estimated 14 million breeding individuals. Protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act; it is illegal to capture, kill, or possess one without a federal permit.

quick_hits: A large, colorful flycatcher with an olive back, a lemon-yellow belly, and a rusty tail - and a famous habit of weaving shed snakeskins into its tree-cavity nest.
Listen before you look. This bird doesn't sing so much as shout: a loud, rising "WHEEP!" that rings through the canopy and usually gives it away long before you spot it.
A genuine aerial acrobat - it perches dead still, then bursts out to snatch a flying insect mid-air with an audible snap of the bill before swinging back to its branch.
Keep your eyes up. This is a canopy bird that rarely drops to the ground, so scan the treetops for that flash of yellow against the green.
species.range_and_origin: Breeds across the eastern United States and southern Canada and winters in the Caribbean, Mexico, and South America. In our area it is a summer breeding resident - present spring through late summer and gone by fall.
more_information: The Great Crested Flycatcher is a cavity nester, raising its young inside holes in trees (and readily using nest boxes). It is famous for lining the nest with a shed snakeskin - and scientists think this is deliberate intimidation: the snakeskin may help scare off mice, squirrels, and other small raiders eyeing the eggs. When no snakeskin is available, these birds improvise with crinkly substitutes that mimic it: plastic wrappers, onion skins, and cellophane have all turned up in their nests.

It is a true flycatcher, hunting on the wing. From a still perch it watches for flying insects, then launches into a quick aerial chase, snapping the prey out of the air with an audible clack of the bill before looping back - pure canopy acrobatics.

blocks.label: Plumage
blocks.text: Olive-brown above with a gray throat and breast and a bright lemon-yellow belly. The tail and wings flash rufous (rusty) - a good field mark.

blocks.label: Shape
blocks.text: A large, big-headed flycatcher with a slight bushy crest and an upright posture when perched.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look up. This is a canopy and mid-story bird that rarely comes to the ground - scan the treetops for a flash of yellow belly and a rusty tail, and let the loud rising "WHEEP!" lead your eyes to it.

species.diet: A true flycatcher with a varied appetite. It hunts beetles, crickets, caterpillars, moths, and bees, and readily supplements its diet with wild fruit such as elderberries and blackberries. It feeds mostly by hawking - perching still, spotting a flying insect, then launching out to snap it from the air with an audible clack of the bill before looping back to its perch.
species.behavior: An active canopy hunter that catches insects in flight (hawking) and gleans them from foliage. Vocal and conspicuous by sound, though often hidden in the leaves. A cavity nester that defends its nest hole vigorously.
species.sounds: Loud and vocal. The signature call is a sharp, rising "WHEEP!" that carries through the treetops, along with a rolling "prrrreet." If you hear a bossy, far-carrying shout from high in the canopy, this is often the bird making it.
species.ecological_role: A canopy insect predator that helps keep beetle, moth, and other insect populations in check, and a seed disperser for the fruiting plants it feeds on. As a cavity nester it depends on older trees and dead snags with suitable holes.
species.plant_connections: Forages in and around various trees and shrubs.

size.lifespan: commonly several years; can exceed 10 years in the wild.

species.habitat: Woodlands, parks, and gardens during migration and winter.
species.where_to_look: High in the canopy and mid-story of woodland edges, hammocks, and shaded park trees - rarely on or near the ground.
species.when_to_see: Spring through late summer, when breeding birds are most vocal and active (roughly March to September). They leave by early fall and are absent in winter, so don't look for them on a January walk. Listen for the loud "WHEEP!" to locate one.

seasonality.note: A summer resident in our area - arrives around March or April to breed and departs by early fall for the Caribbean and South America. Absent in winter.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: Crisp and dapper, with bold Oreo-cookie black-and-white head stripes and a bright pinkish-yellow bill - unmistakable, though an uncommon find this far south.
Not a local. These are winter visitors, dropping in after a long haul from breeding grounds way up in Canada and Alaska.
Watch the feet: they do a funny backward double-hop on the ground, kicking away leaves to scratch up seeds like a little feathered wind-up toy.
A brush-pile bird - look low, along weedy edges and thickets, not up in the trees.
species.range_and_origin: Breeds across the far north - Alaska, northern Canada, and high western mountains - and winters across much of the United States and Mexico. In Southwest Florida it's an uncommon winter visitor; you usually have to look for it, here from roughly October to April before it heads back north.
more_information: While many sparrows look like little brown streaks of camouflage, the adult White-crowned Sparrow stands out like it's wearing a tiny cycling helmet. It brings a bit of northern flair down to Florida for the colder months and makes itself right at home in our thickets and brushy edges.

It's a ground forager with a signature move: the "double-scratch," a quick two-footed hop backward that kicks leaf litter aside to uncover seeds. Even in winter, when it isn't nesting, you'll hear it practicing its song or trading sharp chink call notes with others from deep in the bushes.

And consider the trip it made to get here: some White-crowned Sparrows fly more than 2,500 miles from the Alaskan tundra to their wintering grounds - an astonishing haul for a bird that weighs about as much as a few quarters.

blocks.label: The crown
blocks.text: Adults wear unmistakable high-contrast black-and-white head stripes. A bird with brown-and-tan stripes instead isn't a different species - it's a youngster on its first trip south.

blocks.label: Posture
blocks.text: Sits noticeably upright when alert, showing off a sleek, pearly-grey breast.

blocks.label: The double-scratch
blocks.text: On the ground it feeds with the "double-scratch" - a quick two-footed backward hop that kicks leaves away to reveal hidden seeds. It's an inherited, instinctive move: even birds raised without leaf litter do it automatically.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look low, not high. Scan brushy borders, overgrown fence lines, and low shrubbery near open grassy areas for an upright, boldly striped sparrow scratching in the leaf litter.

species.diet: Mainly a seed-eater, especially here in winter: weed seeds, grass seeds, berries, and the odd bit of spilled birdseed. It does take insects too, mostly up north in the breeding season.
species.behavior: A ground-foraging edge bird that scratches through leaf litter with a two-footed backward hop. Spends most of its time low in brush and along weedy borders, rarely up in the canopy.
species.sounds: A sweet, clear whistled song that opens with one or two long notes and ends in a buzzy trill. Even in winter you'll hear it practicing, along with a sharp chink call note from the bushes as birds keep track of one another.
species.ecological_role: A winter seed-eater that helps disperse the seeds of weeds and grasses, and part of the seasonal influx of northern songbirds that pass the colder months in Florida.
species.plant_connections: Forages along brushy edges and weedy ground, feeding on the seeds of grasses, weeds, and low shrubs.

size.length: about 6.5 to 7 in (a fairly large, long-tailed sparrow)
size.lifespan: commonly several years; banded birds have exceeded 13 years.

species.habitat: Brushy edges, thickets, weedy fields, and shrubby borders near open ground - anywhere with low cover to dive into and open soil to scratch for seeds.
species.where_to_look: Look low, not high. Brushy borders, overgrown fence lines, and the low shrubbery near open grassy areas - classic edge habitat, not the canopy.
species.when_to_see: An uncommon winter visitor, generally here from about October to April. Early morning is best, scanning the brushy edges. Don't expect a guaranteed sighting - finding one is a small birding win. Gone by late spring.

seasonality.note: A winter visitor only - arrives around October from breeding grounds in Canada, Alaska, and the western mountains, and heads back north by April. Absent in summer.

interaction.guidance: Because they spend so much time on the ground, give them a little room so they don't feel cornered against a bush. Stay still and they'll usually carry on foraging while you watch.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A tiny, hyperactive, blue-gray bird with a long black-and-white tail it constantly flicks — like a miniature mockingbird on caffeine.

Despite the name, gnats are not a significant part of its diet. It hunts leafhoppers, beetles, caterpillars, flies, small wasps, and spiders — sometimes shaking a leaf-covered twig to flush prey into the open.

Its nest is a masterwork: a compact cup of bark strips and grasses, bound together with spider silk and camouflaged on the outside with pieces of lichen — so convincing it looks like a natural tree knot.
A pair can build up to seven nests in a single breeding season, often dismantling earlier attempts and reusing the materials when predation or mite infestations cause failures.
species.range_and_origin: The Blue-gray Gnatcatcher breeds across most of the eastern United States and parts of the West, extending into southern Ontario, Mexico, and Cuba. Florida is exceptional: the species is a year-round resident here, with local breeders joined by waves of migrants passing through in spring (peaking around April) and fall (peaking around September). Winter concentrations are highest in south Florida and along the Gulf Coast. The breeding range has been shifting northward since the early twentieth century, tracking rising average temperatures — a trend that continues today.
more_information: Everything about the Blue-gray Gnatcatcher reads as superlative for its size. At just 4 to 4.5 inches long and weighing barely more than a nickel, it is one of the smallest songbirds in North America — yet it forages with relentless energy, flitting through the outer foliage of trees and shrubs, hovering briefly to snatch prey, and sallying into the air after escaping insects. The perpetually flicking white-edged tail is both an identification mark and a foraging tool: flashing those white outer feathers startles insects into flight, making them easier to catch.

The species has earned the nickname 'Little Mockingbird' for good reason. Its nasal, wheezy, rambling song weaves snippets of other birds' vocalizations into a continuous warble that can be surprisingly difficult to pin down. The more often-heard call — a thin, insistent pzzzz or zeee — is usually your first clue the bird is nearby, long before you spot it high in the canopy. Males hold two distinct song types: a softer version used in courtship and territory establishment, and a louder, more urgent version deployed in direct confrontations.

Breeding pairs are devoted architects. Both sexes construct an exquisite cup nest, typically saddled on a horizontal branch 20 to 40 feet up, built from plant fibers, bark strips, and feathers, bound with spider silk, and tiled on the outside with crustose lichens for camouflage so effective the nest resembles nothing so much as a natural knot on the branch. When nests fail to predation or parasitism — which happens often — the pair simply disassembles the structure and starts again nearby, sometimes logging up to seven nest attempts in a single season.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Tiny and slender — about 4 to 4.5 inches long, slightly larger than a Ruby-throated Hummingbird and smaller than a House Wren.

blocks.label: Plumage
blocks.text: Blue-gray above, whitish below, with a bold white eye ring. The long tail is black with white outer feathers that make it appear mostly white from below. Breeding males add a narrow black V-shaped line across the forehead extending above the eyes; females and non-breeding birds lack this marking and appear slightly grayer.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Constantly in motion — flitting through outer foliage, cocking and flicking its tail from side to side. The tail action is one of the best field marks: no other small Florida songbird fidgets quite this persistently.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look for a tiny, restless bird high in the mid-story or canopy, its long tail pumping side to side almost non-stop. The white eye ring, white tail edges, and blue-gray upperparts are diagnostic. Listen for the thin, whiny pzzzz call — it usually announces the bird before you see it.

species.diet: Primarily small insects and spiders: leafhoppers, treehoppers, plant bugs, leaf beetles, caterpillars, flies, small wasps, and spider egg cases. Ironically, gnats form no significant part of the diet. Prey is gleaned from outer foliage, snatched in mid-air, or flushed by shaking leaf-covered twigs.
species.behavior: Intensely active foliage gleaner; barely pauses between perches. Forages by hopping and siduling along outer branches, hovering briefly to snatch prey, and making short aerial sallies. The tail is habitually cocked upright or flicked rapidly side to side — both during foraging and territorial display. Fiercely territorial during the breeding season: rivals can be chased up to 70 feet, and confrontations sometimes escalate into breast-to-breast aerial skirmishes. Outside the breeding season, individuals are more tolerant and will join mixed-species foraging flocks.
species.sounds: Most commonly heard as a thin, nasal, whining call — often written as pzzzz or zeee — delivered in short bursts. The song is a soft, rambling series of wheezy warbles and slurs, sometimes incorporating mimicked fragments of other species' songs, earning this bird its 'Little Mockingbird' nickname. Songs are heard mainly during the breeding season; the insistent call notes are given year-round and are typically the first sign of the bird's presence.
species.ecological_role: An important insectivore of the forest mid-story and canopy, suppressing populations of foliage-dwelling insects including several agricultural pests. Also serves as prey for small raptors and snakes, placing it squarely in the middle of the local food web. Its lichen-decorated nest is sometimes dismantled and rebuilt so many times per season that it becomes a net exporter of spider silk and plant fibers back into the habitat.
species.plant_connections: Forages in and around various trees and shrubs.

size.length: 4–4.5 in (10–11 cm); wingspan about 6.3 in (16 cm)
size.lifespan: Typically up to 4 years; the oldest banded individual on record was at least 4 years and 2 months old.

species.habitat: Deciduous and mixed woodlands, pine flatwoods with oak understory, hardwood swamps, riparian edges, and wooded suburban areas. In Florida, birds make use of a wide range of wooded habitats year-round — from sandhills and scrub oak to residential parks with sufficient tree cover. Prefers habitat edges and areas with openings in the canopy.
species.where_to_look: Scan the mid-story and canopy of wooded areas, especially along edges and near water. Listen first — the thin, nasal call note cuts through background noise and reliably betrays the bird's location before it comes into view. Binoculars are essential; the bird moves fast and stays high. Early morning is the most productive time, when feeding activity peaks.
species.when_to_see: Present year-round at Palma Sola Botanical Park, though the individuals here in summer (breeders) differ from those present in winter (migrants from further north). Numbers swell during spring migration (April) and again in fall (September–October). Most active and vocal from shortly after sunrise through mid-morning. Song is heard primarily in the breeding season (spring–early summer); call notes give it away throughout the year.

seasonality.note: Florida hosts year-round residents plus distinct wintering and breeding populations; individual birds present in summer differ from those present in winter as the species is fully migratory across North America.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: The most colorful vireo in eastern North America — bright yellow throat, yellow 'spectacles' around the eyes, olive back, and two crisp white wingbars. Heavier-billed and far more deliberate in its movements than any warbler.

Unlike the park's year-round resident birds, this vireo is a strict migrant in the Bradenton area, passing through Palma Sola twice a year — once in spring (late March to early April) heading north to breed, and again in fall (September to October) heading south to winter in Mexico and Central America.

While they share similar colors with some spring warblers, behavior gives them away: warblers flit and dart constantly, while Yellow-throated Vireos move slowly and methodically through the upper branches, pausing at each perch to inspect bark and foliage.
During their spring stopover, males sing from the treetops — a slow, lazy series of raspy two-syllable phrases with long pauses in between, often transcribed as 'three-EIGHT' or 'CRE-atte.'
species.range_and_origin: The Yellow-throated Vireo breeds across the eastern United States and southern Canada, in open deciduous forests often near streams and lake edges. It winters from southern Mexico and the Caribbean south to northern South America. Florida lies squarely on its migration corridor — the species is a regular spring and fall transient, with spring movement peaking in mid-March through early April and fall birds moving through September into October. A small number linger into early winter, but the species does not regularly overwinter in Florida.
more_information: Of all the vireos, the Yellow-throated is the showiest — a stocky, big-headed songbird wearing a vivid yellow bib and matching yellow spectacles against an olive-green back and gray wings marked with two bold white bars. Despite that brightness, it can be maddeningly hard to spot: it forages slowly and methodically through the upper and mid canopy, often working the bare inner branches of trees rather than the leaf tips, and it pauses for a long time at each perch. Visitors who hear it before they see it are having the typical experience.

The diet is overwhelmingly insect-based — caterpillars, moths, beetles, leafhoppers, cicadas, aphids, and more — gleaned directly from bark, twigs, and foliage. In fall and during migration, the birds round out their diet with berries. On the wintering grounds, individuals defend private feeding territories and will actively chase away other Yellow-throated Vireos, while still joining mixed-species foraging flocks alongside other small birds.

Nesting biology holds a notable surprise: unlike most North American songbirds, both the male and female Yellow-throated Vireo take turns incubating the eggs and brooding the young throughout the day. The nest itself is an elegantly engineered deep cup woven from bark strips, grasses, and plant fibers, bound to a horizontal forked twig with spider silk and decorated on the outside with lichens. The species is a frequent victim of Brown-headed Cowbird brood parasitism — anywhere from a third to half of nests have been found to contain cowbird eggs — and most pairs end up raising the cowbird chick alongside, or instead of, their own young. Some individuals will desert a heavily parasitized nest entirely, but the elaborate nest-floor burial defense famous in the Yellow Warbler is not part of this species' repertoire.

blocks.label: Summary
blocks.text: A chunky, short-tailed songbird with a thick, slightly hooked bill. Bright yellow throat and breast, yellow eye-ring and lores forming bold 'spectacles,' olive-green head and back, gray rump, and two crisp white wingbars on gray wings. White belly and undertail. Sexes look alike.

blocks.label: Confusion species
blocks.text: Most often confused with the Pine Warbler, which shares yellow-and-white underparts and wingbars. Key differences: the vireo's bill is noticeably thicker and slightly hooked; its yellow throat is unstreaked (Pine Warblers show faint dusky streaking on the sides); and its movements are slower and more deliberate.

identification.what_to_look_for: Search the middle and upper canopy, especially in the interior of trees on bare branches rather than leaf tips. The combination of unstreaked bright yellow throat, matching yellow spectacles, two white wingbars, and a stout hooked bill is definitive. If the bird is moving at all, it will be doing so slowly — this is not a warbler dart.

species.diet: Primarily insects: caterpillars, moths, butterflies, beetles, leafhoppers, aphids, cicadas, dragonflies, and many others gleaned from bark, twigs, and foliage. Supplements with berries, especially in fall during migration.
species.behavior: Slow, methodical canopy forager — works the interior branches of tall trees, pausing at each perch to inspect bark and foliage above and below before moving on. Migrates mostly at night. On the wintering grounds, holds a solo feeding territory and actively chases away other Yellow-throated Vireos, while still joining mixed-species flocks of other birds. Both parents share incubation and chick-rearing duties.
species.sounds: Song is a slow series of short, burry two- and three-syllable phrases with rising and falling inflection, delivered at a leisurely pace with pauses between phrases — lower-pitched and huskier than the Red-eyed or Blue-headed Vireo's song. A common rendition is transcribed as 'three-EIGHT' or 'CRE-atte.' Males sing persistently during spring migration. Females produce a harsh scolding chatter during aggressive encounters.
species.ecological_role: An insect predator of the forest canopy, helping regulate populations of caterpillars, beetles, leafhoppers, and other invertebrates during migration stopovers. Occasional fruit consumption may contribute to minor seed dispersal. As a Neotropical migrant, it also serves as a link between Florida's woodland habitats and tropical forest ecosystems from Mexico to South America.
species.plant_connections: Forages in and around various trees and shrubs.

size.lifespan: up to 6–9 years in the wild

species.habitat: Open deciduous forests and forest edges with tall trees, ideally with an open understory and proximity to water. During migration in Florida, uses a variety of wooded habitats including parks, riparian corridors, and mature shade trees in gardens. Avoids dense closed-canopy forest and pure conifer stands.
species.where_to_look: Scan the canopy and mid-story of the park's largest deciduous trees, particularly the interior branches rather than the outer foliage. Slow-moving and easy to overlook despite its bright coloration — listen first for the slow, burry song or the rustle of deliberate foraging, then trace the sound upward. Usually encountered singly.
species.when_to_see: A spring and fall migrant in the Bradenton area. Spring birds move through primarily in late March and early April; fall migrants pass through September into October. Most active during morning hours. Migrates at night, so freshly arrived birds are most visible in early morning at stopover sites.

seasonality.note: Regular spring and fall transient; rare lingerer into early winter. Does not breed in Florida.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: The most abundant warbler in North America — and one of the most reliable winter visitors to Palma Sola. Look for the bright yellow rump patch flashing as it flies away. Birders affectionately call them 'butter-butts.'

They are the only warbler that can digest the waxes in bayberries and wax myrtles. That rare digestive trick lets them winter farther north than any other warbler — and keeps them common across Florida all winter long.

Extraordinarily versatile foragers: they glean insects from bark, hover-snatch flying bugs like a flycatcher, pick berries from shrubs, forage on the ground, and in Florida have even been spotted drinking the juice from fallen oranges.

Two forms exist under one species name. The eastern 'Myrtle' form — the one you'll see here — has a white throat and bold dark cheek patch. The western 'Audubon's' form has a yellow throat; it almost never reaches Florida.
species.range_and_origin: The Yellow-rumped Warbler breeds across the boreal forests of Canada, Alaska, and the northeastern and Appalachian U.S., then migrates south in enormous numbers each fall. The eastern 'Myrtle' subspecies winters throughout the southeastern United States, the Caribbean, and Central America. In Florida, it is one of the two most common winter warblers, appearing on roughly 37% of winter bird checklists statewide and present from late September through April. It does not breed in Florida. Palma Sola Botanical Park lies squarely in the heart of its Florida wintering range.
more_information: Every October, Yellow-rumped Warblers pour into Florida in loose, chattering flocks, filling shrubs and woodland edges with soft check calls and flickers of yellow. Winter birds wear subdued brownish plumage, but the yellow rump — present in every plumage, every age, every sex — never disappears. By April, departing birds are molting into their breeding finery: charcoal gray, black, white, and four distinct yellow patches. Spring passage birds can look almost like a different species.

The secret to this bird's abundance lies in its stomach. Yellow-rumped Warblers possess unusually high concentrations of bile salts that break down the waxy coatings of bayberries, wax myrtle, juniper berries, poison ivy, and dogwood — fruits that other warblers cannot digest. This unique adaptation extends their winter range far beyond any other warbler, and in Florida it means they can exploit native wax myrtle thickets that other species ignore entirely. When insects are scarce, a flock will strip a fruiting shrub with assembly-line efficiency.

Despite their abundance, Yellow-rumped Warblers are genuinely versatile hunters. They glean insects from bark and leaves, flycatch in mid-air on long aerobatic sallies, forage on the ground, and will even skim insects from water surfaces. In the park, scan the mid-story and shrub layer for small gray-brown birds with a startling yellow rump. A sharp check call overhead — slightly flat in the Myrtle form — is often the first clue they are present. Males sing a slow, soft, sweetly whistled trill that speeds up slightly toward the end; singing is most likely during late-winter and spring departure.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Medium-large warbler; 4.7–5.9 inches (12–15 cm) long. Full-bodied with a large head, sturdy bill, and long, narrow tail — noticeably heftier than most other warblers.

blocks.label: Plumage — Winter (what you'll see here)
blocks.text: Brownish-gray above, pale and finely streaked below, with a persistently bright yellow rump patch and small yellow patches on the sides of the chest. The white throat, framed by a dusky cheek patch, is the key field mark for the eastern 'Myrtle' form.

blocks.label: Plumage — Breeding male (April departure birds)
blocks.text: Striking: blue-gray back with black streaks, black face mask, bold white throat, black breast bib, white wing bars, and vivid yellow patches on the crown, sides, and rump.

blocks.label: Females and immatures
blocks.text: Browner and more streaked than males in all seasons, but the yellow rump is always present and is the most reliable single field mark.

identification.what_to_look_for: The bright yellow rump, visible the moment the bird takes flight, is diagnostic in all plumages. Perched birds show a white throat (not yellow — that would indicate the rare western Audubon's form), a dusky cheek patch, and small yellow side patches. In flight, look also for white spots in the outer tail feathers. The sharp, flat check call is distinctive and easily learned.

species.diet: Primarily insectivorous in summer (caterpillars, beetles, gnats, aphids, wasps, grasshoppers, and spiders), but switches heavily to fruit and berries in fall and winter. Key winter foods in Florida include wax myrtle berries, juniper, poison ivy, dogwood, grapes, and Virginia creeper. They also take wild seeds from goldenrod and beach grasses, and will visit feeders for suet, raisins, sunflower seeds, and peanut butter. In Florida they have been recorded drinking the juice from fallen oranges.
species.behavior: Highly active and acrobatic. Forages at multiple heights — outer tree canopies, mid-story shrubs, bark surfaces, and the ground. Sallies out flycatcher-style to snatch flying insects; hover-gleans insects from foliage tips; and will forage along wrack lines on beaches. In winter, typically forages in flocks, sometimes numbering in the thousands. Males tend to forage higher than females. Fans and raises the tail as a territorial display; the yellow rump patch may also function as a passive social signal. Migrates at night.
species.sounds: The most useful sound is the call: a sharp, flat chek (sometimes rendered as check), given constantly while foraging and in flight — often the first indication the bird is present. Males sing a slow, soft, sweetly whistled warble or trill; the pitch is mostly even, rising or falling slightly, and the song speeds up toward the end, lasting 1–3 seconds. Only males sing, mainly on the breeding grounds and during late-winter pre-migration.
species.ecological_role: An important insect predator, consuming large numbers of flying insects, leaf beetles, aphids, and caterpillars — including spruce budworm (a serious forest pest) during outbreak years. In winter, Yellow-rumped Warblers are significant seed dispersers: they consume enormous quantities of native berries (wax myrtle, dogwood, grapes, Virginia creeper) and deposit seeds widely across the landscape. They also participate in mixed-species winter foraging flocks that increase overall foraging efficiency.
species.plant_connections: Forages in and around various trees and shrubs.

size.lifespan: Typically several years; the oldest recorded individual reached at least 10 years.

species.habitat: Breeds in mature coniferous and mixed coniferous-deciduous forests in the far north. In winter — the only time it occurs in Florida — it occupies a wide range of open habitats: woodland edges, shrubby thickets, coastal vegetation, parks, residential areas, dunes with bayberry or wax myrtle, and even open beaches. One of the most habitat-flexible warblers in North America.
species.where_to_look: Check the mid-story and shrub layer throughout the park, particularly near fruiting wax myrtle, cabbage palms with berries, or any dense shrubby edge. Also scan the outer branches of taller trees where the birds frequently sally out after insects. Listen for the flat check call — it carries well and often arrives before the bird is visible. Loose flocks may move through quickly, so a calling bird is often your best cue to stop and scan.
species.when_to_see: Present in Florida from late September through April; most abundant October through March. Active throughout daylight hours; foraging activity is highest in morning and late afternoon. Winter birds are present and conspicuous on almost any walk through the park during those months. Departing birds in late March and April may show bright spring plumage.

seasonality.note: Abundant winter visitor; breeds far to the north in boreal and montane conifer forests. Arrives late September, departs by late April. Recorded in roughly 37% of Florida winter bird checklists.

danger.people: Harmless. A small songbird with no defensive behavior toward humans.

interaction.guidance: Observe quietly from a short distance. Flocks can be approachable; avoid flushing them from fruiting shrubs where they are feeding.

invasive.notes: Native North American species and a natural seasonal visitor to Florida. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern (IUCN). Estimated global breeding population of 170 million; populations stable 1966–2019 per North American Breeding Bird Survey. Rated low conservation concern by Partners in Flight.

quick_hits: A plain, grayish-brown bird that sits upright on an exposed perch, wags its tail, and darts out to catch a flying insect — that is an eastern phoebe.
The tail-wagging is almost constant and is the quickest way to recognize it — few other small birds pump their tail so persistently.
It calls its own name: a burry, two-note 'fee-bee' that it repeats from a favorite perch.
A winter visitor to Florida, it breeds farther north and returns each fall.
species.range_and_origin: Native to eastern North America. Breeds across the eastern U.S. and southern Canada; winters in the southeastern U.S. and Mexico. A winter visitor in the Bradenton area.
more_information: The eastern phoebe is a flycatcher — a bird that hunts by sallying out from a perch to snatch insects in midair, then returning to the same perch. It is one of the most reliable 'sit-and-wait' hunters in the bird world.
The persistent tail-wagging is diagnostic. It pumps its tail almost continuously, whether perched or just after landing — a habit that immediately narrows the ID.
It was the first bird ever banded in North America — John James Audubon tied silver thread to a phoebe's leg in 1804 to see if it would return the next year. It did.

blocks.label: Shape
blocks.text: Upright posture, medium-sized flycatcher. Plain grayish-brown above, pale below.

blocks.label: Tail
blocks.text: Wags persistently — the best field mark.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Small and dark — a flycatcher bill.

blocks.label: Voice
blocks.text: A burry 'fee-bee' repeated from a perch.

identification.what_to_look_for: A plain gray-brown bird on an exposed perch, wagging its tail and darting out after insects.

species.diet: Flying insects — caught by sallying from a perch. Some berries in winter.
species.behavior: Sits on exposed perches, pumps tail, darts out to catch insects. Returns to the same perch. Solitary.
species.sounds: A burry, emphatic 'fee-bee' — says its name.
species.ecological_role: Flying-insect predator; the first bird banded in North America (Audubon, 1804).
species.plant_connections: Perches on exposed branches; eats some berries in winter.
species.habitat: Open areas with perches — forest edges, fences, buildings, park structures.
species.where_to_look: Exposed perches near open ground — fences, low branches, building edges. Watch for the tail-wag.
species.when_to_see: Winter visitor — typically October through March.

seasonality.note: Breeds to the north; winters in Florida.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: The American robin — brick-red breast, dark back, yellow bill — is one of the most recognized birds in North America, but in Florida it is a winter visitor, not a year-round neighbor.
It arrives in large flocks in fall, feeding on berries and worms across lawns and open ground, then heads north to breed.
Despite its name, it is not closely related to the European robin — early colonists just thought the red breast looked familiar.
A robin on a Florida lawn in January is on vacation; by April it is usually back up north nesting.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North America. Breeds across the U.S. and Canada; winters in the southern U.S. including Florida. A seasonal visitor here.
more_information: The American robin is arguably the most familiar bird in North America — but in Florida it is a seasonal guest, arriving in flocks from late fall and departing by spring.
In winter it shifts from its summer diet of earthworms and insects to one dominated by berries and fruit, and large flocks can strip a fruiting tree in hours.
Its presence on a Florida lawn is a good seasonal marker: if you see robins, it is probably winter.

blocks.label: Breast
blocks.text: Warm brick-red to orange.

blocks.label: Back
blocks.text: Dark gray-brown.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Yellow.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Runs across lawns, pauses, tilts head, then pulls up a worm.

identification.what_to_look_for: A plump, upright bird with a red breast hopping across a lawn. In Florida, a winter visitor in flocks.

species.diet: Earthworms and insects (summer); berries and fruit (winter).
species.behavior: Runs across open ground, tilts head to listen for worms. Forms large flocks in winter. Feeds heavily on fruit.
species.sounds: A rich, caroling song ('cheerily, cheer-up, cheerio') — but mostly heard on the breeding grounds, not in Florida.
species.ecological_role: Berry disperser in winter; earthworm and insect predator in summer.
species.plant_connections: Heavy berry consumer in winter — disperses many native and ornamental fruit species.
species.habitat: Lawns, parks, open ground, and berry-producing trees and shrubs.
species.where_to_look: Open lawns and under fruiting trees in winter.
species.when_to_see: Winter visitor — typically November through March.

seasonality.note: Breeds far to the north; present in Florida only in the cold months.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A dragonfly with orange-and-brown banded wings — like a tiny Halloween decoration perched on a plant stem near the pond.
It perches at the very tip of a stem and flutters in the breeze like a flag, which is unusual for a dragonfly — most prefer rigid, still perches.
Males patrol territories over open water, but they spend long stretches resting on stem tips between patrols.
The wing pattern makes it one of the easiest dragonflies to identify at a glance.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the eastern United States. Common in Florida near ponds and wetlands.
more_information: The Halloween pennant is one of the most recognizable dragonflies in the eastern U.S. thanks to its bold orange-and-brown wing bands — a pattern that immediately suggests its common name.
Unlike most dragonflies that prefer rigid perches, it often lands on the tips of flexible stems and sways in the breeze, fluttering like a small pennant or flag.
Like all dragonflies, it is a precision aerial predator, catching small insects in flight.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Orange-amber with dark brown bands — the Halloween pattern.

blocks.label: Perch
blocks.text: Sits at the very tip of flexible stems, swaying in the breeze.

blocks.label: Body
blocks.text: Yellowish to orange body.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Medium dragonfly, wingspan about 3 inches.

identification.what_to_look_for: A dragonfly with boldly banded orange-and-brown wings perched at the tip of a stem near water.

species.diet: Small flying insects, caught in the air.
species.behavior: Perches on flexible stem tips and flutters in the wind. Males patrol open water. Aerial hunter.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: Aerial insect predator; part of the pond-edge food web.
species.plant_connections: Perches on emergent wetland vegetation.
species.habitat: Ponds, marshes, and wetland edges.
species.where_to_look: Stem tips near the ponds. Look for the distinctive wing pattern and swaying perch.
species.when_to_see: A warm-season dragonfly, most common spring through fall.

quick_hits: The enormous, brightly colored grasshopper lumbering across the path — too big and too slow to be any other species — is an eastern lubber.
It can barely fly. Those tiny wings are essentially useless for flight, so it walks, climbs, and hops instead.
The bold yellow, orange, and black coloring is a warning: it is toxic, producing a foul-smelling, noxious foam from its thorax when threatened.
Most predators learn quickly to leave it alone, though a few — loggerhead shrikes among them — have figured out how to eat it safely.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States. Common in Florida year-round.
more_information: The eastern lubber is the unmistakable giant of Florida's grasshoppers — up to three inches long, painted in warning colors, and moving with a slow, confident waddle that says 'I dare you.'
That confidence is earned. When harassed, it hisses, spreads its tiny wings to flash bright colors, and secretes a foul, toxic foam. Most birds and mammals learn to avoid it after one attempt.
Juveniles are jet black with a yellow or orange stripe, looking nothing like the adults. They emerge in spring and grow through several molts into the full adult pattern.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Up to 3 inches — by far the largest grasshopper you will see.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Adults are yellow to orange with black markings. Juveniles are black with a bright stripe.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Tiny, vestigial — cannot support flight.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Slow, deliberate walker. Does not jump far or fly.

identification.what_to_look_for: A very large, brightly colored grasshopper walking slowly along a path or through vegetation. If it is big, slow, and bold, it is a lubber.

species.diet: Broad variety of plants — grasses, herbs, garden plants. Can be a nuisance in gardens.
species.behavior: Walks slowly and deliberately. When threatened, hisses, spreads tiny wings, and produces toxic foam.
species.sounds: A hissing sound when threatened.
species.ecological_role: Herbivore and occasional garden pest. Toxic to most predators, so it has few natural enemies.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on many garden and wild plants.

size.lifespan: about one year (one generation per year).

species.habitat: Gardens, roadsides, fields, and open woodland — common in disturbed habitats.
species.where_to_look: Paths, garden beds, and low vegetation. Hard to miss when present.
species.when_to_see: Most visible spring through fall; juveniles emerge in spring.

seasonality.note: Juveniles appear in spring; adults most visible summer through fall.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.
