quick_hits: A small, dark rabbit with short ears and a tiny brownish tail — not the long-eared cottontail most people picture when they hear 'rabbit.'
It is one of the very few rabbits that swims, and it does so willingly — wading into water to escape predators or to reach better feeding areas.
Unlike cottontails, the marsh rabbit's tail is not white and fluffy — it is small, brown, and inconspicuous.
It is a true Florida native, tied to the state's wetlands, marshes, and dense, damp vegetation.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States from Virginia to Florida. Year-round resident in Florida's wetlands.
more_information: The marsh rabbit is the rabbit of Florida's damp places — swamps, pond edges, marshes, and dense wet thickets. It is smaller and darker than the eastern cottontail, with proportionally shorter ears and a dingy brown tail instead of the cottontail's white powder puff.

Its willingness to swim sets it apart from most rabbits. When threatened, it may plunge into water and swim with only its nose above the surface, or sit motionless in shallow water among emergent vegetation.
It is most active at dawn and dusk, feeding on grasses, sedges, and aquatic plants.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Small — smaller than a cottontail, about 14 to 16 inches.

blocks.label: Ears
blocks.text: Short for a rabbit.

blocks.label: Tail
blocks.text: Small, brown — not the white 'cotton ball' of a cottontail.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Dark brown to blackish-brown, darker than a cottontail.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small, dark rabbit near water with short ears and no white tail. If it runs toward water instead of away, it is a marsh rabbit.

species.diet: Grasses, sedges, aquatic plants, and other low vegetation — a strict herbivore.
species.behavior: Crepuscular — most active at dawn and dusk. Swims readily. Freezes when threatened before bolting.
species.sounds: Generally silent; may let out a high squeal if captured.
species.ecological_role: Herbivore and prey animal — a food source for hawks, owls, bobcats, and snakes.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on grasses, sedges, and wetland plants.

size.lifespan: commonly 1 to 4 years in the wild.

species.habitat: Marshes, wet thickets, pond edges, and dense vegetation near water.
species.where_to_look: Along pond edges and in dense low vegetation, especially at dawn or dusk.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, most visible at dawn and dusk.

seasonality.note: Crepuscular; rarely seen in midday.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: The brown pelican is the one that dives — plunging headfirst from the air into the water to scoop up fish, unlike the white pelican which scoops from the surface.
It is a conservation comeback: nearly eliminated by DDT in the 1960s and 70s, it recovered after the pesticide was banned and was removed from the endangered list in 2009.
The massive bill and expandable throat pouch can hold roughly three gallons of water — which it drains before swallowing the fish.
Despite the big bill, an adult weighs only about eight pounds — surprisingly light for a bird that looks so heavy.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the Atlantic and Pacific coasts of the Americas. Year-round resident along the Florida coast.
more_information: The brown pelican is the only pelican in the world that hunts by plunge-diving. It spots fish from the air, folds its wings, and drops headfirst into the water — hitting at enough speed to stun small fish, which it scoops up in its enormous throat pouch.

Its recovery from DDT is one of the landmark conservation stories. The pesticide thinned eggshells to the point of collapse, and pelican populations crashed. After DDT was banned in 1972, populations slowly recovered, and the species was delisted in 2009.
Gulls and other birds often try to steal fish right from the pelican's pouch as it drains the water — an occupational hazard.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Large, heavy-bodied, with a massive bill and throat pouch.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Gray-brown body, dark belly, white to yellowish head. Breeding adults show a dark chestnut nape.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Flies low over water in lines, alternating flaps and glides.

blocks.label: Feeding
blocks.text: Plunge-dives headfirst — the only pelican that does this.

identification.what_to_look_for: A large brown-and-gray pelican flying low over water or plunge-diving. Distinguished from the white pelican by color and the diving behavior.

species.diet: Fish — caught by plunge-diving from the air.
species.behavior: Plunge-dives for fish. Flies in formation low over the water. Rests on pilings and docks.
species.sounds: Generally silent except at the nest; adults make low grunting sounds.
species.ecological_role: Fish predator in coastal and nearshore waters; an indicator of marine ecosystem health.
species.habitat: Coastal waters, bays, estuaries, and nearshore areas.
species.where_to_look: Overhead or on nearby water — a flyover visitor to the park, not a pond bird. More likely near the coast.
species.when_to_see: Year-round along the coast; occasional flyovers at the park.

seasonality.note: Coastal bird; park sightings are flyovers.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A compact white egret standing in a field next to cattle or mowing equipment — not at the water's edge — is almost certainly a cattle egret.
It is the only egret in Florida that forages primarily on dry land, following large animals and machinery to catch the insects they stir up.
It arrived in the Americas on its own, crossing the Atlantic from Africa in the 1940s and spreading rapidly across the continent — a natural colonizer, not an introduction.
In breeding plumage it flushes with patches of orange-buff on the head, chest, and back.
species.range_and_origin: Originally from Africa. Naturally colonized South America in the 1870s and North America by the 1940s–50s. Now a common year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The cattle egret is a remarkable natural success story. Without any human assistance, it crossed the Atlantic from Africa and colonized the New World, reaching Florida by the 1950s and spreading across the continent within decades.

Its trick is its feeding strategy: instead of hunting in water like other egrets, it follows large animals — cattle, horses, tractors — and snaps up the grasshoppers, crickets, and other insects they disturb. This terrestrial niche was largely unoccupied, which is part of why it spread so fast.
Despite being a recent arrival, it is not considered invasive — it filled an open niche without displacing native species.

blocks.label: Habitat
blocks.text: Dry fields and lawns, not water — the key distinction from other egrets.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Compact and stocky — smaller and thicker-necked than a snowy egret.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Short, stout, yellow to orange.

blocks.label: Breeding
blocks.text: Patches of orange-buff on head, chest, and back.

identification.what_to_look_for: A compact white egret in a field or lawn, often near livestock or mowing equipment. Stockier and shorter-necked than other egrets, with a yellow bill.

species.diet: Insects — grasshoppers, crickets, flies, and other invertebrates flushed by large animals or machinery.
species.behavior: Follows livestock and mowers on foot. Gregarious — often in loose groups. Nests colonially.
species.sounds: Generally quiet; croaks at the colony.
species.ecological_role: Insect predator in agricultural and open-ground habitats. A natural colonizer that filled an unoccupied niche.
species.plant_connections: Nests colonially in trees near water, though it forages far from it.
species.habitat: Fields, lawns, pastures, and open ground — near livestock or mowers.
species.where_to_look: Open grassy areas, especially if mowing is happening nearby. Not at the water's edge.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A medium white egret with a black bill, black legs, and bright yellow feet — as if it is wearing golden slippers.
Those yellow feet are a feeding tool: it shuffles them through the water to stir up small fish and shrimp, then strikes.
A century ago it was nearly wiped out for its lacy breeding plumes, just like the great egret — and its recovery helped fuel the early Audubon movement.
Smaller and more active than the great egret, it dashes and darts through the shallows rather than standing still.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the Americas. Year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The snowy egret is the energetic counterpart to the patient great egret. Where the great egret stands motionless and waits, the snowy runs, hops, and shuffles its feet to flush prey — a distinctly different hunting style.

The golden-yellow feet are the single best field mark. In breeding plumage, it also grows delicate, recurved plumes on its back, head, and chest — the same plumes that made it a target for the millinery trade in the late 1800s.
Its recovery is one of the early triumphs of the conservation movement, though more recently its numbers in some areas have declined again with wetland loss.

blocks.label: Feet
blocks.text: Bright yellow — the 'golden slippers.' The single best field mark.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Black and slender.

blocks.label: Legs
blocks.text: Black.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Medium — about 24 inches, smaller than the great egret.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium white egret actively running through shallows. Check the feet: if they are yellow, it is a snowy egret.

species.diet: Small fish, shrimp, crabs, insects, and aquatic invertebrates — often flushed with foot-shuffling.
species.behavior: Active, energetic hunter — runs, shuffles feet, and strikes rapidly. More animated than other egrets.
species.sounds: Generally quiet; some harsh squawks at the colony.
species.ecological_role: Predator of small aquatic animals; historically a flagship for the conservation movement.
species.plant_connections: Forages in wetland vegetation; nests colonially in trees near water.
species.habitat: Wetlands, ponds, marshes, tidal flats — any shallow water.
species.where_to_look: Pond edges and shallows, often actively running and shuffling. Look for the yellow feet.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A small, dark-blue heron stalking the shallows — quiet, solitary, and easy to overlook next to the showier egrets.
Young birds are entirely white, and often mistaken for snowy egrets — the blue comes in gradually during their first year, creating a patchy, piebald look.
That white-to-blue transition may be a survival strategy: white juveniles are tolerated by snowy egret flocks, gaining safety in numbers until they can fend for themselves.
It is a State Threatened species in Florida — its numbers have declined with wetland loss.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States, Caribbean, and Central and South America. Year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The little blue heron is one of the quieter wading birds — it lacks the dramatic plumes of the great egret and the pink flash of the spoonbill, but it is a skilled, patient hunter of small fish, frogs, and crustaceans.

The juvenile plumage trick is remarkable: young little blue herons are entirely white, looking almost exactly like snowy egrets. Scientists believe this lets them join snowy egret flocks, where more eyes mean more safety from predators. As they mature, blue feathers gradually replace white, creating a mottled, calico look before they settle into full adult blue.
It is listed as State Threatened in Florida, part of a broader decline in wading bird populations linked to wetland loss and degraded water quality.

blocks.label: Adult
blocks.text: Entirely dark slate-blue with a purplish head and neck. Dark-tipped bill.

blocks.label: Juvenile
blocks.text: Entirely white — easily confused with a snowy egret.

blocks.label: Molting immature
blocks.text: Patchy blue-and-white, a unique calico look.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Gray with a dark tip — not the yellow bill of an egret.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small, dark-blue heron hunting alone in the shallows. If you see a white heron with a gray-tipped bill instead of yellow, it may be a juvenile little blue.

species.diet: Small fish, frogs, crayfish, and aquatic insects — stalks slowly in shallow water.
species.behavior: Patient, slow stalker. Hunts alone more often than in flocks. Quiet and unobtrusive.
species.sounds: Generally silent away from the nest.
species.ecological_role: Wading bird predator of small aquatic animals; an indicator of wetland health.
species.plant_connections: Forages in wetland vegetation; nests in trees near water.
species.habitat: Freshwater and saltwater wetlands, pond edges, marshes.
species.where_to_look: Pond edges and shallows, often alone. Look for the dark blue adult or the white juvenile with a gray-tipped bill.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident.

interaction.guidance: Observe from a distance. As a State Threatened species, avoid disturbing it.

conservation.status: State Threatened in Florida. Numbers have declined due to wetland loss and water-quality degradation.

quick_hits: The deep, resonant 'hoo-hoo-hoooo hoo-hoo' at dusk is the great horned owl — the most powerful owl in North America.
Its grip strength is estimated at about 300 pounds per square inch — enough to crush prey instantly and strong enough that it routinely takes skunks, which most predators avoid.

The feathered 'horns' are not ears — they are tufts used for communication and camouflage. The real ears are hidden openings on the sides of the head, offset so the owl can pinpoint sound in three dimensions.
It nests earlier than almost any bird — eggs often laid in January, with young in the nest while winter is still underway.
species.range_and_origin: Native across the Americas. Year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The great horned owl is the apex nocturnal predator of Florida's woodlands. It is large, powerful, and unfussy about what it eats — mammals, birds, reptiles, and even other raptors are on the menu.
It does not build its own nest. Instead it takes over the nests of hawks, herons, or squirrels, which is one reason it nests so early — it needs to claim a nest before the original builder returns.
The deep hooting call is one of the signature sounds of the natural night. Pairs often duet, with the female's voice pitched higher than the male's.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Large — about 22 inches tall with a 4-foot wingspan.

blocks.label: Ear tufts
blocks.text: Prominent feathered 'horns' on the head.

blocks.label: Eyes
blocks.text: Large, forward-facing, bright yellow.

blocks.label: Sound
blocks.text: Deep, resonant 'hoo-hoo-hoooo hoo-hoo' — the classic owl hoot.

identification.what_to_look_for: A large, bulky owl with ear tufts, most often detected by its deep hooting at dusk or dawn. Daytime sightings are rare — look for a large shape pressed against a tree trunk.

species.diet: Mammals, birds, reptiles — including skunks, rabbits, hawks, and other owls. One of the least picky predators.
species.behavior: Nocturnal hunter, perch-and-pounce. Roosts during the day pressed against tree trunks. Nests very early (January–February in Florida).
species.sounds: A deep, resonant hooting: 'hoo-hoo-hoooo hoo-hoo.' Also hisses and bill-clacks when disturbed.
species.ecological_role: Apex nocturnal predator — controls populations of rodents, rabbits, and other prey.

size.lifespan: commonly 13 years or more in the wild.

species.habitat: Woodlands, swamps, and suburban areas with large trees.
species.where_to_look: Listen at dusk for the hooting. Scan large tree trunks for a roosting shape during the day.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, but nocturnal — most likely heard at dusk or early morning.

seasonality.note: Nocturnal; detected mainly by voice.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A medium-sized hawk built for speed in tight spaces — long tail, short rounded wings, and the agility to chase songbirds through dense trees.
If the birds at a feeder suddenly vanish, a Cooper's hawk is often the reason.
Adults have a blue-gray back and rusty-barred chest; immatures are brown and streaky — a different look entirely.
It has adapted well to suburban life, and backyard bird feeders are effectively hawk feeders from its perspective.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North America. Present year-round in Florida, with additional birds arriving in winter from the north.
more_information: The Cooper's hawk is an accipiter — a family of hawks built for fast, maneuverable flight through dense vegetation. Its short wings and long rudder-like tail let it twist through trees in pursuit of smaller birds.
It is increasingly common in suburban areas, where bird feeders concentrate its prey. The hawk doesn't eat seed — it eats the birds that come for the seed.
Telling it from the smaller, similar sharp-shinned hawk is one of birding's classic puzzles; size overlap and similar plumage make it tricky.

blocks.label: Shape
blocks.text: Long tail with a rounded tip, short rounded wings — built for agility.

blocks.label: Adult
blocks.text: Blue-gray above, rusty-barred chest, dark cap.

blocks.label: Immature
blocks.text: Brown above, white below with brown streaks.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Fast, agile, with quick wingbeats and glides through trees.

identification.what_to_look_for: A sleek hawk with a long tail gliding through trees or perched quietly near a bird feeder. Adults are blue-gray and rusty; immatures are brown and streaky.

species.diet: Mostly birds — doves, robins, jays, starlings, and smaller songbirds. Also takes squirrels and chipmunks.
species.behavior: Ambush hunter, using cover to approach prey. Fast, agile flight through dense trees. Often perches quietly near feeders.
species.sounds: A sharp, rapid 'kak-kak-kak' — most vocal near the nest in spring.
species.ecological_role: Top predator of songbirds and small mammals in wooded and suburban habitats.
species.plant_connections: Nests in dense trees; hunts in wooded areas.

size.weight: roughly 8 to 24 oz (220 to 680 g); females much larger than males

species.habitat: Woodlands, parks, and suburban areas with trees and prey.
species.where_to_look: Scan the canopy edges and listen for alarming songbirds — a sudden silence often means a Cooper's hawk is nearby.
species.when_to_see: Year-round, with more birds present in winter.

seasonality.note: Northern migrants supplement residents in winter.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A loud, repeated 'kee-ahh' ringing through the trees is the signature call of the red-shouldered hawk — Florida's most vocal and commonly seen hawk.
It is a woodland hawk, preferring to hunt from a perch under the canopy rather than soaring over open fields.
The reddish-orange bars across the chest and the translucent crescent near the wingtips in flight are the key field marks.
Blue jays imitate its call convincingly — so well that even experienced birders do a double-take.
species.range_and_origin: Native to eastern North America. A common year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The red-shouldered hawk is the hawk of Florida's neighborhoods and wooded parks. It is more tied to trees and water than the open-country red-tailed hawk, so it turns up regularly in suburban landscapes with mature canopy.
It hunts from low perches, dropping onto lizards, frogs, snakes, crayfish, and small mammals — prey it spots by watching the ground from a branch.
Its loud, carrying call is one of the most commonly heard raptor sounds in Florida, and blue jays mimic it often enough that the call alone is not a guarantee.

blocks.label: Chest
blocks.text: Reddish-orange barring across the breast.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Translucent crescent-shaped panel near the wingtips; banded tail.

blocks.label: Sound
blocks.text: Loud, repeated 'kee-ahh' — carrying and distinctive.

blocks.label: Habitat
blocks.text: Perches in wooded areas near water, not typically soaring high over open fields.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium hawk perched in a tree near water, with a barred reddish chest. The loud 'kee-ahh' call is often the first clue.

species.diet: Lizards, frogs, snakes, crayfish, small mammals, and insects — hunted from a perch.
species.behavior: Perch-hunts from mid-canopy, dropping onto prey below. Very vocal. Pairs often call back and forth.
species.sounds: A loud, descending 'kee-ahh, kee-ahh' — one of the most recognizable hawk calls in the eastern U.S.
species.ecological_role: Top predator of small vertebrates in wooded habitats; helps control lizard and rodent populations.
species.plant_connections: Nests in large trees; hunts from canopy perches.
species.habitat: Wooded areas near water — swamps, riparian corridors, suburban neighborhoods with mature trees.
species.where_to_look: Listen for the 'kee-ahh' call in the canopy near the ponds.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, vocal and visible in every season.

seasonality.note: Nests in spring.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A stocky blackbird with a glossy brown head (male) and a drab gray-brown body (female) — smaller and chunkier than a grackle.
It is North America's most notorious brood parasite: it lays its eggs in the nests of other birds and leaves them to do all the work.
Over 200 species have been documented raising cowbird young, often at the expense of their own chicks.
The strategy evolved on the Great Plains, where cowbirds followed bison herds and could not stay in one place long enough to raise their own young.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North America. Year-round resident in much of the southeastern U.S. including Florida.
more_information: The brown-headed cowbird is native, but it is one of the most controversial native birds in conservation. By parasitizing the nests of smaller songbirds, it can depress breeding success for species already under pressure.

The female is a stealth operator — she watches other birds build nests, slips in to lay an egg when the owner is away, and moves on. The cowbird chick often hatches first and grows faster, outcompeting the host's own young.
Despite its reputation, the cowbird is doing what it evolved to do. The real problem is that habitat fragmentation puts more songbird nests within reach of cowbirds than ever before.

blocks.label: Male
blocks.text: Glossy black body with a rich chocolate-brown head.

blocks.label: Female
blocks.text: Plain gray-brown all over — easy to overlook.

blocks.label: Shape
blocks.text: Stocky, short-tailed, with a thick conical bill.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Often on the ground near livestock or in mixed blackbird flocks.

identification.what_to_look_for: A stocky blackbird with a brown head (male) or a plain gray-brown bird (female), often feeding on the ground in open areas.

species.diet: Seeds, grain, and insects — forages on the ground, often near livestock.
species.behavior: Feeds on the ground. Males display with a gurgling song and a bow. Females scout nests of other species.
species.sounds: Male gives a liquid, gurgling 'glug-glug-gleee.' Female makes a dry chatter.
species.ecological_role: Brood parasite — a significant pressure on small songbird nesting success. Also an insect and seed consumer.
species.plant_connections: Forages on seeds from grasses and weeds.
species.habitat: Open ground, fields, lawns, edges, and anywhere near livestock or feeders.
species.where_to_look: Open lawns and ground near the park edges; sometimes in mixed flocks with grackles.
species.when_to_see: Year-round in Florida, though numbers may fluctuate seasonally.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A lanky, iridescent blackbird with a long keel-shaped tail and piercing yellow eyes — the common grackle walks across lawns like it owns them.
In the right light, that all-black look gives way to a bronze or purple sheen across the head and body.
It is a bold, noisy, and highly social bird, gathering in large flocks that can overwhelm a parking lot or a feeder.
Its voice is unmistakable — a loud, rusty-gate squeak that sounds like a hinge in need of oil.
species.range_and_origin: Native to eastern North America. Year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The common grackle is one of those birds people either love or tolerate. It is smart, bold, and everywhere — striding across lawns, raiding feeders, and gathering in noisy flocks in parking lots and trees.
The iridescence is real and striking in good light: the Florida form typically shows a bronze or greenish sheen on the body and a purplish head.
Grackles are among the most adaptable birds in North America, thriving in cities, suburbs, farms, and parks.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: All black with iridescent bronze, purple, or green sheen in sunlight. Pale yellow eyes.

blocks.label: Tail
blocks.text: Long and keel-shaped — folded into a V in flight.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: About 12 inches, larger than a robin.

blocks.label: Sound
blocks.text: A loud, squeaky, rusty-hinge call.

identification.what_to_look_for: A sleek black bird with yellow eyes and a long, V-shaped tail, walking boldly across open ground. The squeaky call clinches it.

species.diet: Insects, seeds, grain, fruit, small fish, and scraps — eats almost anything.
species.behavior: Walks on the ground, forages in flocks, visits feeders. Roosts in large communal groups.
species.sounds: A loud, squeaky, creaking call — like a rusty hinge.
species.ecological_role: Seed disperser and insect consumer; also a nest predator.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on seeds and berries from many plants.
species.habitat: Lawns, parks, parking lots, wetland edges — almost everywhere.
species.where_to_look: Open lawns, pond edges, and parking areas. Hard to miss.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: The large, dark bird soaring overhead in lazy circles with its wings held in a slight V — rocking side to side without flapping — is almost certainly a turkey vulture.
It finds carrion primarily by smell, one of the very few birds that can — its sense of smell is powerful enough to detect a carcass hidden under a forest canopy.
The bare red head is not decorative; it is hygienic — featherless skin is easier to keep clean when you eat by plunging your head into a carcass.
Despite their association with death, vultures are essential sanitation workers, cleaning up carrion that would otherwise spread disease.
species.range_and_origin: Native across the Americas, from southern Canada to South America. A common year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The turkey vulture is one of the most recognizable silhouettes in the Florida sky. It soars for hours on thermals with almost no flapping, tilting and rocking in a distinctive wobbly flight that experienced birders can identify at great distance.

Unlike most birds, it relies heavily on smell — a rare trait. Gas-pipeline crews have historically watched for circling vultures to find leaks, because the odorant added to natural gas mimics the smell of decay.
It is harmless to live animals and pets. Despite its intimidating size and association with death, it feeds exclusively on carrion.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Large dark bird soaring with wings in a shallow V (dihedral), rocking side to side. Rarely flaps.

blocks.label: Underwing
blocks.text: Two-toned: dark leading edge, silvery-gray flight feathers.

blocks.label: Head
blocks.text: Small, bare, red (adult) — visible at close range.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Wingspan about 6 feet.

identification.what_to_look_for: A large dark bird soaring in circles with wings held in a V and a distinctive side-to-side rocking. Up close, the small bare red head is unmistakable.

species.diet: Carrion exclusively. Does not kill live prey.
species.behavior: Soars on thermals for hours. Roosts communally, often in dead trees or on buildings. Spreads wings to sun-dry in the morning.
species.sounds: Essentially voiceless — lacks the vocal anatomy of most birds. Hisses and grunts when disturbed.
species.ecological_role: Nature's cleanup crew — removes carrion that would otherwise breed disease. A keystone scavenger.
species.habitat: Open country, roadsides, and anywhere thermals form. Roosts in trees, on buildings, and on communication towers.
species.where_to_look: Look up — almost any time you see a large bird circling overhead without flapping, it is a turkey vulture.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, visible overhead on any warm day.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A sleek, slate-gray bird with a black cap and a rusty patch hidden under the tail — listen for the cat-like mewing call that gives it its name.
Like its cousin the mockingbird, it is a mimic, stringing together borrowed phrases — but it rarely repeats them, giving its song a rambling, unrepeated quality.
It skulks in dense shrubs and tangles, more often heard than seen.
A winter visitor and passage migrant in our area, most likely from fall through spring.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North America. Breeds across the eastern and central U.S. and southern Canada; winters in the southeastern U.S., Caribbean, and Central America. Present in the Bradenton area mainly as a winter visitor.
more_information: The gray catbird is a member of the Mimidae — the same mimic family as the mockingbird and brown thrasher. Its song is a long, wandering string of notes and phrases, many borrowed from other birds, but unlike the mockingbird it rarely repeats a phrase, so the song sounds more improvised.
The cat-like mewing call is distinctive and often the first clue that a catbird is hiding in the bushes nearby.
Despite its drab color, the rusty undertail coverts are a reliable field mark if you catch the bird flicking its tail.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Uniform slate gray with a black cap and a rusty-red undertail patch.

blocks.label: Shape
blocks.text: Sleek, medium-sized, long-tailed.

blocks.label: Sound
blocks.text: A distinctive cat-like mewing; also a rambling, non-repeating song.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Skulks in dense vegetation; flicks tail to reveal rusty patch.

identification.what_to_look_for: A plain gray bird skulking in dense shrubs, mewing like a cat. Look for the black cap and, if it flicks its tail, the hidden rusty patch underneath.

species.diet: Insects and fruit — a mixed diet that shifts toward berries in fall and winter.
species.behavior: Skulks in dense cover, foraging low. Sings from concealed perches. Flicks tail frequently.
species.sounds: A nasal, cat-like 'mew' and a long, rambling song of unrepeated phrases.
species.ecological_role: Berry disperser and insect consumer in shrubby habitats.
species.plant_connections: Eats many native berries; forages in dense plantings and hedgerows.
species.habitat: Dense shrubs, thickets, hedgerows, and garden edges.
species.where_to_look: Listen for the mewing call in dense shrubbery along garden edges and near the ponds.
species.when_to_see: Mainly a winter visitor and migrant in this area, most likely October through April.

seasonality.note: Breeds farther north; present here mainly fall through spring.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: The smallest woodpecker in North America — barely bigger than a sparrow, with a stubby bill and a black-and-white pattern that looks like a shrunken version of the hairy woodpecker.
Males wear a small red patch on the back of the head; females have none.
It readily joins mixed flocks of chickadees, titmice, and nuthatches working through the trees in winter.
Its small size lets it forage on thinner branches and weed stems that larger woodpeckers cannot use.
species.range_and_origin: Native across most of North America. A year-round resident in Florida.
more_information: The downy woodpecker is the entry-level woodpecker — small, confiding, and common enough that most birdwatchers learn it first. Its black-and-white plumage and tiny bill distinguish it from the nearly identical but larger hairy woodpecker.
Despite its size it is a capable excavator, chiseling out nest cavities in dead wood and probing bark for insects.
It often visits suet feeders, making it one of the more approachable woodpeckers for casual observers.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: About 6 inches — the smallest North American woodpecker.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Short and stubby, noticeably smaller than the similar hairy woodpecker's.

blocks.label: Pattern
blocks.text: Black-and-white with a white back stripe; male has a small red nape patch.

blocks.label: Behavior
blocks.text: Forages on thin branches and weed stems, often acrobatically.

identification.what_to_look_for: A very small black-and-white woodpecker with a short bill, hitching along branches or clinging to thin stems.

species.diet: Insects, insect larvae, seeds, and suet. Forages on bark, thin branches, and weed stems.
species.behavior: Quiet, methodical forager on thin branches and bark. Often joins mixed-species flocks in winter. Taps lightly rather than hammering hard.
species.sounds: A high, sharp 'pik' call and a descending whinny rattle.
species.ecological_role: Insect control on small branches; nest cavities used by other small cavity nesters.
species.plant_connections: Forages on a wide variety of trees; nests in dead wood.
species.habitat: Woodlands, parks, suburbs, orchards — wherever there are trees.
species.where_to_look: Scan thin branches and weed stems; listen for the sharp 'pik' call. Often with mixed flocks.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, active by day.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-19 from iNat observations export (June 2026 batch). Content drafted; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

quick_hits: Despite the name, the red belly is just a faint blush you rarely see — what catches your eye is the vivid red cap running from bill to nape on the male.
It is one of Florida's most common woodpeckers, turning up in parks, yards, and woodlands alike.
It wedges nuts and seeds into bark crevices to hold them steady while hammering them open — a built-in vise.
Its long, barbed tongue can extend nearly two inches past the tip of its bill to extract insects from deep inside wood.
species.range_and_origin: Native throughout the eastern United States. A year-round resident in Florida, common in woodlands, suburbs, and parks.
more_information: The red-bellied woodpecker is one of those birds whose name sends people looking for the wrong field mark. The belly has a faint pinkish wash, but it is the bold red stripe on the head that identifies this bird — running from the bill to the nape on males, limited to the nape on females.
It is an adaptable, opportunistic feeder: insects, spiders, nuts, seeds, fruit, and occasionally tree frogs or small lizards. That flexibility is part of why it thrives in suburban Florida.
Its undulating flight — a few quick wingbeats, then a fold and a dip — is typical of woodpeckers and easy to spot crossing an open space.

blocks.label: Head
blocks.text: Bold red stripe from bill to nape (male) or just on the nape (female). Black-and-white barred back.

blocks.label: Belly
blocks.text: Faint pinkish wash — rarely visible in the field despite the name.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: About 9 inches, roughly robin-sized.

blocks.label: Sound
blocks.text: A rolling 'churr' call and steady drumming on wood.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium woodpecker with a barred black-and-white back and a red cap, hitching up a trunk or clinging to a branch. Listen for the distinctive rolling call.

species.diet: Insects, spiders, seeds, nuts, fruit, and occasionally small vertebrates. Wedges food into bark crevices to hold it while eating.
species.behavior: Hitches up and around tree trunks and branches, probing bark for insects. Stores food by jamming it into crevices. Undulating flight between trees.
species.sounds: A rolling, somewhat nasal 'churr-churr-churr' and steady drumming. One of the more vocal woodpeckers.
species.ecological_role: Controls bark-dwelling insects; its nest cavities are later used by other species including screech-owls, squirrels, and small birds.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on native fruits and berries; nests in dead wood of oaks, palms, and other trees.
species.habitat: Woodlands, suburbs, parks — anywhere with mature trees for foraging and nesting.
species.where_to_look: Listen for drumming and the rolling call; scan tree trunks and larger branches in the canopy.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, active by day in every season.

interaction.guidance: Observe and enjoy.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A tiny pale-blue butterfly, barely bigger than a thumbnail, flickering low over the flower beds is a cassius blue — one of Florida's smallest and most common butterflies.
Males shine pale blue above; females are duller, with more dusky gray and brown.
Flip to the underside and you find a delicate maze of fine gray-and-white bands, with one or two small black-and-orange eyespots near the tail.
Those eyespots, paired with tiny hindwing tails the butterfly wiggles, create a "false head" that fools a predator into striking the wrong end.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States, the Caribbean, and south into the American tropics. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident of gardens, parks, and weedy open ground.
more_information: The cassius blue is a member of the gossamer-wing family — small, fragile butterflies often no bigger than a fingernail. Males are a soft, pale blue above; females wear more gray and brown, so the two can look like different butterflies at a glance.

Turn one over and the underside tells the real story: a fine pattern of gray-and-white bands and a couple of small black-and-orange eyespots near the rear edge of the hindwing. Combined with tiny tails that the resting butterfly slowly wiggles, those spots mimic a head and antennae at the wrong end of the body. A bird aiming for the "head" gets a harmless beakful of wingtip, and the butterfly escapes.

It is an easy butterfly to host. Its caterpillars feed on the buds and flowers of legumes and on plumbago, both common in Florida landscaping, which is part of why the cassius blue is such a constant presence in gardens.

blocks.label: Upperside
blocks.text: Males pale blue; females duller blue-gray with brown.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: White to pale gray with fine gray bands and one or two small black-and-orange eyespots near the tail.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Very small, wingspan around 1 in (2.5 cm).

blocks.label: Tails
blocks.text: Tiny threadlike tails on the hindwings.

identification.what_to_look_for: A very small, pale powder-blue butterfly fluttering low around flowers. The fine-banded underside with little eyespots near a tiny tail confirms a cassius blue.

species.diet: Adults sip nectar from small flowers. Caterpillars feed on the flowers and buds of legumes and on plumbago.
species.behavior: Active by day, with a low, fluttery flight close to the ground and flowers. Often perches with wings closed, slowly moving the hindwings so the false-head eyespots and tails twitch.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: A small pollinator of low flowers and prey for spiders, birds, and other insects — a minor but constant thread in the garden food web.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants include legumes (such as peas and wild beans) and plumbago (leadwort) — common garden plants, which keeps this butterfly close to home.

size.lifespan: adults live a couple of weeks.

species.habitat: Gardens, parks, weedy fields, and disturbed open ground with low flowers and legume or plumbago host plants.
species.where_to_look: Low around the flower beds and any plumbago plantings, fluttering close to the blooms. Look small — it is easy to miss.
species.when_to_see: Year-round in Florida's warm climate, most numerous in the warmer months.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Avoid handling its delicate wings; low-growing nectar flowers and plumbago will keep it around.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the southeastern U.S. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread.

quick_hits: A swimming crab with sapphire-blue claws turning up in a park pond is the Atlantic blue crab — and its presence is a clue that the water is brackish, not pure fresh.
Its scientific name, Callinectes sapidus, means "savory beautiful swimmer," and it lives up to all three words.
The back pair of legs are flattened into paddles, letting it swim sideways through the water rather than only crawl.
Tip for telling males from females: females have red-tipped claws, often described as wearing "painted fingernails."
species.range_and_origin: Native to the western Atlantic, from the U.S. East Coast through the Gulf of Mexico. It lives in estuaries and coastal waters and ranges into brackish and even fresh water, which is how it reaches inland ponds with a tidal or saltwater connection.
more_information: The blue crab is an estuary animal, built for the in-between waters where rivers meet the sea. Its Latin name, Callinectes sapidus, translates neatly as "savory beautiful swimmer" — a nod to its blue-tinged beauty, its swimming ability, and its long history as prized seafood along the Atlantic and Gulf coasts.

What sets it apart from a typical crab is the back pair of legs, flattened into broad paddles. With them the blue crab can truly swim, sculling sideways through open water rather than only scuttling along the bottom.

That tolerance for a wide range of saltiness is why it shows up where you might not expect a crab. Finding one in a park pond usually means the water is brackish or connected to tidal flow — the crab itself reading as a living water-quality clue. Sex is easy once you know the trick: mature females have red-tipped claws, like painted nails, while males' claws stay all blue.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Olive to grayish body with bright blue legs and claws; mature females have red-tipped claws.

blocks.label: Claws
blocks.text: Strong pincers, blue in males, red-tipped in females.

blocks.label: Back legs
blocks.text: Flattened into swimming paddles.

blocks.label: Shell
blocks.text: Broad, with a sharp spine on each side.

identification.what_to_look_for: A broad crab with blue-tinged claws and paddle-shaped back legs in or near the water. Red-tipped "painted" claws mark a female; all-blue claws mark a male.

species.diet: An omnivore — clams, snails, small fish, plants, detritus, and dead animals — scavenged and hunted along the bottom.
species.behavior: Active in and around the water, swimming with its paddle legs and foraging on the bottom. Defends itself readily with strong pincers when cornered. Burrows into mud to overwinter and to molt.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: A wide-ranging omnivore and scavenger that helps clean and recycle material in estuarine and brackish waters, and an important link in coastal food webs.
species.plant_connections: Forages among submerged and shoreline vegetation, eating plant material and detritus along with animal prey.

size.width: shell up to about 9 in (23 cm) across, including the side spines.

species.habitat: Estuaries, bays, marshes, and brackish coastal waters, ranging into fresh water; reaches inland ponds with a tidal or saltwater connection.
species.where_to_look: In and around the ponds if the water carries any brackish influence. Scan the shallows and edges for a crab with blue claws.
species.when_to_see: Possible whenever conditions suit; most active in warm water.

danger.people: Harmless at a distance, but the pincers are strong and a cornered or handled crab can deliver a hard pinch. Do not try to pick one up.
danger.pets: Best kept away from pets — a curious dog that mouths a blue crab can get a painful pinch.

interaction.guidance: Look, do not touch. Give it room at the water's edge and do not handle it; those claws mean business.

invasive.notes: Native to the western Atlantic and Gulf, including Florida. Not invasive in its home range.

conservation.status: Not a conservation concern in its native range, though it is a heavily fished species whose stocks are managed.

quick_hits: A small dark ladybird beetle with a rusty-orange head and tail, working over a plant, is a mealybug destroyer — a beetle introduced on purpose to fight a pest.
It was brought from Australia over a century ago to control mealybugs and scale insects, and it has been doing the job ever since.
Its larva is a clever disguise artist: covered in white waxy fluff, it looks almost exactly like the mealybugs it eats, hiding in plain sight among them.
Despite being introduced, it is a welcome helper, not an invader — it sticks to soft scale pests and does not run wild.
species.range_and_origin: Native to Australia, introduced into the United States in 1891 as a biological control agent and now established in warm regions including Florida, where it lives wherever mealybugs and soft scales occur.
more_information: The mealybug destroyer is one of the early success stories of biological control. A ladybird beetle from Australia, it was imported in 1891 to fight citrus mealybugs in California and has since become a standard ally in greenhouses, groves, and gardens across the warm parts of the country.

Both the adult and the larva are predators of mealybugs, soft scales, and their eggs. The larva is the strange one: it grows a coat of white waxy filaments that makes it look uncannily like an oversized mealybug, a disguise that lets it feed right inside a colony of its prey.

Unlike many introduced insects, this one is considered beneficial rather than invasive. It specializes on soft-bodied scale pests, does not damage plants, and does not displace native wildlife in any notable way, which is why growers still release it deliberately.

blocks.label: Adult
blocks.text: Small, oval, dark brown to black with a rusty-orange head and rear end.

blocks.label: Larva
blocks.text: Covered in white, waxy filaments — looks like a large, fuzzy mealybug.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Tiny, only a few millimeters long.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small dark beetle with orange ends moving over plants where mealybugs or scale insects gather — or its fuzzy white larva creeping among the pests it mimics. The larva's mealybug disguise is the memorable clue.

species.diet: Mealybugs and soft scale insects, along with their eggs, eaten by both the adult beetle and the larva.
species.behavior: Active by day, searching foliage for colonies of mealybugs and scale. Both larva and adult feed steadily on the pests. Slow-moving and harmless to handle.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: A beneficial predator that controls mealybugs and soft scales, widely used to protect plants without chemicals.
species.plant_connections: Found on plants infested with mealybugs or soft scale — ornamentals, citrus, and many others — where its prey lives.

size.lifespan: adults live a few weeks to a couple of months.

species.habitat: Gardens, greenhouses, groves, and landscapes wherever mealybugs and soft scale insects occur.
species.where_to_look: On plantings affected by mealybugs or scale, especially in the warmer, sheltered parts of the garden. Look for the fuzzy white larva among the pests.
species.when_to_see: Active in the warm season and year-round in mild, sheltered spots.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy — and consider it a friend in the garden. No need to handle it; it does its best work left alone.

invasive.notes: Introduced from Australia as a biocontrol agent in 1891. Considered beneficial rather than invasive — it specializes on soft scale pests and does not run wild.

conservation.status: Not a conservation concern. Common and intentionally maintained in agriculture and horticulture.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #288246402 (brice_c); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

seasonality.note: Year-round only in mild, sheltered spots.

quick_hits: A small moth flying by day in bright pink, orange, yellow, and white is the ornate bella moth — a rare moth you are more likely to see in sunshine than at a porch light.
Its gaudy colors are a warning. The moth is toxic, and the pattern tells predators not to bother.
The poison comes from its food. Caterpillars eat rattlebox plants and bank the plants' bitter alkaloids, which the adult keeps for life.
There is a twist in its courtship: males pass some of that chemical defense to females as a wedding gift, helping protect the eggs.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States and the American tropics. In Florida it is found in open, sunny places where its rattlebox host plants grow.
more_information: The bella moth breaks the usual moth rules. It flies in daylight and wears a riot of pink, orange, yellow, and white — colors that function as a billboard rather than camouflage. The message is honest: this insect is chemically defended and tastes terrible.

The defense is borrowed from plants. Its caterpillars feed on rattlebox (Crotalaria) and related legumes, which are loaded with toxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids, and the moth carries those compounds from caterpillar into adulthood. Birds quickly learn to leave the bright moth alone.

The chemistry even shapes its love life. A male advertises his alkaloid load to a female during courtship and transfers a dose to her when they mate; she folds that gift into her eggs, giving the next generation a head start on being unpalatable. Few small insects carry such a tidy story of plants, poison, and parenting.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Pink to crimson hindwings; forewings banded in orange, yellow, and white with small black-rimmed white spots.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Small, with a wingspan around 1.5 in (4 cm).

blocks.label: Activity cue
blocks.text: Flies by day, low over open vegetation.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small, brightly multicolored moth fluttering low through sunny weedy ground in daylight. The pink-and-orange palette and daytime flight set it apart from drab night-flying moths.

species.diet: Caterpillars feed on rattlebox (Crotalaria) and related legumes, taking in the plants' toxins. Adults sip nectar.
species.behavior: Active by day, flying low and weakly over open, weedy ground near its host plants. Relies on its warning colors and stored toxins rather than fast escape.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: A toxic, day-flying moth that advertises its defenses with bright color — a living example of how insects borrow chemistry from plants for protection.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants are rattlebox (Crotalaria spp.) and related legumes, the source of the moth's protective alkaloids.
species.habitat: Open, sunny, weedy places — fields, roadsides, pond edges, and disturbed ground — wherever rattlebox grows.
species.where_to_look: Low over open, weedy ground and pond edges in sunshine, especially near any rattlebox or pea-family plants.
species.when_to_see: A warm-season moth, most likely from spring through fall.

danger.people: Completely harmless to people. Its toxins only matter to animals that try to eat it.
danger.pets: Not a concern. A pet would find it unpalatable; it poses no real danger.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Avoid handling its delicate wings, and the best help is leaving native rattlebox and wild margins in place.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the southeastern U.S. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Not a conservation concern. Local but widespread where its host plants grow.

quick_hits: A dragonfly with a vivid rose-pink to purple body cruising the pond edge is a male roseate skimmer — one of the few truly pink dragonflies you will see.
The color belongs to mature males. Females and young males are a more modest brown, so the showy pink is a male's badge.
It is a creature of warm, often disturbed water, and is common around ponds, ditches, and canals across the southern states.
Like every dragonfly, it is a superb flier and hunter, catching small insects in mid-air with a basket formed by its legs.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southern United States and south into the American tropics. In Florida it is a common warm-season dragonfly of ponds, ditches, and other still waters.
more_information: The roseate skimmer stands out for a color most dragonflies never wear. Mature males develop a rosy-pink to red-violet abdomen and thorax, a striking look against the greens and browns of a pond edge. Females and immatures are brown and tan, which is why the pink reads as a male's signal.
It is a hardy, adaptable insect, at home on ponds, roadside ditches, retention basins, and other still or slow water, and it has spread comfortably through the warm parts of the country.

As a predator it follows the dragonfly playbook: adults hunt small flying insects on the wing, gathering prey in a basket made of their bristly legs, while the aquatic young hunt small creatures (including mosquito larvae) underwater before climbing out to transform.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Mature males are rose-pink to red-violet; females and young are brown and tan.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Mostly clear, sometimes with a faint amber tint at the base.

blocks.label: Body
blocks.text: Slender, typical skimmer shape.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Perches on low stems and patrols low over the water.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium dragonfly with a rose-pink or purplish body working a pond edge — the pink is unmistakable on a male. Brown individuals with the same shape and habits are females or young males.

species.diet: Small flying insects caught in flight; aquatic young prey on mosquito larvae and other small water creatures.
species.behavior: Active by day in warm weather, patrolling low over still water and perching on low vegetation between sorties. Males hold territories along the shoreline.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: A predator at both life stages, helping keep flying insects and mosquito larvae in check around still waters.
species.plant_connections: Breeds in ponds and ditches with vegetated margins, perching on low pondside plants.
species.habitat: Ponds, ditches, canals, and other still or slow waters, including human-made basins, with sunny vegetated edges.
species.where_to_look: Around the ponds and any ditches or basins, perched on low stems or patrolling the shoreline on warm days. Watch for the pink.
species.when_to_see: A warm-season flier, common from spring through fall.

danger.people: Harmless. It has no sting and poses no threat to people.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. No need to catch or handle them — dragonflies are best seen in flight.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the southern U.S. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common, adaptable, and widespread.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #167766870 (jeffreypatton); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

quick_hits: A tiny dragonfly with glowing amber-orange wings, hovering low over the pond, is an eastern amberwing — one of the smallest dragonflies in North America.
At barely an inch long, it is easy to mistake for a wasp, and that is the point: it bluffs.
When perched, it wags its abdomen up and down and twitches its wings in a fair imitation of a stinging wasp, discouraging predators that would not bother a harmless dragonfly.
Like all dragonflies, it is a precise aerial hunter, snatching small insects out of the air on the wing.
species.range_and_origin: Native and widespread across the eastern and central United States. In Florida it is common near still and slow waters through the warm months.
more_information: The eastern amberwing is a study in small-package boldness. Males have wings washed a warm amber-orange, unusual among local dragonflies, and a body not much over an inch long.

That small size and orange-banded look pay off as mimicry. Perched amberwings bob the abdomen and flick the wings in a way that resembles a wasp tidying itself, and the resemblance is thought to make birds and other predators think twice — a harmless dragonfly borrowing a wasp's reputation.

For all the bluff, it earns its keep as a predator. Adults hawk tiny flying insects over the water, and the aquatic young (naiads) hunt mosquito larvae and other small prey beneath the surface, making amberwings quiet allies against biting insects.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Very small for a dragonfly, about 1 in (2.5 cm) long.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Males have entirely amber-orange wings; females have patchy brown-and-clear wings.

blocks.label: Body
blocks.text: Reddish-amber in males.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Wags its abdomen and twitches its wings while perched, wasp-style.

identification.what_to_look_for: A very small dragonfly with glowing orange wings, perched on pondside vegetation or hovering low over the water. The wasp-like abdomen-wagging while perched is a giveaway.

species.diet: Small flying insects — midges, mosquitoes, gnats, and the like — caught in flight. Aquatic young eat mosquito larvae and other small water creatures.
species.behavior: Active by day in warm, sunny weather, patrolling and perching low around still water. Males defend small territories over the surface. Frequently perches, unlike some dragonflies that stay airborne.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: A small predator at both stages of life — adults thin out flying insects, naiads eat mosquito larvae — making it a natural check on biting-insect numbers around the pond.
species.plant_connections: Breeds in ponds with submerged and emergent plants, perching on pondside stems and reeds.
species.habitat: Ponds, lakes, ditches, and slow waters with vegetated edges and plenty of sun.
species.where_to_look: Low over the ponds and along their planted edges on warm, sunny days. Look for a tiny dragonfly with orange wings perched on a reed or stem.
species.when_to_see: A warm-season flier, most numerous from late spring through early fall.

danger.people: Harmless. Despite the wasp act, it has no sting and cannot hurt you.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Dragonflies are best appreciated on the wing — no need to catch or handle them.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the eastern U.S. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread on suitable waters.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #295720857 (catalaniclaire); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

quick_hits: A huge, dark, bat-like moth — wings spanning up to seven inches — resting in a shady spot is the black witch, one of the largest moths in North America.
It is wrapped in folklore. In parts of Mexico it is the "mariposa de la muerte," an omen of death, while in the Caribbean and Texas it can be a sign of coming good fortune or money.
For all its menacing looks, it is utterly harmless. The adult drinks fruit juices and cannot bite or sting.
It is a long-distance wanderer, drifting north from the tropics through the warm months and turning up far from where it was born.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the American tropics, from South America up through Mexico, and a regular warm-season wanderer into Florida and across the southern United States.
more_information: The black witch is a moth built on a grand scale, with a wingspan that can top seven inches — broad, dark, and scalloped, with a small comma-and-number-eight marking on each forewing. Seen flapping at dusk, it is easy to mistake for a bat.

Few insects carry as much folklore. Across its range it has gathered names and beliefs that pull in opposite directions: in much of Mexico and Central America it is the butterfly of death, an ill omen if it enters a home where someone is sick; in parts of the Caribbean and South Texas, by contrast, one landing over your doorway is said to bring money or luck. The same big dark moth, read as a curse in one place and a blessing in another.

Behind the stories is a gentle, mobile insect. Adults feed on rotting fruit and sap, not blood or nectar exclusively, and they have no interest in people. Each year individuals drift far north of the tropics, which is how a tropical giant ends up resting under a Florida eave or bridge.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Very large, wingspan to about 5 to 7 in (12 to 18 cm).

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Mottled dark brown and gray, with faint iridescent purple and a pale band across the wingtips.

blocks.label: Marking
blocks.text: A small comma- or number-9-shaped spot near the front edge of each forewing; females show a paler band.

blocks.label: Posture
blocks.text: Rests flat and wings-spread in shaded, sheltered spots by day.

identification.what_to_look_for: A bat-sized dark moth tucked into shade — under eaves, bridges, or dense foliage — by day, or flapping heavily at dusk. The sheer size and the little hook-shaped wing marks set it apart.

species.diet: Adults sip juices from overripe and rotting fruit and tree sap. Caterpillars feed on the leaves of legumes such as acacia and other woody plants.
species.behavior: Mainly active at dusk and night, hiding by day in shaded, sheltered places. A strong, wandering flier that ranges far from where it developed. Drawn to lights and to fermenting fruit.
species.sounds: Silent.
species.ecological_role: Part of the nighttime insect world as both pollinator-adjacent fruit feeder and prey for bats and birds. As a tropical wanderer, its appearances mark the seasonal movement of insects northward.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillars feed on legume trees and shrubs (such as acacia and cassia); adults are drawn to fermenting and fallen fruit.

size.wingspan: 5 to 7 in (12 to 18 cm) — among the largest of any moth in the region.

species.habitat: Tropical and subtropical woodlands and gardens; as a wanderer it turns up almost anywhere with shade and fruiting plants, including buildings and bridges.
species.where_to_look: Resting in shady, sheltered spots by day — under eaves, in dense foliage, or beneath structures near the ponds — and around lights at night in the warm season.
species.when_to_see: A warm-season visitor, most likely from late spring through fall as individuals wander north from the tropics.

danger.people: Completely harmless. It has no bite, sting, or venom — only an outsized, dramatic appearance.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. If one is resting in the shade, leave it be — it is only waiting out the daylight.

invasive.notes: Native to the American tropics and a natural seasonal wanderer into Florida. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Not a conservation concern. Widespread across the tropics and a regular visitor northward.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #143209669 (teschaim); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

seasonality.note: Wanders north from the tropics.

quick_hits: A slender lizard that can shift from bright green to brown, flashing a pink throat fan, is the green anole — Florida's only native anole.
Despite the color-changing, it is not a chameleon. It switches between green and brown with mood and temperature, not to match its background.
It has been quietly pushed upward. Since the brown anole arrived from the Caribbean, green anoles spend more time high in shrubs and trees, out of the newcomer's way.
That pressure is driving fast change: green anoles on brown-anole turf have been measured evolving larger, stickier toepads for gripping higher, smoother perches.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States, including all of Florida. It is the only anole native to the state, found in gardens, woodland edges, and shrubby areas.
more_information: The green anole is often called the "American chameleon," but the nickname oversells it. It can turn from vivid green to brown and back, yet the change tracks temperature, light, and mood rather than its surroundings — a cold or stressed anole tends toward brown, a warm relaxed one toward green.

For a long time it was the only anole in Florida. Then the brown anole arrived from Cuba and the Bahamas, wanting the same food and the same sunny perches. Rather than disappear, green anoles largely shifted upward, spending more time in shrubs and tree canopies where browns are scarcer.

That shift has left a mark you can measure. In areas shared with brown anoles, green anoles have been found evolving larger, stickier toepads over just a handful of generations — a textbook case of rapid evolution, prompted by an introduced neighbor, happening right in Florida gardens.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Bright green to brown, able to switch between the two; the only local anole that turns true green.

blocks.label: Throat fan (dewlap)
blocks.text: Males flash a pink dewlap during displays.

blocks.label: Build
blocks.text: Slender, with a long tail and a pointed snout; smoother-scaled than the brown anole.

blocks.label: Perch
blocks.text: Often higher up — in shrubs and trees — than the ground-loving brown anole.

identification.what_to_look_for: A slim lizard that is bright green (or a green-brown in between), often up in shrubbery, with a pink throat fan in displaying males. The ability to turn true green is the clincher — the brown anole never can.

species.diet: Small insects and other invertebrates — flies, beetles, spiders, crickets, and the like — caught by day among leaves and branches.
species.behavior: Active by day and a confident climber, favoring shrubs, vines, and tree trunks. Males defend territory with push-up displays and pink dewlap flashes. Basks to warm up and can drop its tail to escape a predator, regrowing it later.
species.sounds: Silent. Like other anoles, it communicates with head-bobs and its colorful dewlap rather than sound.
species.ecological_role: A native insect-eater and, in turn, prey for birds, snakes, and larger lizards. Its push toward higher perches is one of Florida's clearest stories of an introduced species reshaping a native's life.
species.plant_connections: Lives among shrubs, vines, and trees, using leafy cover for hunting, basking, and shelter from the ground-dwelling brown anole.

size.length: 5 to 8 in (13 to 20 cm), including the tail.
size.lifespan: usually 2 to 4 years in the wild.

species.habitat: Shrubs, vines, trees, and garden plantings with sun and cover, from woodland edges to landscaped beds.
species.where_to_look: Up in the shrubbery and on tree trunks and vines, a step higher than the brown anoles on the fences and ground. Look for a green lizard with a pink throat fan.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, most active and most often green on warm, sunny days.

danger.people: Harmless. It has no venom and cannot meaningfully hurt you; a handled one might nip, nothing more.
danger.pets: Not dangerous to pets. Cats and dogs may chase them, but the lizard is the one at risk.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. No need to handle them — and leaving shrubby cover in the garden is the best way to help the natives hold their ground.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida — the state's only native anole. Not invasive. It is under pressure from the introduced brown anole.

conservation.status: Least Concern overall, but locally squeezed by the introduced brown anole. Native shrub and tree cover helps it persist.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372610677 (jengunter); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

seasonality.note: Most often green on warm, sunny days.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The brown anole cannot turn green, has an orange-red throat fan, and stays low; the green anole can be green or brown, has a pink fan, and perches higher.

quick_hits: A slim, soft gray-brown dove with a long pointed tail, giving a low mournful coo, is a mourning dove — one of the most abundant birds in North America.
That sad-sounding call fools people into thinking they are hearing an owl. It is just the dove, and it is how the bird got its name.
Listen when it takes off: the wings make a sharp whistling twitter. The sound is mechanical, made by the feathers, not the voice.
It feeds almost entirely on seeds, picked off the ground, and can store a big load in its crop to digest later in a safe spot.
species.range_and_origin: Native and abundant across North America. In Florida it is a common year-round resident of open country, farmland, suburbs, and parks.
more_information: Few birds are as widespread or as numerous as the mourning dove; it is found coast to coast and is among the most plentiful birds on the continent. Much of that success comes from a simple diet of seeds and a long breeding season that lets pairs raise several broods a year.
The call does most of the work of naming it. The slow, drawn-out "coo-OO-oo-oo-oo" carries a melancholy tone that people regularly mistake for an owl, especially at dawn and dusk.

The whistling wings are a separate trick. The narrow outer wing feathers vibrate in flight, producing a sharp twitter on takeoff and landing that may also serve as an alarm, alerting the flock when one bird flushes in a hurry.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Soft gray-brown, with a few black spots on the wings and a pinkish wash on the breast.

blocks.label: Shape
blocks.text: Slim and streamlined, with a small head and a long, pointed tail edged in white.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Fast and direct, wings whistling on takeoff.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Often seen feeding on the ground or perched on wires.

identification.what_to_look_for: A trim, plain dove with a long pointed tail, either walking and pecking on open ground or perched quietly. The mournful coo and the whistling wing-twitter on takeoff confirm it.

species.diet: Almost entirely seeds — grasses, weeds, and grains — gathered from the ground and stored in the crop to digest later.
species.behavior: Active by day, feeding on the ground and resting on wires, branches, and rooftops. Often in pairs or loose flocks. Flushes fast with whistling wings when startled, then settles nearby.
species.sounds: The signature low, mournful "coo-OO-oo-oo-oo," most often heard at dawn and dusk, plus the whistling twitter of the wings in flight.
species.ecological_role: An abundant seed-eater that helps move and process plant seeds, and a key prey species for hawks, falcons, and other predators.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on the seeds of many grasses and weedy plants, gleaned from open and disturbed ground.

size.lifespan: commonly a few years, occasionally much longer.

species.habitat: Open and semi-open places — fields, roadsides, lawns, gardens, and parks — with bare ground for feeding and scattered perches.
species.where_to_look: On open lawns and paths, on wires and bare branches, and around any seeding plants. Listen for the mournful coo to locate one.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, present and calling in every season.

interaction.guidance: Watch and enjoy, and please do not feed park wildlife. Doves find plenty of seed on their own.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Extremely abundant and widespread.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #349782433 (coleguyton); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The collared-dove is larger and paler with a square tail and a black half-collar on the neck; the mourning dove is smaller, browner, and long-tailed with no collar.

quick_hits: A crested blue, white, and black bird with a loud, ringing call is a blue jay — one of the boldest and brainiest birds in the park.
The blue is a trick of the light. Its feathers contain no blue pigment; tiny structures scatter light to make the color, so a crushed feather looks plain brown.
It is a champion oak-planter. A single jay can cache thousands of acorns each fall, and the ones it never retrieves grow into new oak trees.
It is also a skilled mimic, copying the scream of a red-shouldered hawk — handy for scaring other birds off a feeder.
species.range_and_origin: Native to eastern and central North America. In Florida it is a common year-round resident of woodlands, parks, and leafy neighborhoods, especially where oaks grow.
more_information: Blue jays are members of the crow family, and it shows in their intelligence. They are curious, social, and quick to learn, with a wide vocabulary of calls and a knack for problem-solving.

They are also unsung foresters. In autumn a jay gathers acorns and other nuts and buries them across its territory for winter — far more than it can ever eat. The forgotten caches sprout, and over centuries jays have helped oaks spread across the continent, a quiet partnership written into whole forests.

That famous blue is structural, not pigmented. Microscopic structures in the feathers scatter light so we see blue, which is why a backlit or crushed feather loses the color entirely. Jays round out their reputation with mimicry, especially convincing imitations of hawk calls that can clear rivals away from food.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Blue above, white to pale gray below, with a black necklace across the chest.

blocks.label: Wings and tail
blocks.text: Barred with black and white and tipped with white.

blocks.label: Crest
blocks.text: A pointed blue crest that rises and lowers with the bird's mood.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Loud, bold, and often in small noisy groups.

identification.what_to_look_for: A crested blue-and-white bird, larger than a cardinal, calling loudly from the trees or visiting feeders. The black necklace and white-tipped wings and tail confirm it.

species.diet: Mainly acorns, nuts, and seeds, plus insects, fruits, and the occasional egg or nestling. Caches large numbers of acorns for winter.
species.behavior: Active by day, social, and noisy, often moving in family groups. Bold at feeders and quick to mob hawks, owls, and cats as a group. Raises and lowers its crest to signal alarm or excitement.
species.sounds: Loud and varied: a ringing "jay-jay" scream, a musical "queedle" like a squeaky gate, and dead-on imitations of hawk calls.
species.ecological_role: A key seed disperser, especially of oaks, and a sharp-eyed alarm system whose calls alert other animals to predators.
species.plant_connections: Closely tied to oaks and other nut trees — it eats their acorns and, by burying and forgetting them, plants the next generation of trees.

size.lifespan: commonly 7 years or more in the wild.

species.habitat: Forests, woodland edges, and well-treed parks and neighborhoods, especially where oaks and other nut trees grow.
species.where_to_look: Around the park's oaks and larger trees, often announcing itself with loud calls before you spot the flash of blue.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, conspicuous in every season and especially busy caching acorns in fall.

danger.people: Harmless to people, though a bold jay near a nest may scold loudly. It poses no real threat.

interaction.guidance: Watch and enjoy, and please do not feed park wildlife. Jays are confident enough to approach on their own — no encouragement needed.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common, adaptable, and widespread.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #145591163 (wabbster); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

quick_hits: A small, plump brown waterbird that dives and vanishes the moment you notice it is a pied-billed grebe.
It would rather sink than fly. By squeezing air from its feathers, it can slip below the surface like a submarine, leaving barely a ripple.
That short, thick bill gets a black ring around it in the breeding season — the "pied" (two-toned) look that gives the bird its name.
Its feet are built for diving, not walking: the toes have paddle-like lobes instead of webbing, set far back on a body that is clumsy on land.
species.range_and_origin: Native across the Americas. In Florida it is a common winter visitor and a year-round breeder on ponds, lakes, and marshes with plenty of vegetation.
more_information: The pied-billed grebe is a diver first and a flier last. Rather than burst into the air like a duck, it usually escapes trouble by submerging — and it can control how it sinks, trapping or releasing air in its plumage to ride high or settle low in the water, sometimes leaving only its head showing like a tiny periscope.

Those diving skills come from a body that is all wrong for land. Its lobed toes and far-back legs make it a powerful underwater swimmer but an awkward walker, so it spends nearly all its time on the water.

Grebe chicks are striped like little humbugs and often ride on a parent's back, even diving along while hitching a ride. Adults also do something odd and useful: they eat their own feathers, which seems to help protect the stomach and form pellets they later cough up.

blocks.label: Size and shape
blocks.text: Small and stocky, with a short neck and almost no visible tail.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Plain brown overall, paler on the throat.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Short, thick, and pale, with a black vertical ring in breeding season.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
