blocks.text: Dives constantly and sits low in the water.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small brown waterbird riding low on a pond, repeatedly diving and popping back up somewhere unexpected. The stubby, ringed bill separates it from ducks and other divers.

species.diet: Mostly crayfish, small fish, aquatic insects, and other small water creatures, caught on underwater dives.
species.behavior: Active by day on open and weedy water, diving frequently to feed or to escape. Rarely seen flying or on land. Often solitary or in small numbers, slipping into cover when disturbed.
species.sounds: Surprisingly loud for its size in the breeding season: a far-carrying series of hollow "kuk-kuk-kuk-cow-cow-cow" calls from marshy cover.
species.ecological_role: A small diving predator that helps keep crayfish, insect, and small-fish numbers in check on quiet, vegetated waters.
species.plant_connections: Lives among emergent and floating marsh plants, which give it cover, nesting material, and the still water it prefers.
species.habitat: Ponds, lakes, and marshes with open water and plenty of submerged and emergent vegetation for cover and nesting.
species.where_to_look: On the open water of the ponds, often near the weedy edges. Watch for a small brown bird that keeps diving and reappearing — and be patient, because it spends a lot of time underwater.
species.when_to_see: Most numerous in fall and winter, with some present year-round. Active throughout the day on the ponds.

interaction.guidance: Watch and enjoy, and please do not feed park wildlife. Grebes are shy — if one keeps diving away, you are simply a bit too close.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the Americas. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Widespread and common on suitable waters.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #69689744 (pammy63); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

quick_hits: A stocky, hunch-shouldered heron with a black cap and back, pale gray wings, and startling red eyes is a black-crowned night heron.
It works the late shift. While other herons fish by day, this one feeds mostly at dusk and after dark, sidestepping the competition.
That squat, motionless posture is a hunting strategy — it stands frozen at the water's edge and waits, then strikes fast at whatever swims past.
Young birds look like a different species entirely: streaky brown and white, with none of the adult's clean black-and-gray.
species.range_and_origin: Native and found on every continent except Antarctica — one of the most widespread herons in the world. In Florida it is a fairly common year-round resident of marshes, pond edges, and wooded swamps.
more_information: The black-crowned night heron makes its living by avoiding a crowd. Most herons and egrets fish through the day, so this bird shifts its main feeding to dusk, night, and early morning, when it has the shallows largely to itself. Its large red eyes are part of the package, gathering what little light there is.

Its hunting style is patience over speed. It hunches at the water's edge, neck drawn in, and stays still for long stretches before snapping its bill forward at a fish, frog, crayfish, or insect. It is not above raiding other birds' nests for eggs and chicks when the chance comes.

The brown, heavily streaked youngsters look so unlike their parents that they are often mistaken for a different bird. It takes a couple of years for them to grow into the crisp black cap, gray wings, and red eyes of an adult.

blocks.label: Adult
blocks.text: Black crown and back, pale gray wings, white underparts, red eyes, and short yellow legs.

blocks.label: Build
blocks.text: Stocky and short-necked, with a hunched look.

blocks.label: Juvenile
blocks.text: Brown, heavily streaked and spotted with white, with orange-yellow eyes.

blocks.label: Plumes
blocks.text: A couple of long, thin white head plumes in breeding adults.

identification.what_to_look_for: A short, thickset heron sitting hunched and still at the water's edge, especially near dusk. The black cap and back over gray wings mark an adult; a streaky brown version is a young bird, not a separate species.

species.diet: Fish, frogs, crayfish, aquatic insects, and small animals, plus the occasional egg or nestling — caught mostly at dusk and through the night.
species.behavior: Most active from late afternoon through the night, roosting quietly in trees and shrubs by day. Hunts by standing still and ambushing prey rather than stalking. Often nests in colonies with other wading birds.
species.sounds: Usually quiet. Best known for a harsh, barking "quok" given in flight, often heard at dusk as birds head out to feed.
species.ecological_role: A nocturnal predator that helps balance fish, amphibian, and crayfish numbers, filling the night shift in the pond's web of wading birds.
species.plant_connections: Roosts and nests in waterside trees and shrubs by day — so the trees ringing a pond give it the cover it needs between feeding bouts.
species.habitat: Almost any wetland with cover — marshes, pond and lake edges, wooded swamps, and mangroves — with trees or shrubs nearby for daytime roosting.
species.where_to_look: Around the pond margins, often tucked into waterside shrubs or standing hunched at the edge near dawn and dusk. Look low and still rather than out in the open.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, but easiest to see in the low light of early morning and evening when it comes out to feed.

danger.people: Harmless to people. It is shy and keeps its distance.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and please do not feed it or any park wildlife. A roosting bird is resting before its night shift — give it space.

conservation.status: Least Concern globally. Widespread and adaptable, though it depends on healthy wetlands.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #182494203 (pammy63); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

seasonality.note: Easiest at dawn and dusk.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The yellow-crowned has a black-and-white face and creamy crown over an even gray body; the black-crowned has a black crown and back with pale gray wings.

quick_hits: A slim gray bird belting out one song after another from an exposed perch is almost certainly a northern mockingbird — Florida's state bird.
The name is earned. A single mockingbird can learn and copy 200 or more sounds, stringing together imitations of other birds, frogs, and even car alarms.
Unmated males sometimes sing through the night, which is why you might hear one performing under a streetlight at 2 a.m.
It is fearless near the nest. A mockingbird will dive-bomb cats, hawks, and people many times its size to drive them away from its young.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North America and resident across the southern and eastern United States. In Florida it is an abundant year-round resident of yards, parks, roadsides, and open areas with scattered shrubs — and the official state bird.
more_information: The mockingbird is a born mimic. It builds its song from snatches of whatever it hears, repeating each borrowed phrase several times before moving to the next, and it keeps adding new material throughout its life. Males with the largest repertoires tend to win the best territories and mates, so there is real payoff in being a good copycat.

That drive to sing can run around the clock. Unpaired males in spring will sing on into the night, often from a high, lit perch — beautiful or maddening depending on how close it is to your bedroom window.

Mockingbirds are also famously bold. They defend nests and feeding territories with relentless dive-bombing runs at anything they see as a threat, and they have a long memory for individual people who have come too close before.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Plain gray above, paler below, with two thin white wing bars.

blocks.label: Wing flash
blocks.text: Large white patches burst into view when it flies or flicks its wings open.

blocks.label: Tail
blocks.text: Long, with white outer feathers.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Sings from high, open perches and runs in short bursts across lawns.

identification.what_to_look_for: A slender gray bird with a long tail singing from a fence, wire, or treetop, flashing white wing patches when it flies. If it is reeling off many different songs in a row, that is the giveaway.

species.diet: Insects and other small invertebrates in the warm months, shifting to fruits and berries in fall and winter. Often forages on open lawns.
species.behavior: Active by day and conspicuous. Sings from exposed perches, runs and hops across short grass, and aggressively defends its territory and nest year-round. Frequently flashes its white wing patches while foraging, which may help startle insects.
species.sounds: The reason for the name — long, varied songs of repeated, imitated phrases, delivered by day and sometimes at night. Also a harsh "chack" scold note.
species.ecological_role: A widespread songbird that helps control insects and, by eating fruits, spreads the seeds of many shrubs and small trees through its droppings.
species.plant_connections: Eats the berries and fruits of many native shrubs and trees and nests in dense shrubbery — so fruiting plants and thick cover both draw it in.

size.lifespan: commonly up to 8 years in the wild.

species.habitat: Open and semi-open country with scattered shrubs and trees — yards, parks, hedgerows, roadsides, and woodland edges.
species.where_to_look: Almost anywhere in the open parts of the park — singing from treetops and fence lines, or running across the lawns. Listen for a long, ever-changing song.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident and one of the park's most reliable singers, vocal in every season and sometimes after dark in spring.

danger.people: Harmless to people, though a parent defending a nest may swoop close to warn you off. It cannot hurt you.
danger.pets: No real danger to pets, but a nesting mockingbird may dive at a cat or dog that wanders too close.

interaction.guidance: Watch and enjoy, and please do not feed park wildlife. If one is dive-bombing, you are simply near its nest — step away and it will settle.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common, adaptable, and comfortable around people.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #328061189 (jerseymeg); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The loggerhead shrike wears a black bandit mask and has a hooked bill; the mockingbird has a plain face, a slim straight bill, and bold white wing flashes in flight.

quick_hits: A crow-sized woodpecker with a flaming red crest, hammering at a tree trunk — that is a pileated woodpecker, now the largest woodpecker in North America since the ivory-billed is almost certainly extinct.
Listen before you look. Its loud, ringing "kuk-kuk-kuk" and deep, slow drumming carry through the woods well before the bird comes into view.
The rectangular holes it chisels are a giveaway. Most woodpeckers make round holes; the pileated carves long, squared-off cavities to reach the carpenter ants it loves.
It is also a quiet landlord. The old nest and feeding holes it abandons become homes for owls, ducks, and other animals that cannot dig their own.
species.range_and_origin: Native to forests across much of the eastern United States and Canada, west into the Pacific Northwest. In Florida it is an uncommon but year-round resident of mature woods, wooded suburbs, and parks with large trees.
more_information: The pileated is the heavyweight of North American woodpeckers — about the size of a crow, with a wingspan close to two and a half feet. For a long time the only larger woodpecker on the continent was the ivory-billed, now almost certainly extinct, which leaves the pileated at the top of the list.

Its signature is the rectangular excavation. Working with a chisel-tipped bill and a long, barbed, sticky tongue, it digs deep squared-off holes into rotting wood to pull out carpenter ants and beetle larvae. A single foraging tree can end up looking as if someone went at it with a hand axe.

Those abandoned cavities matter to the whole forest. Wood ducks, screech-owls, flying squirrels, and many other animals nest in old woodpecker holes they could never excavate themselves, which makes the pileated a kind of habitat engineer for everything around it. (And yes — its flopping crest and laughing call helped inspire Woody Woodpecker.)

blocks.label: Size and shape
blocks.text: Crow-sized, with a long neck and a pointed red crest.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Mostly black with bold white stripes on the face and neck; white shows under the wings in flight.

blocks.label: Male vs. female
blocks.text: Males have a red "mustache" stripe; females show black there.

blocks.label: Sign left behind
blocks.text: Large rectangular holes chiseled into tree trunks.

identification.what_to_look_for: A big black-and-white woodpecker with a tall red crest, working a tree trunk or flying with deep, slow wingbeats that flash white underwing patches. If you find fresh rectangular holes in a dead trunk, a pileated is likely nearby.

species.diet: Mostly carpenter ants and wood-boring beetle larvae dug out of dead and dying wood, rounded out with other insects, wild fruits, and nuts.
species.behavior: Active by day, usually alone or in pairs, working steadily up and around tree trunks and fallen logs. Pairs hold a large territory year-round. The flight is strong and slightly undulating, and the bird often calls in flight.
species.sounds: Loud and far-carrying: a ringing, laughing "kuk-kuk-kuk-kuk" that rises and falls, plus deep, resonant drumming on hollow wood used to claim territory.
species.ecological_role: A keystone forager. By chiseling into dead wood it controls carpenter-ant and beetle populations, and the cavities it leaves behind become nest and shelter sites for a long list of other animals.
species.plant_connections: Depends on mature trees and standing dead wood (snags) for both food and nest cavities — so leaving old and dying trees standing directly supports it.
species.habitat: Mature forests and well-treed parks and neighborhoods with plenty of large, old, and dead trees for feeding and nesting.
species.where_to_look: Among the park's larger trees and any standing dead trunks. Listen for the loud laughing call and slow drumming, and scan big trunks for a crow-sized bird with a red crest.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Often heard before it is seen; most active foraging in the morning.

danger.people: Harmless to people. It keeps to the trees and has no interest in visitors.

interaction.guidance: Watch and enjoy from a distance, and please do not feed park wildlife. Give a foraging bird room and it will carry on working.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Populations are stable and have slowly expanded as forests have regrown.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #362821784 (randall_carter); Unique ID written to iNat. Content drafted and ready; at research status pending iNat photo collection + licensing, then promotion to html.

seasonality.note: Often heard before it is seen; ranges over a big territory, so not always present.

quick_hits: The gulf fritillary is a bright orange butterfly with black markings above and an underside scattered with brilliant, mirror-like silver spots.
Like the zebra longwing, it depends entirely on passionflower vines — the two butterflies share the same host plant.
Its caterpillars are showy too: bright orange with rows of soft-looking black spines that look fierce but do not sting.
In late summer and fall, large numbers move south into the Florida peninsula, a seasonal drift that brings them into gardens in good numbers.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southern United States and the Americas. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident, with numbers rising in fall as butterflies move south down the peninsula.
more_information: The gulf fritillary is one of the most familiar orange butterflies in Florida gardens, and despite the name it belongs to the longwing group rather than the true fritillaries.

Its caterpillars feed only on passionflower vines — the very same Passiflora the zebra longwing uses — so a single planting of native passionvine can support both of Florida's beloved longwings at once. The caterpillars take in the vine's compounds, which makes them and the adults unpalatable to many predators.
The undersides of its wings are the real surprise: long, gleaming silver-white spots that flash like little mirrors when the butterfly basks or nectars with its wings closed.

blocks.label: Upper side
blocks.text: Bright orange with black spots and markings.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: Brown with elongated, iridescent silver-white spots.

blocks.label: Shape
blocks.text: Somewhat long-winged, like its longwing relatives.

identification.what_to_look_for: A bright orange butterfly nectaring in the open; when it closes its wings, look for the unmistakable rows of shiny silver spots underneath. The caterpillar is orange with branching black spines, found on passionflower vines.

species.diet: Caterpillars feed only on passionflower (Passiflora) vines. Adults nectar at many flowers, including lantana, Spanish needles, and other open blooms.
species.behavior: Active by day in sunny, open areas. Breeds through several generations a year and joins a southward movement into the Florida peninsula in fall. Often nectars with wings held closed, flashing its silver underside.
species.sounds: Silent, like all butterflies.
species.ecological_role: A common garden pollinator and, with the zebra longwing, a passionflower specialist — together they make native passionvine a high-value plant for supporting butterflies.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillars feed only on passionflower vines (Passiflora spp.) — the same host as the zebra longwing — so one native passionvine planting supports both butterflies.

size.lifespan: adults live a few weeks; several generations are produced each year.

species.habitat: Open, sunny places — gardens, fields, roadsides, edges, and parks — wherever passionflower vines and nectar flowers grow.
species.where_to_look: In sunny, flowery spots, nectaring with wings closed to show the silver underside. Check passionflower vines for the spiny orange caterpillars. Most numerous in late summer and fall.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, most numerous during the fall southward movement into Florida.

danger.people: Completely harmless to people. The caterpillar's spines look intimidating but do not sting.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Native passionflower vine supports both the gulf fritillary and the zebra longwing, making it a standout butterfly plant.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread, and a frequent visitor to butterfly gardens.

species.internal_notes: Promoted into Top 30. Ties to passionvine plant pages (host plant). Recent genus from Agraulis.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The monarch is broader-winged with heavy black veins and no silver; the gulf fritillary is narrower with bright silver spots on the underside.

quick_hits: The zebra longwing is Florida's official state butterfly, named in 1996 — long, narrow wings boldly striped in black and pale yellow.
It is one of the very few butterflies that eats pollen as well as nectar, and that extra protein lets it live for months rather than the usual few weeks.
Its caterpillars feed only on passionflower vines, taking in the plant's natural toxins so that the adult is distasteful to predators — the bold stripes are a warning.
At dusk, zebra longwings gather to sleep together, returning to the same communal roost night after night, sometimes dozens of butterflies in one spot.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the Americas, reaching north into Florida and southern Texas. It is common throughout peninsular Florida, favoring shaded hammocks, woodland edges, and gardens — and it has been the state butterfly since 1996.
more_information: The zebra longwing is unusual on several counts. Most butterflies live only two to four weeks, but the zebra longwing can live several months. The secret is its diet: along with nectar, it gathers and digests pollen, a rare ability among butterflies that supplies extra protein and greatly extends its life.

Its whole life cycle runs through passionflower vines. The caterpillars eat only Passiflora, and in doing so absorb compounds that make the adult butterfly distasteful, even toxic, to predators — which is what the bold black-and-yellow stripes advertise.

It is also social in a way few butterflies are. At dusk, zebra longwings return to shared roosting spots, clustering together overnight for safety, sometimes in groups of dozens, then dispersing again to feed by day with their slow, drifting flight.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Long and narrow, black with pale-yellow stripes.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Slow, graceful, and floating, usually in shade.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Medium-large, with that distinctive elongated wing shape.

identification.what_to_look_for: A black butterfly with thin yellow stripes drifting slowly through a shady garden or wood edge. The long, narrow wings and unhurried, floating flight are unmistakable. At dusk, look for several roosting together.

species.diet: Caterpillars eat only passionflower (Passiflora) leaves. Adults take both nectar and pollen — the pollen-feeding is what gives them their unusually long lives.
species.behavior: Active by day with a slow, graceful flight, favoring shade. Gathers in communal night roosts. Females lay eggs on passionflower vines. Produces several generations a year.
species.sounds: Silent. (Curiously, the chrysalis can make a faint rasping sound if disturbed.)
species.ecological_role: A pollinator with a twist — by feeding on pollen it moves it efficiently between flowers — and a passionflower specialist whose presence reflects the health of native vine habitat.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillars feed only on passionflower vines (Passiflora spp.) such as corkystem and purple passionflower — the same vines that support the gulf fritillary. Adults also gather pollen from many flowers.

size.lifespan: up to about six months — far longer than most butterflies — thanks to its pollen diet.

species.habitat: Shaded hammocks, woodland edges, thickets, and gardens, especially where passionflower vines grow in dappled light.
species.where_to_look: Drifting slowly through shaded plantings and wood edges, especially near passionflower vines. At dusk, check sheltered spots for small groups roosting together.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident through much of central and southern Florida, active in every month in warm areas.

danger.pets: Not a concern; a pet that ate one might find it bitter, but it poses no real danger.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy, and avoid handling the delicate wings. Planting native passionflower vine is the single best way to support them.

conservation.status: Least Concern, and a point of state pride as Florida's official butterfly.

species.internal_notes: FLORIDA STATE BUTTERFLY — include regardless of count. Re-keyed 2026-06-19 from subspecies H. c. tuckeri (taxon 49765) to species Heliconius charithonia (taxon 49766); Florida race kept in also_known_as and noted in text. obs_stats still reflect the old subspecies key — recompute at species taxon on next pipeline run (likely undercount).

quick_hits: The green heron is a small, stocky, secretive heron — far smaller than the great blue — with a velvet-green back, rich chestnut neck, and a dark cap it can raise into a short crest.
It is one of the very few birds in the world known to use tools: it drops bait onto the water to lure fish within striking range.
Its 'bait' can be a twig, an insect, a feather, even a bit of bread, and it will reposition the lure if it drifts — a genuinely clever piece of hunting.
It hunts by stealth, crouched low and still on a branch or bank at the water's edge, often half-hidden in vegetation.
Watch for a bird that looks almost neckless — until it strikes: the neck shoots out like a coiled spring, far longer than it seemed, to snatch prey from the water.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North and Central America. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident of small, vegetated wetlands, pond edges, and quiet, shaded water.
more_information: Where the great blue heron is tall and conspicuous, the green heron is small and easy to miss, often tucked into the vegetation at the edge of a pond, body stretched low over the water as it waits.

What sets it apart is its use of tools. The green heron will drop a small object — a twig, an insect, a feather, sometimes a crust of bread — onto the water's surface to draw curious fish close, then strike. It will even pick the lure back up and move it if it drifts out of position. Tool use is rare among birds, shared with only a handful of species, which makes this common little heron quietly remarkable.
Unlike many herons it usually nests alone rather than in big colonies, building a stick platform in a tree or shrub near water.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Small and stocky — roughly a third the size of a great blue heron.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Dark green-gray back, deep chestnut neck and breast, dark cap, and yellow to orange legs.

blocks.label: Posture
blocks.text: Often crouched low and horizontal at the water's edge.

blocks.label: Neck
blocks.text: Usually held tucked in, so the bird looks neckless and hunched — but it extends suddenly and surprisingly far when the heron strikes.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small, dark, hunched heron at the edge of a pond, often partly hidden in vegetation or on a low branch. If you see a heron seemingly fishing with a dropped twig or insect, it is almost certainly this species.

species.diet: Mainly small fish, plus insects, frogs, and other small aquatic animals — often caught by luring fish with dropped bait.
species.behavior: Active mostly by day, hunting alone with patient, crouched stillness from banks and overhanging branches. Secretive and easily overlooked. Usually nests singly in waterside trees and shrubs.
species.sounds: A sharp, explosive 'skeow' call, usually given as the bird flushes and flies off.
species.ecological_role: A small-fish predator of pond edges and one of the few tool-using birds — a reminder that resourcefulness shows up in unexpected, everyday animals.
species.plant_connections: Hunts from overhanging shrubs and low branches at the water's edge and nests in waterside trees — so dense pond-edge vegetation is part of how it lives and feeds.

size.lifespan: commonly several years, occasionally much longer.

species.habitat: Small freshwater wetlands, pond and stream edges, marshes, and mangroves with dense vegetation and overhanging branches for cover.
species.where_to_look: At the quiet, vegetated edges of the ponds, crouched low on a bank or overhanging branch — easy to miss until it moves or flushes with a sharp call.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, but much more active and vocal in the spring and summer breeding season — quieter and more stealthy in winter. Often solitary and well hidden, so it rewards a patient, careful look at pond margins.

danger.people: Harmless to visitors; a frightened bird can jab with its bill, so injured ones are best left to trained rehabbers.

interaction.guidance: Watch quietly from a distance and do not feed it. This shy heron flushes easily, and crowding it ends the hunt.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread, though, like all wetland birds, dependent on healthy, vegetated water edges.

species.internal_notes: One of the few birds documented to use tools (drops bait to catch fish) — strong 'wow that's good to know' fact for research.

seasonality.note: Quieter and more stealthy in winter.

quick_hits: The cloudless sulphur is a large, clear lemon-yellow butterfly that crosses gardens and open spaces with a quick, restless flight.
Its very name points to its plant partner: the species name 'sennae' comes from Senna, the group of plants its caterpillars eat.
It carries an unusually long tongue, letting it sip nectar from deep, tubular flowers — often red ones — that many other butterflies cannot reach.
Each fall, cloudless sulphurs from farther north stream southward into and through Florida, a quieter migration than the monarch's but a real one.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the Americas and common across the southern United States, ranging north in summer. In Florida it is a year-round resident and breeder, with numbers swelling in fall as northern butterflies move south.
more_information: The cloudless sulphur is one of Florida's most familiar butterflies — those flickering patches of pure yellow crossing a lawn or garden are usually this species.

Its life is tied to plants in the genus Senna (and the closely related Cassia), members of the pea family. The caterpillars feed on their leaves and flowers, and a caterpillar munching yellow blossoms often turns yellow itself, blending neatly into its meal. The butterfly's scientific name, Phoebis sennae, even records the connection.

In autumn, cloudless sulphurs join a broad southward movement of butterflies down the peninsula. With an extra-long proboscis, the adults can drink from long, tubular flowers — they are especially fond of red ones — that shorter-tongued butterflies cannot use.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Large and lemon-yellow; males clear yellow, females yellow to whitish with a dark wing border and a small dark spot.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Among the larger common yellow butterflies.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Fast and somewhat erratic.

identification.what_to_look_for: A big, bright yellow butterfly moving quickly across open ground or nectaring at tubular flowers. The clear, unmarked yellow of the male is the easiest clue; females are paler with darker edges.

species.diet: Caterpillars feed on Senna and Cassia plants. Adults nectar at many flowers, favoring long, tubular blooms — especially red ones — that suit their long tongue.
species.behavior: Active by day with a fast, restless flight. Breeds through several generations a year and joins a southward migration in fall. Males often gather at damp ground to 'puddle,' sipping water and minerals.
species.sounds: Silent, like all butterflies.
species.ecological_role: A wide-ranging pollinator that reaches deep flowers other insects cannot, and a specialist on Senna plants that ties it to the pea family.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants are Senna and Cassia (pea family) — including native privet senna (Senna ligustrina) and partridge pea — the very plants the butterfly is named for.

size.lifespan: adults live a few weeks; several generations are produced each year.

species.habitat: Open, sunny places — gardens, fields, roadsides, edges, and parks — wherever Senna host plants and nectar flowers grow.
species.where_to_look: Flitting across open, sunny areas and visiting nectar flowers, especially anywhere Senna or Cassia plants are growing. Most numerous in late summer and fall.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, with a noticeable increase during the fall southward migration.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Planting native Senna (such as privet senna or partridge pea) gives the caterpillars a home.

conservation.status: Least Concern. One of Florida's most common and widespread butterflies.

quick_hits: The white peacock is a pale, almost ghostly butterfly — chalky white with faint tan markings, dark eyespots, and scalloped, orange-edged wings.
It stays low, flying just above the ground in damp, open places like pond edges, ditches, and wet fields.
Its caterpillars feed on low, creeping native plants — frogfruit and water hyssop — that grow right along the water's edge.
Males are territorial, patrolling a small patch of host plants and chasing off rivals and other insects that wander in.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States, the Caribbean, Mexico, and South America. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident of low, moist, open habitats, especially in the central and southern parts of the state.
more_information: The white peacock is a butterfly of wet, open ground. You will rarely see it high in the air; instead it flutters low over damp fields, ditches, and pond margins, close to the small plants its caterpillars depend on.

Those host plants — frogfruit and water hyssop especially — are humble, low-growing natives that thrive at the water's edge, exactly the kind of groundcover a pond-side planting provides. Where they grow, white peacocks tend to follow.

Male white peacocks are notably territorial for a butterfly, staking out a small area around the host plants and actively driving off rival males and other insects that intrude — a lot of bustle from such a delicate-looking creature.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Chalky white with faint light-brown markings.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Scalloped edges trimmed in orange, with small dark eyespots.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Low and close to the ground.

identification.what_to_look_for: A pale, whitish butterfly flying low over damp, open ground near water, showing orange-trimmed, scalloped wings and small dark spots. Its habit of staying near the ground around pond edges is a good clue.

species.diet: Caterpillars feed on low native plants, chiefly frogfruit and water hyssop. Adults sip nectar from low-growing flowers such as Spanish needles and frogfruit.
species.behavior: Active by day, flying low to the ground. Males defend small territories around host plants. Breeds through multiple generations across the warm months.
species.sounds: Silent, like all butterflies.
species.ecological_role: A pollinator of low, moist-ground flowers and a specialist on creeping native groundcovers — a small but telling member of the pond-edge community.
species.plant_connections: Caterpillar host plants are low native groundcovers — frogfruit (Phyla nodiflora) and water hyssop (Bacopa monnieri) — the same plants that carpet moist pond edges.

size.lifespan: adults live a few weeks; several generations are produced each year.

species.habitat: Low, damp, open places — pond and marsh edges, ditches, wet fields, and parks — wherever its low-growing host plants occur.
species.where_to_look: Flying low around the moist edges of the ponds and in damp open ground, especially near frogfruit and other low groundcovers.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, most numerous in the warm months. Look low to the ground near water.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Low plantings of native frogfruit and water hyssop near water are the best way to invite them in.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and secure in Florida, though always tied to the moist, low habitats it needs.

quick_hits: The Florida red-bellied cooter is a large, dark basking turtle named for the reddish wash on its underside and shell.
It is a Florida native, at home in the state's ponds, lakes, and slow rivers.
Adults are mostly vegetarian, grazing on underwater plants — turtles that essentially tend the pond's vegetable garden.
It has an unusual nesting trick: females sometimes lay their eggs right in the warm nest mounds of alligators, where the guarding gator unintentionally protects them.
species.range_and_origin: Native to Florida and a bit of southern Georgia. It is one of the common large freshwater turtles across the Florida peninsula.
more_information: The red-bellied cooter is one of the big, dark turtles hauled out to bask alongside sliders and other cooters on sunny days, told apart by the reddish tones on its shell and the red wash on its underside (the plastron).
Like its cousin the peninsular cooter, the adult is largely a plant-eater, grazing submerged vegetation — a useful role in keeping pond plant growth in balance.

Its most surprising habit is where it nests. Females will sometimes dig their nests directly into the mounded nests of American alligators. The female gator, fiercely guarding her own eggs, ends up shielding the turtle eggs too — an accidental partnership that gives the cooter's eggs protection they would not have on an open bank.

blocks.label: Shell
blocks.text: High-domed and dark, with reddish vertical bars on the side scutes.

blocks.label: Underside (plastron)
blocks.text: Washed with orange to reddish color.

blocks.label: Head
blocks.text: Dark with yellow stripes, including an arrow-shaped stripe between the eyes.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Large, with females bigger than males.

identification.what_to_look_for: A big, dark, high-domed turtle basking at the pond, showing reddish bars on the shell. The red-tinted underside and the arrow-shaped head stripe help separate it from the similar sliders and peninsular cooter.

species.diet: Adults are mostly herbivorous, grazing on submerged aquatic plants and algae; young turtles also take some insects and other small animals.
species.behavior: Active by day. Alternates basking on logs and banks with feeding dives. Wary, sliding into the water when approached. Nests on land, sometimes within alligator nest mounds.
species.sounds: Essentially silent.
species.ecological_role: A plant-grazing turtle that helps shape underwater vegetation, with eggs and young that feed many predators — and an unusual nesting link to alligators.
species.plant_connections: Adults graze largely on submerged aquatic plants and algae, making this cooter one of the pond's natural plant-grazers.

size.shell_length: 8 to 13 in (20 to 33 cm), with large females reaching about 15 in
size.lifespan: long-lived, often 20 to 30 years or more.

species.habitat: Freshwater ponds, lakes, marshes, springs, and slow rivers with abundant aquatic plants and good basking spots.
species.where_to_look: Basking on logs and banks at the ponds on sunny days, often among sliders and peninsular cooters. Look for the reddish tones on the shell.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Most visible basking on warm, sunny days.

danger.people: Harmless if left alone; may nip if handled, so it is best not to pick one up.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and do not handle or feed it. Let basking turtles keep their sunning spots.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common in suitable Florida fresh water.

seasonality.note: Most visible basking on warm, sunny days.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The peninsular cooter has yellow hairpin-loop stripes on the head; the red-bellied cooter has a reddish belly and a red arrow mark between the eyes.
Sliders are smaller with a colored ear patch behind the eye; the red-bellied cooter is larger and lacks it.

quick_hits: The pond slider is the classic basking turtle you see lined up on logs, sliding into the water at the first hint of trouble — which is how it got its name.

Two kinds turn up in Florida, and telling them apart matters: the yellow-bellied slider, with a yellow patch behind the eye, is native here; the red-eared slider, with a red stripe behind the eye, is not.
The red-eared slider is one of the most widely released pet turtles in the world, and where people dump unwanted pets, it competes with and even interbreeds with native turtles.
Young sliders eat mostly small animals; as they grow, they shift toward a diet of aquatic plants.
species.range_and_origin: The native yellow-bellied slider (Trachemys scripta scripta) belongs to the southeastern U.S., including north Florida. The red-eared slider (Trachemys scripta elegans) is native to the Mississippi Valley, not Florida — but released pets have spread it across much of the peninsula and beyond.
more_information: 'Pond slider' covers two subspecies that look similar but tell very different stories in Florida. The yellow-bellied slider is a true native. The red-eared slider — the little green turtle once sold by the millions in pet stores — comes from farther west and does not naturally belong here.

The trouble starts when people release pet turtles they can no longer keep. Red-eared sliders are hardy and adaptable, and in the wild they compete with native turtles for basking sites and food, and can interbreed with the native yellow-bellied slider, blurring its genetic line. Globally, the red-eared slider is listed among the world's worst invasive species, largely because of the pet trade.

The takeaway for visitors is simple: enjoy the turtles basking on the park's logs, but never release a pet turtle into a pond. What looks like a kindness can quietly harm the native wildlife already living there.

blocks.label: Shell
blocks.text: Smooth, oval, olive-green to brown with yellow markings, darkening with age.

blocks.label: Head stripe
blocks.text: A yellow patch behind the eye (yellow-bellied) or a red stripe behind the eye (red-eared) — the key to telling the two apart.

blocks.label: Skin
blocks.text: Dark with yellow stripes.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Females larger than males.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium turtle basking on a log, with yellow stripes on the head and neck. Check just behind the eye: a yellow blotch points to the native yellow-bellied slider; a red stripe points to the introduced red-eared slider.

species.diet: Omnivorous. Young sliders eat mostly insects, tadpoles, snails, and small fish; adults shift toward aquatic plants and algae.
species.behavior: Active by day. Spends long stretches basking on logs and banks to warm up, sliding quickly into the water when disturbed, and forages in between.
species.sounds: Essentially silent.
species.ecological_role: A basking pond turtle that grazes vegetation and scavenges. The native yellow-bellied slider is part of Florida's natural pond life, while released red-eared sliders can crowd out native turtles.
species.plant_connections: Adults graze on submerged aquatic plants and algae (younger turtles eat more animal prey) and bask among pond-edge vegetation.

size.shell_length: up to about 12 in (30 cm); females larger than males
size.lifespan: commonly 20 to 30 years, longer in captivity.

species.habitat: Almost any quiet fresh water — ponds, lakes, marshes, canals, and slow rivers — with basking logs and soft, vegetated bottoms.
species.where_to_look: Basking on logs, banks, and debris at the ponds on sunny days, often several together. Look behind the eye to tell native from introduced.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Most visible basking on warm, sunny days.

danger.people: Harmless if left alone; like any turtle it may nip if handled, so it is best not to pick one up.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance, and never release a pet turtle into the park or any wild pond — released red-eared sliders harm native turtles. Do not handle or feed them.

invasive.notes: Mixed. The native yellow-bellied slider belongs in Florida. The red-eared slider is an introduced subspecies, spread by released pets, that competes and interbreeds with native turtles and ranks among the world's worst invasive species.

conservation.status: The species overall is Least Concern and abundant. The concern is local and genetic: released red-eared sliders pressuring native yellow-bellied sliders and red-bellied turtles.

species.internal_notes: Species-level entry spanning two subspecies: native yellow-bellied slider and introduced red-eared slider (released pets). Invasive flag = Yellow to reflect the red-eared subspecies; Conservation = Green because the species overall is abundant. Strong responsible-pet-ownership teaching animal. native:false is intentional and reflects the introduced red-eared subspecies that dominates released-pet populations here; the native yellow-bellied subspecies is acknowledged in the species text. Do not 'correct' native to true.

seasonality.note: Most visible basking on warm, sunny days.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: Sliders are smaller and have a distinct colored patch (red or yellow) just behind the eye; cooters are larger and lack the ear patch.
The slider's colored ear patch behind the eye is the giveaway; the larger red-bellied cooter has none.

quick_hits: The yellow-crowned night heron is a stocky gray wading bird with a bold black-and-white face and a creamy crown.
It is a crustacean specialist — more than most herons, it lives on crabs and crayfish, and its stout, heavy bill is built for cracking their shells.
Despite the 'night' in its name, it feeds by day as well as after dark, whenever its prey is active.
You will usually find it alone, hunched and leaning forward at the water's edge, watching the mud and shallows for movement.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the Americas and found only in this hemisphere, unlike its black-crowned cousin, which occurs worldwide. In Florida it is a year-round resident, most common around coastal marshes, mangroves, and other wetlands.
more_information: Among the herons, the yellow-crowned is the great crab specialist. Diet studies routinely find crustaceans making up the large majority of what it eats — crabs along the coast, crayfish inland — and its unusually thick, strong bill is the tool that lets it crush those hard shells.

It is also less tied to the night than its name suggests. It forages whenever crabs and crayfish are active, which can be midday as easily as midnight, usually hunting alone with a slow, deliberate, forward-leaning stalk.
Like other herons it nests in trees and shrubs near water, sometimes alone and sometimes in small colonies, building a stick platform that a pair may enlarge and reuse year after year.

blocks.label: Adults
blocks.text: Smooth gray body, a black head with a broad white cheek patch and a pale creamy crown, and long white head plumes in breeding season.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Stout, heavy, and black — noticeably thicker than other herons'.

blocks.label: Young
blocks.text: Brown with fine white spots and streaks.

blocks.label: Posture
blocks.text: Hunched and forward-leaning.

identification.what_to_look_for: A compact gray heron with a striking black-and-white face, often hunched at the water's edge while hunting — though it stands more upright and longer-legged when walking. The heavy bill and bold face pattern set it apart from the lankier, plainer herons; young birds are brown and spotted.

species.diet: Mostly crabs and crayfish, cracked open with its heavy bill, plus some fish, insects, and other small animals.
species.behavior: Forages day and night, usually alone, with a slow, hunched stalk. Roosts in trees and shrubs and nests in them too, sometimes in small colonies. Most active where crabs and crayfish are plentiful.
species.sounds: Generally quiet; gives a sharp, barking squawk, especially in flight or around the colony.
species.ecological_role: A specialized predator of crabs and crayfish, helping regulate those populations and linking the park's waters to the wider coastal food web.
species.plant_connections: Nests and roosts in waterside trees and shrubs, including mangroves — so the trees ringing a pond matter to it, even though its food comes from the water.
species.habitat: Coastal marshes, mangroves, estuaries, and tidal flats, plus inland swamps, wooded wetlands, and pond edges with crabs or crayfish.
species.where_to_look: Alone at the pond edges and shallows, hunched and watching for crayfish, or roosting quietly in waterside trees. Active by day as well as toward dusk.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, though often quiet and easy to overlook. Active in daylight as well as at night.

danger.people: Harmless to visitors. As with all herons, a frightened bird can jab with its bill, so injured ones are a job for trained rehabbers.
danger.pets: Not a danger to pets; just keep dogs from chasing one.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and do not feed it. A hunting heron needs quiet to catch its prey.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Widespread and fairly common, and not state-listed in Florida — unlike several of its wading-bird neighbors.

seasonality.note: Quiet and easy to overlook.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The black-crowned has a black crown AND back over pale gray wings; the yellow-crowned is evenly gray with a black-and-white face and creamy crown.

quick_hits: The male northern cardinal is one of the most recognizable birds in the eastern U.S. — bright red all over, with a pointed crest and a black mask around a stout orange bill.
The female is easy to overlook by comparison: warm buff-brown with red highlights, but every bit a cardinal, crest and all.
Unlike most songbirds, the female sings too, often answering the male in a back-and-forth from the shrubs.
That thick, cone-shaped bill is a seed-cracking tool, which is why cardinals are such reliable visitors to feeders and seed-bearing plants.
species.range_and_origin: Native and widespread across the eastern and central United States; a common, year-round resident throughout Florida. It does not migrate, holding the same territory all year.
more_information: The cardinal is a year-round neighbor rather than a seasonal visitor — pairs often hold the same territory through every season, which is part of why they feel so familiar.

They are unusual among songbirds in that the female sings as readily as the male, and the two will trade phrases back and forth. During courtship you may also catch a tender behavior called mate-feeding, where the male passes a seed to the female beak-to-beak.
Everything about that heavy, conical bill is built for seeds: it lets the cardinal crack tough seed coats many birds cannot, so it thrives wherever seed-bearing plants and feeders are found.

blocks.label: Male
blocks.text: Bright red overall, with a pointed crest, black face mask, and stout orange-red bill.

blocks.label: Female
blocks.text: Warm buff-brown with reddish tinges on the crest, wings, and tail, and the same orange bill.

blocks.label: Both
blocks.text: A tall crest and a short, thick, cone-shaped bill.

identification.what_to_look_for: A crested songbird with a heavy orange bill — vivid red if a male, soft brown with red accents if a female. Listen for clear, whistled 'cheer-cheer-cheer' and 'birdy-birdy-birdy' phrases from both sexes.

species.diet: Mainly seeds and grains, plus fruits, berries, and insects — especially in summer, when insects feed the growing young.
species.behavior: Active by day, year-round, usually in pairs or small family groups. Forages on or near the ground and in shrubs, and comes readily to feeders. Males may even attack their own reflection in a window during breeding season, mistaking it for a rival.
species.sounds: Clear, whistled songs — phrases like 'cheer-cheer-cheer' and 'birdy-birdy-birdy' — sung by both sexes, plus a sharp metallic 'chip' note.
species.ecological_role: A seed- and fruit-eater that helps disperse the seeds of native plants, and an insect-eater that helps keep bug numbers down while raising young.
species.plant_connections: Eats the seeds, fruits, and berries of many native shrubs and trees and nests in dense shrubbery — so seed- and berry-bearing native plantings draw cardinals in.

size.lifespan: commonly up to about 3 years, sometimes much longer.

species.habitat: Woodland edges, thickets, shrubby gardens, and backyards with dense cover for nesting and seed-bearing plants for food.
species.where_to_look: In shrubs, hedges, and woodland edges, often near the ground. The male's red is easy to spot; listen for the loud, clear whistles of both sexes.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, active by day in every season.

interaction.guidance: Simply enjoy them. A feeder with seeds is the easiest way to welcome cardinals, but they do just fine on natural food in the park.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Abundant and even expanding its range, and one of the most familiar birds in the region.

quick_hits: Despite the name, the screech-owl does not screech. Its usual call is a soft, descending whinny or an even, trilling purr — a gentle sound from such a capable little hunter.
It is small, only about the size of a soda can, with feather tufts on its head that look like ears but are not.
It comes in two color forms, gray and reddish, and both blend so well into tree bark that one can doze the day away on an open branch unnoticed.
A nighttime hunter, it takes a wide range of prey, from insects and lizards to small rodents and birds.
species.range_and_origin: The Eastern Screech-Owl ranges across the eastern United States; the form here is the Florida subspecies. It is a common, year-round resident of woodlands, suburbs, and parks anywhere there are trees with cavities for nesting.
more_information: The screech-owl is a master of staying hidden. By day it tucks into a tree cavity or sits tight against a trunk, where its bark-patterned feathers make it nearly invisible. Many people live alongside screech-owls for years without ever noticing one.
It comes in two color morphs, a gray form and a reddish form, and both can turn up in the same area, even within the same family.

Though it is a capable predator of insects, small mammals, birds, and reptiles, its voice is anything but fearsome. The usual calls are a soft, horse-like whinny that falls in pitch and a steady trilling purr — far gentler than the word 'screech' suggests. Like many owls, it nests in tree cavities and will readily take to a nest box.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Small — roughly eight to nine inches tall, about the size of a soda can.

blocks.label: Ear tufts
blocks.text: Two feather tufts that can be raised or flattened (not actually ears).

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Either gray or reddish-brown, both heavily patterned to mimic bark.

blocks.label: Eyes
blocks.text: Yellow.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small, stocky owl with ear tufts, roosting quietly in a tree cavity or against a trunk by day. It is far more often heard than seen — listen at dusk for a soft, descending whinny or a steady trill.

species.diet: A varied hunter — large insects, spiders, earthworms, small rodents, lizards, frogs, and small birds, caught mostly at night.
species.behavior: Active at night, roosting by day in cavities or tucked against bark. Hunts from a perch, dropping onto prey below. Nests in tree cavities and nest boxes. Generally solitary or in pairs.
species.sounds: Two main calls, neither a true screech: a soft, descending whinny like a small horse, and an even, trilling purr on one pitch. Most often heard at dusk and after dark.
species.ecological_role: A nighttime predator that helps keep insect and rodent numbers in check, and a cavity-dependent species — a good reason hollow and dead trees have real value in a healthy woodland.
species.plant_connections: Depends on trees with natural cavities — often old oaks and other hardwoods — for roosting and nesting, and readily uses nest boxes where old trees are scarce.

size.lifespan: commonly up to about 8 to 10 years in the wild.

species.habitat: Woodlands, forest edges, swamps, suburbs, and parks with mature trees and natural cavities for roosting and nesting.
species.where_to_look: Among mature trees — scan cavities and quiet, shaded trunks by day, and listen for soft whinnies and trills at dusk. Far more often heard than seen.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Active at night; daytime sightings usually mean a roosting bird tucked into a cavity or against bark.

danger.people: Harmless to visitors. Screech-owls are small, secretive, and pose no threat to people.
danger.pets: No danger to pets under normal circumstances.

interaction.guidance: If you find a roosting owl, enjoy it quietly from a distance and move on — daytime disturbance robs it of rest. Never disturb a nest cavity.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and adaptable, though it depends on trees with natural cavities and benefits from nest boxes where old trees are scarce.

species.internal_notes: Owl = high visitor appeal. Re-keyed 2026-06-19 from subspecies M. a. floridanus (taxon 725930) to species Megascops asio (taxon 19765); Florida race kept in also_known_as and noted in text. obs_stats updated from project view (2 obs / 2 observers: robcarr52 species-level + brice_c subspecies-level — the latter was all the old subspecies key was catching).

seasonality.note: Nocturnal; daytime sightings usually mean a roosting bird tucked into a cavity or against bark.

quick_hits: The mangrove skipper is a large, fast-flying butterfly — mostly dark blackish-brown, but lit up with streaks of electric, almost neon, blue.
Its life is tied to a single plant: the caterpillars feed only on red mangrove, so the butterfly lives only where mangroves grow along warm, salty coasts.
Florida has its own form of this butterfly, and the Bradenton area sits near the northern edge of its Gulf Coast range.
The caterpillar is a tidy little engineer, folding and silking a red mangrove leaf into a shelter to live and feed inside.
species.range_and_origin: Native to Florida, the Caribbean, and nearby regions. The Florida form (subspecies okeechobee) is found along both coasts of the southern two-thirds of the peninsula, roughly as far north as Pinellas County on the Gulf and Volusia on the Atlantic — placing our area near the northern edge of its range.
more_information: The mangrove skipper is one of the more striking skippers in Florida. Most skippers are a puzzle of browns and small spots, but this one carries unmistakable flashes of brilliant blue across its dark wings and body.

It is what biologists call a host-plant specialist: its caterpillars eat only red mangrove. That single dependence anchors the butterfly to mangrove forests and warm saltwater shorelines, and its range closely tracks where red mangroves grow. Because mangroves are cold-sensitive, hard freezes can knock back both the trees and the skippers near the northern edge of their range.

A note on its name: scientists have gone back and forth on whether the Florida and Caribbean mangrove skippers are one species or several. You may see it listed as Phocides batabano or as Phocides pigmalion; both names refer to the same beautiful blue-streaked butterfly.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Large for a skipper, dull blackish-brown, with bold electric-blue streaks.

blocks.label: Head and body
blocks.text: Dark with a white face and blue markings.

blocks.label: Flight
blocks.text: Fast and powerful; often perches under leaves.

identification.what_to_look_for: A big, dark, fast butterfly near mangroves or flowers, flashing surprising streaks of bright blue. Those neon-blue marks on an otherwise dark butterfly are the giveaway — no other Florida skipper looks like it.

species.diet: Caterpillars eat only red mangrove leaves. Adults sip nectar from a range of flowers, including mangrove blossoms, Spanish needles, citrus, and bougainvillea.
species.behavior: Active by day, especially in warm sun. A fast, strong flier that stays close to its mangrove host and nearby nectar flowers. The caterpillar builds a folded-leaf shelter on red mangrove and feeds from within it.
species.sounds: Silent, like all butterflies.
species.ecological_role: A pollinator of coastal flowers and a specialist tied to red mangrove — a living link in the mangrove ecosystem and a small indicator of healthy coastal habitat.
species.plant_connections: Red mangrove (Rhizophora mangle) is the only larval host plant — the butterfly lives only where it grows. Adults nectar at mangrove blossoms, Spanish needles, and other flowers.

size.lifespan: adults live a few weeks; active in Florida much of the year.

species.habitat: Coastal mangrove forests and edges, especially where red mangrove grows, plus nearby flowers for nectar. Restricted to warm, frost-free shorelines.
species.where_to_look: Near any mangrove or coastal plantings and at nectar-rich flowers on warm days. A localized find, since the butterfly stays close to its mangrove host.
species.when_to_see: Present in southern Florida year-round; here, near its northern Gulf limit, it is a more localized and seasonal find tied to warm weather and mangroves.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Avoid handling butterflies, whose wings are delicate, and remember that mangroves themselves are legally protected in Florida.

conservation.status: Not formally listed, but localized and entirely dependent on red mangrove. Its future is tied to the protection of mangrove habitat, which is safeguarded under Florida law.

species.internal_notes: Re-keyed 2026-06-19 from subspecies P. b. okeechobee (taxon 1607213) to species Phocides batabano (taxon 1607210); Florida race kept in also_known_as and noted in text. NOTE: this taxon is in flux on iNat (historical pigmalion/batabano lumping, active curator debate) — species roll-up may be imperfect until iNat settles. obs_stats reflect the old subspecies key — recompute at species taxon on next pipeline run.

seasonality.note: Near its northern Gulf limit here, so a localized, weather-dependent find.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The long-tailed skipper is smaller with two long tails on the hindwings; the mangrove skipper is larger, tail-less, with bolder neon-blue streaks.

quick_hits: The American white pelican is one of the largest birds in North America, with a wingspan of up to nine feet — a giant, snow-white bird with black wingtips and a long yellow-orange bill.
It is a winter visitor to Florida, not a year-round local; the adults breed far to the north and west and come south to escape the cold.
Unlike the diving brown pelican, the white pelican does not plunge from the air; it floats on the surface and scoops fish up with its bill.
It often fishes as a team, with groups herding fish into the shallows and dipping their bills together — a striking bit of cooperative hunting.
species.range_and_origin: Breeds on inland lakes of western Canada and the northwestern United States, and winters along the Gulf Coast, including Florida, and other warm regions. In Florida it is a non-breeding winter visitor.
more_information: Despite sharing a name with the familiar brown pelican, the white pelican lives very differently. It is far larger, mostly white, and tends to favor inland lakes and shallow coastal waters, often soaring in high, wheeling flocks.

Its feeding is a group effort. Rather than diving, white pelicans swim together and herd fish into shallow water, then dip their great bills in unison to scoop them up — a coordinated technique that makes them effective without ever leaving the surface.

Because Florida is a wintering ground rather than a breeding home, white pelicans appear mainly in the cooler months and then head north to nest. They are mostly silent here, drifting in serene formations on the water and in the sky.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Bright white overall, with black flight feathers that show mainly in flight.

blocks.label: Bill and pouch
blocks.text: Long and yellow-orange.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Enormous — among the largest birds on the continent.

blocks.label: Feeding
blocks.text: Floats and scoops; does not dive.

identification.what_to_look_for: A huge white bird with black wingtips, floating in groups on open water or soaring high in slow circles. If a pelican is plunge-diving at the coast, that is the smaller, darker brown pelican; the white pelican scoops from the surface, usually inland.

species.diet: Mainly fish, scooped from the water at the surface, often while fishing cooperatively in groups.
species.behavior: Active by day and highly social. Fishes in coordinated groups on the water and soars in high flocks, often in V-formation. Spends the Florida season feeding and resting before migrating north to breed.
species.sounds: Mostly silent, especially on the wintering grounds.
species.ecological_role: A large surface-feeding fish predator that, as a seasonal visitor, links Florida's waters to the interior North American lakes where it breeds.
species.habitat: Shallow inland lakes, reservoirs, estuaries, and calm coastal waters. In winter it favors large, quiet bodies of water with abundant fish.
species.where_to_look: On open pond water in the cooler months, usually in a group. A seasonal treat rather than a bird you will see in summer.
species.when_to_see: Winter visitor, typically present from late fall into early spring, then gone north to breed. Sightings are occasional and seasonal.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and do not feed it. White pelicans are shy and easily flushed, and discarded fishing line is a serious hazard to them — pack it out.

invasive.notes: Native to North America and a natural winter visitor to Florida. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Populations are generally stable, though the species can be sensitive to disturbance at its northern breeding colonies and to fishing-line entanglement.

species.internal_notes: Winter visitor — single sighting Dec 2025. Confirm seasonality; do not imply year-round.

seasonality.note: Breeds far to the north and west; gone for the summer.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: In flight both are white with black wing edges, but the white pelican has a huge orange bill and pouch and short legs; the wood stork has long trailing legs, a bare dark head, and a down-curved bill.

quick_hits: The osprey is a large fish-hunting hawk found near water almost worldwide, easy to know by its white underside and dark eye-stripe.
It lives on live fish almost exclusively — and dives feet-first into the water to grab them, sometimes going completely under.
Its feet are specially built for the job: a reversible outer toe and rough, spiny pads let it lock onto slippery fish like a pair of fish-grippers.
Like the bald eagle, the osprey was hit hard by the pesticide DDT and has rebounded strongly since the chemical was banned.
species.range_and_origin: Found on every continent except Antarctica, near both fresh and salt water. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident, with extra birds passing through and wintering.
more_information: The osprey is a fishing specialist unlike any other North American raptor. It hunts by hovering over the water, then folding its wings and plunging feet-first to snatch a fish from at or just below the surface.

Everything about its feet is tuned for gripping slippery prey: the outer toe swivels backward so it can hold a fish with two toes front and two back, and the soles are covered in short spines. An osprey will even rotate a caught fish to carry it head-first, cutting wind resistance in flight.
Ospreys readily build their bulky stick nests on poles, channel markers, and platforms, which has helped them thrive around people — a recovery, like the eagle's, made possible by the banning of DDT.

blocks.label: Underside
blocks.text: Mostly white, with a white head crossed by a bold dark stripe through the eye.

blocks.label: Upperparts
blocks.text: Dark brown.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: Long wings held with a distinctive bend, or 'crook,' like a shallow M.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Hovers over water, then plunges feet-first.

identification.what_to_look_for: A large white-bellied hawk over water, hovering and then diving feet-first, or carrying a fish head-forward. The dark eye-stripe and crooked wings separate it from the bald eagle, which is bigger, darker-bodied, and white-headed.

species.diet: Almost entirely live fish, caught by diving feet-first. It very rarely takes anything else.
species.behavior: Active by day. Hunts on the wing over open water, hovering and plunging. Builds large stick nests on tall, open supports, often reusing them year after year.
species.sounds: A series of clear, high, whistled chirps, often given around the nest.
species.ecological_role: A specialized fish predator near the top of the aquatic food chain, and — because it sits at the top and is sensitive to pollution — a useful indicator of healthy water and fish stocks.
species.plant_connections: Nests in tall trees and on snags near water (and readily on poles and platforms).

size.lifespan: commonly 7 to 10 years, sometimes 20 or more.

species.habitat: Near almost any fish-bearing water — lakes, rivers, reservoirs, estuaries, and coasts — with tall, open structures or trees for nesting.
species.where_to_look: Overhead or perched on tall snags and poles near water, sometimes hovering before a dive. More regular than the bald eagle but still a treat to catch.
species.when_to_see: Florida's own ospreys are largely year-round residents, joined each winter by migrants from farther north. Often seen flying over or perched near water.

danger.people: No threat to visitors. Ospreys focus entirely on fish and keep their distance from people.

interaction.guidance: Enjoy from a distance and do not disturb nests. Active nests are protected, and approaching one can cause the birds to abandon eggs or young.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and found nearly worldwide. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Populations recovered strongly after DDT was banned in the 1970s, helped by their willingness to nest on man-made platforms.

species.internal_notes: Osprey sits in its own family (Pandionidae), not with the other hawks/eagles — nice 'good to know' angle.

seasonality.note: Local residents joined by northern migrants in winter.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The bald eagle is far larger with a dark body and, when adult, a white head and tail; the osprey is smaller, white below with a dark eye-stripe, and crooks its wings into an M in flight.

quick_hits: The bald eagle is the national bird of the United States — a huge raptor with a snow-white head and tail, dark body, and bright yellow bill.
Florida has one of the densest concentrations of nesting bald eagles in the lower 48 states, with an estimated 1,500 nesting pairs — making it one of the great strongholds for this iconic species.

The world-record largest tree nest ever documented — for any animal species — belonged to a pair of bald eagles just down the road in St. Petersburg, Florida. It measured 9.5 feet wide, 20 feet deep, and weighed almost 6,000 pounds.

It is not actually bald; the name comes from an old word meaning white-headed, and juvenile eagles are completely chocolate brown and mottled with white — they require four to five years to acquire their definitive adult plumage.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North America, found near water across most of the continent. Florida has one of the densest concentrations of nesting bald eagles in the lower 48 states, with an estimated 1,500 nesting pairs — and a nesting season unlike anywhere else in the country.
more_information: For all its power, the bald eagle is a practical hunter. It catches fish from the water's surface, takes waterbirds and small mammals, scavenges carrion, and is famous for harassing ospreys until they drop their catch.

Eagles pair for the long term and build the largest tree nests of any animal on Earth, adding to them year after year until they can weigh thousands of pounds. The world record belongs to a pair near St. Petersburg, Florida — their nest measured 9.5 feet across, 20 feet deep, and weighed almost 6,000 pounds.

Florida's bald eagles run on an inverted nesting calendar compared to their northern counterparts. Rather than nesting in spring, they return to their territories in October, lay most eggs between December and early January, and fledge young by May — timing their breeding to avoid the worst summer heat and to take advantage of Florida's dry-season fish concentrations in shallow water. Most breeding adults, especially in the southern peninsula, remain in Florida year-round; it is subadults and non-breeding birds that migrate north for the summer.

The eagle's recovery is a landmark conservation story. By the mid-1900s, DDT was thinning eggshells and crashing populations across the country. After DDT was banned in 1972 and federal protections took hold, the species rebounded dramatically. It was delisted nationally in 2007 and remains protected under the Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act and the Migratory Bird Treaty Act. Want to help? Audubon Florida's EagleWatch program trains volunteer citizen scientists to monitor nests across the state — one of the premier community science programs in Florida.

blocks.label: Adults
blocks.text: Massive dark brown body with an unmistakable white head and tail; the heavy hooked bill, eyes, and powerful legs are all brilliant yellow.

blocks.label: Young birds
blocks.text: No white head — instead a large, dark brown bird with mottled white on the underwings and belly. Often confused with a Golden Eagle, which is exceptionally rare in Florida.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: Very large, with broad, plank-like wings held flat — unlike the turkey vulture, which tilts its wings in a shallow V.

identification.what_to_look_for: A big dark raptor with a brilliant white head and tail, perched in a tall tree near water or soaring on flat wings. An all-brown bird of similar size with white mottling is likely a young bald eagle. If the wings tilt in a V-shape, look again — that's probably a turkey vulture.

species.diet: Mainly fish, plus waterbirds, small mammals, and carrion. Often steals catches from ospreys and other birds.
species.behavior: Active by day. Hunts from a perch or on the wing, soars on thermals, and roosts and nests near water. In Florida, pairs invert the typical northern nesting calendar: they return to territories in October, lay eggs between December and early January, and fledge young by May — timing the season to cooler temperatures and peak fish availability in shallow water.
species.sounds: A surprisingly weak, high, chattering or whistling call — far less dramatic than the screams Hollywood usually dubs over eagle footage (those are often a red-tailed hawk).
species.ecological_role: A top predator and scavenger that helps keep fish, waterbird, and carrion in balance, and a powerful symbol of healthy, protected waterways.
species.plant_connections: Builds its massive nests in tall living trees near water.

size.weight: 6.5 to 14 lb (3 to 6.3 kg); females are larger than males
size.lifespan: commonly 20 years or more in the wild.

species.habitat: Lakes, rivers, reservoirs, and coastlines with tall trees for nesting and perching and plenty of fish and waterbirds.
species.where_to_look: Overhead or perched high in a large tree near water. A genuine thrill when it happens, but an occasional visitor rather than an everyday sight.
species.when_to_see: Present in Florida year-round, but Florida's breeding schedule is the reverse of northern populations. Eagles return to local territories in October, with egg-laying peaking between December and early January and young fledging by May. Most breeding adults — especially in the southern peninsula — stay in Florida year-round; it is subadults and non-breeding birds that head north for summer. Late fall through spring is the best window for a park sighting.

danger.people: No threat to visitors. Bald eagles are wary of people and keep their distance; those powerful talons are for fish and prey, not humans.
danger.pets: Very rarely an issue, though it is always wise to supervise very small pets outdoors around any large raptor.

interaction.guidance: Enjoy from a distance and never disturb a nest. Bald eagles, their active nests, and their nest trees are strictly protected under the federal Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act — approaching a nest can carry real legal penalties as well as harming the birds. Maintain at least 330 feet from any nesting area and use binoculars or a zoom lens. Local nests are monitored by volunteer citizen scientists through Audubon Florida's EagleWatch program.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern and recovered. After DDT-driven declines, the bald eagle was removed from the U.S. endangered species list in 2007 (and Florida's list in 2008). It remains protected under the Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act and the Migratory Bird Treaty Act.

species.internal_notes: Single sighting (Mar 2026). Verify it's a recurring visitor before promising it on signage.

seasonality.note: Florida eagles invert the northern nesting calendar: pairs return to territories in October, lay eggs December–January, and fledge young by May. Most breeding adults remain year-round; subadults and non-breeders migrate north for summer. Park sightings are occasional flyovers or perched birds, most likely late fall through spring.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The osprey is smaller, white underneath with a dark eye-mask and crooked wings; the bald eagle is much bigger and bulkier, adults with a white head and tail.

quick_hits: Most waterfowl nest on the ground, but the wood duck nests up high — inside hollow tree cavities, often old woodpecker holes in cypress and oaks along wooded, swampy shorelines.

Unlike most ducks, wood ducks have strong, sharp claws on their webbed feet — built for gripping bark and perching high in the canopy, which is exactly where you may spot one along the wooded edges of the pond.
The day after hatching, the ducklings make a leap of faith — jumping from the high nest hole, sometimes dozens of feet down, and bouncing unhurt before following their mother to the water.
A century ago wood ducks were nearly gone, hit hard by overhunting and the loss of bottomland forest; hunting limits and a huge nest-box effort brought them back.
species.range_and_origin: Native to North America, common across the eastern United States and the Pacific Northwest. In Florida it is a year-round resident, and winter brings additional birds from the north.
more_information: The wood duck is a bird of wooded swamps and shaded water, and its whole life is tied to trees. It nests in natural cavities, often old woodpecker holes, which sets it apart from the ground-nesting ducks of open marshes.

That cavity-nesting habit leads to a remarkable sight. A day or so after hatching, the tiny ducklings climb to the cavity entrance and jump, tumbling to the ground or water below and bouncing unharmed thanks to their light, downy bodies, then trailing their mother off to feed.

By the early 1900s, unregulated hunting and the clearing of bottomland forests had pushed wood ducks to the brink. Hunting limits under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act, habitat protection, and a huge volunteer nest-box effort turned the species around — one of conservation's quieter success stories.

blocks.label: Male
blocks.text: Boldly patterned — a glossy green and chestnut head with white stripes, a swept-back crest, and red eyes.

blocks.label: Female
blocks.text: Soft gray-brown with a distinctive white teardrop around the eye.

blocks.label: Shape
