blocks.text: A slim duck with a boxy, crested head, often holding the head low.

identification.what_to_look_for: Look for a slim, boxy-headed duck moving through deeply shaded, wooded water rather than out in the open. Wood ducks favor tucked-away spots near overhanging branches — and if approached too closely, they'll flush fast with a loud, rising squeal.

species.diet: Seeds, acorns, fruits, and aquatic plants, along with insects and other small invertebrates. Acorns are a favorite in fall and winter.
species.behavior: Active by day, usually in pairs or small groups, often staying near cover at the edge of shaded water. Perches readily in trees — unusual for a duck — and nests in tree cavities or nest boxes.
species.sounds: The female gives a loud, rising 'oo-eek' squeal when flushed; the male a softer, thin whistle.
species.ecological_role: Spreads the seeds of wetland and bottomland plants and is part of the food web as both forager and prey. Its reliance on tree cavities makes it a flagship for protecting wooded wetlands.
species.plant_connections: Eats acorns, seeds, fruits, and aquatic plants, and nests in tree cavities (often old woodpecker holes) in wooded wetlands.

size.lifespan: usually 3 to 4 years, occasionally much longer.

species.habitat: Wooded swamps, shaded ponds, slow wooded streams, and marshes with nearby trees for nesting. Favors quiet water with overhanging cover.
species.where_to_look: On quieter, shadier stretches of the ponds and wooded edges, often in pairs near overhanging cover rather than out in open water.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, with more birds present in winter. Most likely along sheltered, wooded water.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and do not feed it. Wood ducks are shy and stick to cover; bread and snacks harm waterfowl health.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern today, and a notable comeback from near-collapse in the early 1900s thanks to hunting limits, habitat protection, and nest-box programs.

quick_hits: The tricolored heron is a slender, elegant wader in blue-gray and purple with a clean white belly — the white stripe down its front is the quickest field mark.
It is an active, almost theatrical hunter, dashing, lunging, and twisting through the shallows after small fish rather than standing still.
It tends to hunt alone, a bit more solitary than the other wading birds it shares the ponds with.
Once known as the Louisiana heron, it is now a Threatened species in Florida, with declining numbers in the Tampa Bay area.
species.range_and_origin: Native to coastal regions of the Americas, from the U.S. Atlantic and Gulf coasts through the Caribbean to northern South America. In Florida it is a widespread, year-round resident, though less common in parts of the Panhandle.
more_information: Where a great blue heron hunts by patience, the tricolored heron hunts by motion. It runs through the shallows with wings half-raised, stabs, pivots, and chases — an energetic style that is fun to watch and well suited to catching small, fast fish.
It is also more of a loner than most herons, often feeding by itself even where other waders gather, though it still nests in mixed colonies.

Its status is a concern: the tricolored heron is listed as Threatened in Florida, and biologists have documented declines around Tampa Bay tied to lost nesting habitat, predation, and disturbance — including the danger of discarded fishing line.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Blue-gray head, back, and wings washed with purple, and a distinctive white belly and underparts.

blocks.label: Neck
blocks.text: Long and slender, often with a rusty-and-white stripe down the front.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Long, thin, and pointed.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Active, darting feeding style.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium, slim heron that is dark above and clean white below, working the shallows in quick, restless movements. That white belly on an otherwise dark heron is the giveaway, and the active hunting style separates it from the patient great blue and the all-white egrets.

species.diet: Mainly small fish, plus aquatic insects, crustaceans, and amphibians, caught with quick stabs during active pursuit.
species.behavior: Active by day and an energetic forager, often hunting alone. Wades, runs, and lunges through shallow water. Nests in colonies with other wading birds between roughly February and August.
species.sounds: Generally quiet away from the colony, where it gives groans and squawks.
species.ecological_role: A small-fish specialist that helps balance shallow-water fish populations, and a sensitive member of Florida's wading-bird colonies whose decline signals wetland stress.
species.habitat: Fresh and saltwater marshes, estuaries, mangrove swamps, lagoons, and pond edges with shallow water for hunting.
species.where_to_look: Along the pond shallows and edges, usually hunting alone with quick, darting movements. Look for the dark heron with the bright white belly.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Most often seen actively feeding in the shallows during the day.

danger.people: Harmless to visitors. As with all herons, a frightened bird can jab with its sharp bill, so injured ones should be left to trained rehabbers.
danger.pets: Not a danger to pets; just keep dogs from chasing or cornering one.

interaction.guidance: Keep your distance and do not feed it. As a Threatened species, it is especially worth giving room — and please pack out any fishing line, which entangles wading birds.

conservation.status: A State-designated Threatened species in Florida, and globally Least Concern. Florida populations, including around Tampa Bay, have been declining due to habitat loss, predation, and disturbance.

quick_hits: The Florida softshell turtle looks like no other turtle in the state — a flat, leathery, pancake-shaped reptile with a long, snorkel-like snout.
Its shell is not the hard dome you expect; it is soft and rubbery, covered in skin rather than hard plates.
That snorkel nose lets it breathe at the surface while staying almost completely hidden underwater, just the nostrils poking up.
It is the largest softshell turtle in North America, and a powerful one: handled or cornered, it can deliver a serious bite, so it is strictly a look-but-don't-touch animal.
species.range_and_origin: Native to Florida and the surrounding southeastern coastal plain. It is one of the most common large turtles in Florida's fresh waters.
more_information: Almost everything about the Florida softshell is built for an ambush life underwater. The flat, flexible shell and webbed feet make it a fast swimmer, and the long snout works like a snorkel: the turtle can rest on the bottom of a shallow pond and simply extend its nose to the surface to breathe, staying out of sight.
It is a hunter, not a grazer. The softshell eats fish, snails, frogs, crayfish, and other animals, and its long neck and strong jaws can strike with surprising speed and force.

That bite is the reason for caution. Florida softshells are not aggressive toward people in the water and pose no threat to someone simply watching, but a cornered or handled one can bite hard — a good reason to admire them and leave them be.

blocks.label: Shell
blocks.text: Flat, round, and leathery rather than hard; olive to dark brown, often with darker blotches in young turtles.

blocks.label: Snout
blocks.text: Long and tube-like, with the nostrils at the tip.

blocks.label: Feet
blocks.text: Broadly webbed for swimming.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Females grow much larger than males.

identification.what_to_look_for: A flat, pancake-shaped turtle with a pointed, snorkel-like nose, either basking on a bank or showing just its nostrils at the water's surface. The soft, leathery shell tells it apart instantly from the park's hard-shelled cooters and sliders.

species.diet: A carnivore — fish, snails, frogs, crayfish, insects, and other small aquatic animals, and occasionally even small waterbirds.
species.behavior: Active by day. Spends much of its time buried in sand or mud at the bottom of shallow water, ambushing prey, and surfacing to breathe through its snorkel snout. Females come ashore to dig nests and lay eggs.
species.sounds: Essentially silent.
species.ecological_role: A top turtle predator of Florida's ponds, helping control fish and invertebrate numbers, while its eggs are an important food for raccoons, birds, and other animals.

size.shell_length: females reach about 24 in (61 cm) — the largest softshell in North America; males are much smaller, around 6 to 12 in
size.weight: large females can exceed 40 lb (18 kg)

species.habitat: Ponds, lakes, canals, marshes, and slow rivers with soft, sandy or muddy bottoms it can burrow into.
species.where_to_look: At the ponds — often only the pointed snout breaking the surface, or the whole turtle basking on a muddy bank. Give it room; it will slip away fast if disturbed.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Most often seen surfacing to breathe or basking on warm days.

danger.people: Harmless to people who keep their distance, but a cornered or handled softshell can bite hard with strong jaws and a fast, long-reaching neck. Never try to pick one up or block its path.
danger.pets: A dog that harasses one could receive a serious bite. Keep pets away from any large turtle at the water's edge.

interaction.guidance: Look but do not touch. Do not handle, feed, or corner it. Observe from a comfortable distance and let it move freely.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common across Florida, though like many turtles it benefits from clean water and protected nesting areas.

species.internal_notes: Distinctive pancake-flat turtle — easy pond spotting.

quick_hits: The showstopper of Florida's wetlands, instantly recognizable by its vibrant pink plumage and unique, flat bill.
That pink is not built in: spoonbills get their color from their food, the same way flamingos do, absorbing pigments from the shrimp and small creatures they eat.
The spoon-shaped bill is a feeding tool. The bird sweeps it side to side through shallow water and snaps it shut by touch the instant it feels prey.
A little over a century ago plume hunters nearly wiped them out for their feathers; the species recovered, but it is still listed as Threatened in Florida.
species.range_and_origin: The only spoonbill native to the Americas, ranging from the southern United States through Central and South America. In Florida it is found mainly in the central and southern peninsula, and the Tampa Bay region — close to home — holds one of the state's most important breeding populations.
more_information: People often mistake the roseate spoonbill for a flamingo, and the pink does come from the same source: carotenoid pigments in the small aquatic animals it eats. A well-fed spoonbill is a deep rosy pink; a younger or leaner one is paler.

Its bill is a marvel of touch-based feeding. Rather than looking for prey, the spoonbill sweeps that flat 'spoon' through the shallows and clamps down the moment something brushes the sensitive inside — letting it feed effectively even in muddy water.

The species has a hard history. During the plume-hunting era of the late 1800s and early 1900s, spoonbills were slaughtered for their feathers and nearly vanished from Florida, dropping to just a few dozen breeding pairs. Legal protection brought them back, but they remain a State-designated Threatened species, and biologists watch their Florida Bay colonies closely as an indicator of Everglades health.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Pink body with deeper red on the wings and a white neck; legs pinkish-red.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Long, gray, and flattened into a spoon shape at the tip — unmistakable.

blocks.label: Head
blocks.text: Bare and pale greenish in adults.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: Flies with neck outstretched, showing off the rosy wings.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium-large pink wading bird swinging a flat, spoon-tipped bill side to side through shallow water. Nothing else in Florida looks quite like it. If you are wondering whether it is a flamingo, check the bill — the spoonbill's is straight and flat, not down-bent.

species.diet: Small fish, shrimp, crayfish, crabs, aquatic insects, and other small creatures, sifted from shallow water by feel with that spoon-shaped bill.
species.behavior: Active by day, usually in small flocks, often mixing with other wading birds. Feeds by wading slowly and sweeping its bill through the shallows. Nests in colonies in trees and shrubs over or near water.
species.sounds: Generally quiet, giving low grunts and clucking sounds, mostly around the colony.
species.ecological_role: A touch-feeding specialist of shallow wetlands whose breeding success tracks water conditions, making it a closely watched indicator of wetland and Everglades health.
species.plant_connections: Nests in waterside trees and shrubs, including mangroves.
species.habitat: Shallow fresh and brackish water — mangrove edges, estuaries, coastal lagoons, marshes, and mudflats — with the small aquatic prey it sweeps up.
species.where_to_look: In the shallows and along the pond edges. Their bright pink plumage stands out instantly against the water, though they are more occasional, nomadic visitors compared to our resident herons and egrets.
species.when_to_see: Present in Florida year-round, but it wanders widely in search of feeding areas, so park sightings are more occasional than for the resident herons.

interaction.guidance: Admire from a distance and never feed it. As a Threatened species sensitive to disturbance, spoonbills especially deserve their space.

conservation.status: A State-designated Threatened species in Florida, and globally Least Concern. It recovered from near-extinction during the plume-hunting era, but habitat loss and changing water conditions keep it on Florida's watch list.

species.internal_notes: High visitor 'wow' factor. Recent genus from Ajaia — current name used.

seasonality.note: Wanders widely in search of feeding areas, so sightings are sporadic.

quick_hits: The peninsular cooter is a large, dark freshwater turtle you will most often spot basking on a log or bank, soaking up the sun — often several piled together.
Look for the bright yellow pinstripes running along its dark neck and shell; they are the easiest way to recognize it.
Adults are almost entirely vegetarian, grazing on underwater plants, which is unusual among Florida's pond turtles.

All that basking has a purpose: warming in the sun helps the cold-blooded turtle digest its fibrous, plant-based meals. Approach too quickly and you will hear a series of quiet plops as the whole row slips back into the water.
species.range_and_origin: Native to peninsular Florida and found almost exclusively here. A close relative of other cooters and sliders found across the southeastern United States.
more_information: The peninsular cooter is one of the big, dark turtles you will see hauled out and basking on logs, banks, and floating debris on sunny days. Basking is not just for show — it helps the turtle warm up, dry off, and fight skin parasites and algae.

While young cooters eat some insects and other small animals, adults shift to a largely plant-based diet, grazing on submerged aquatic vegetation. That makes them important grazers in the pond ecosystem.
Cooters are long-lived and slow to mature, so the adults basking at the park's ponds may well be older than many of the visitors watching them.

blocks.label: Shell
blocks.text: Domed and dark olive to brown, often with faint yellowish markings, growing dull and worn with age.

blocks.label: Skin
blocks.text: Dark with thin yellow stripes on the head, neck, and legs.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Large — clearly bigger than a slider when full grown; females larger than males.

identification.what_to_look_for: A big, dark turtle basking on a log or bank, or just its head poking above the water as it swims. The combination of large size, domed dark shell, and yellow head stripes points to a cooter rather than the smaller, more boldly marked pond slider.

species.diet: Adults are mostly herbivorous, grazing on submerged aquatic plants and algae. Young turtles also eat insects, snails, and other small aquatic animals.
species.behavior: Active by day. Spends much of its time basking to regulate temperature, alternating with feeding dives. Wary of people and quick to drop into the water when approached.
species.sounds: Essentially silent.
species.ecological_role: As a plant-grazing turtle, the cooter helps shape underwater vegetation, and its eggs and young feed a range of predators. A healthy basking population is a good sign of a healthy pond.
species.plant_connections: Adults graze on submerged native vegetation such as eelgrass (tapegrass) and pondweeds, along with algae — making the cooter one of the pond's natural plant-grazers.

size.shell_length: 9 to 13 in (23 to 33 cm), with large females reaching about 15 in

species.habitat: Freshwater ponds, lakes, slow rivers, springs, and canals with plenty of aquatic plants and good basking spots like logs and banks.
species.where_to_look: Basking on logs, banks, and debris at the ponds on sunny days, or as a dark head moving across the water's surface.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Most visible while basking on warm, sunny days.

danger.people: Harmless if left alone. Like any turtle it may nip if picked up, so it is best not to handle it.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and do not handle or feed it. Basking turtles need their sun time; approaching just sends them diving off their logs.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common in suitable Florida freshwater habitat.

species.internal_notes: Count includes 1 genus-level 'Cooters' observation folded in.

seasonality.note: Most visible basking on warm, sunny days.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The red-bellied cooter shows a reddish wash below and a red arrow-shaped mark on the head; the peninsular cooter has yellow hairpin-loop stripes and no red.
Sliders are smaller and wear a colored patch just behind the eye; the peninsular cooter is larger with no ear patch.

quick_hits: The double-crested cormorant is a sleek black diving bird that you will often see swimming low in the water or perched with its wings held open to dry.
Like the anhinga it shares the ponds with, its feathers are not fully waterproof — a trade-off that lets it dive and chase fish underwater.
It is a strong, agile underwater swimmer, propelling itself with webbed feet to catch fish in fast pursuit.
The easiest way to tell it from the similar anhinga: the cormorant has a hook-tipped bill and a shorter tail, while the anhinga's bill is straight and pointed.
species.range_and_origin: Native and widespread across North America, from Alaska and Canada to Mexico. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident on lakes, ponds, rivers, and coastlines wherever there are fish.
more_information: The double-crested cormorant is one of North America's most adaptable water birds, equally at home on freshwater ponds and saltwater coasts.

Like the anhinga, it lacks fully waterproof plumage, so after fishing it perches in the open with wings spread to dry and warm up. Unlike the anhinga, which spears its prey, the cormorant grabs fish in its hooked bill after chasing them down underwater.
The name comes from two small tufts of feathers that appear on the head only briefly, during the breeding season, so most of the year you will not see the crests at all.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: All dark, blackish, with a patch of orange-yellow bare skin at the base of the bill.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Slender with a distinct hook at the tip.

blocks.label: Eyes
blocks.text: Bright blue-green up close.

blocks.label: In the water
blocks.text: Swims low with its bill tilted slightly upward.

identification.what_to_look_for: A dark water bird swimming low or perched with wings spread to dry. The hooked bill, orange face patch, and shorter tail separate it from the anhinga, whose bill is straight and tail longer.

species.diet: Mainly fish, caught by diving and chasing them underwater. It also takes the occasional crayfish, amphibian, or aquatic insect.
species.behavior: Active by day. Dives repeatedly to fish, then perches on snags, posts, or banks to dry its wings. Often seen in loose groups, and nests in colonies, sometimes alongside herons and anhingas.
species.sounds: Usually silent away from the colony, where it gives low, pig-like grunts.
species.ecological_role: An efficient fish predator that helps keep fish populations in balance, and part of the larger waterbird community sharing the park's ponds.

size.lifespan: commonly around 6 years, sometimes 20 or more.

species.habitat: Fresh and salt water of almost any kind — ponds, lakes, rivers, reservoirs, and coastlines — with fish to catch and perches for drying.
species.where_to_look: On the open ponds, swimming low, and perched on snags, posts, or banks with wings spread to dry in the sun.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Often easiest to spot perched and drying its wings in the morning.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and do not feed it. Give perched, wing-drying birds space so they can warm up undisturbed.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Widespread and common; populations rebounded after the DDT era of the mid-1900s.

species.internal_notes: Recent genus change from Phalacrocorax — current name reflected here.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The anhinga has a straight, pointed bill and a long fan-shaped tail; the cormorant's bill is hook-tipped and its tail shorter.

quick_hits: The eastern gray squirrel is the bushy-tailed acrobat of Florida's oaks — equally at home leaping through the canopy and raiding a bird feeder.
It is a dedicated scatter-hoarder, burying thousands of nuts one at a time and relying on memory and smell to find them again later.
It never finds them all — and the nuts it forgets sprout into trees, making the squirrel an accidental but genuine forest planter.
It can head down a tree trunk face-first, thanks to ankles that rotate almost backward to grip the bark.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the eastern United States, including all of Florida. It is a familiar year-round resident of forests, parks, and neighborhoods anywhere there are trees, especially oaks.
more_information: The gray squirrel's whole lifestyle revolves around food storage. In fall it gathers acorns and other nuts and buries them one by one across its territory — a strategy called scatter-hoarding — then recovers them through winter using a sharp memory and an even sharper sense of smell.

It is far from perfect at the recovery part, and that is good news for the forest. The acorns a squirrel forgets are effectively planted, buried at just the right depth to sprout. In a very real sense, some of the oaks shading this park owe their start to generations of forgetful squirrels.

They are also built for life in the trees. A gray squirrel can rotate its hind feet nearly 180 degrees, letting it grip the bark and head down a trunk face-first, and its long tail serves as a counterbalance, a rudder in mid-leap, a sunshade, and a blanket.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Usually gray above with a paler, whitish belly; some individuals are unusually dark or reddish-brown.

blocks.label: Tail
blocks.text: Long, broad, and very bushy, often held in a curve over the back.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Clearly larger than a chipmunk, smaller than a cat.

identification.what_to_look_for: A gray, bushy-tailed rodent bounding across the ground or spiraling up an oak. The big, flicking tail and the habit of freezing, then dashing, are dead giveaways.

species.diet: Mostly acorns, nuts, and seeds, plus buds, flowers, fungi, berries, and the occasional insect or egg. Quick to take advantage of bird feeders and picnic leftovers.
species.behavior: Active by day, year-round (it does not hibernate). Spends its time foraging, caching food, and chasing other squirrels through the trees. Builds leafy nests called dreys high in the branches, and stays constantly alert for hawks, owls, and other predators.
species.sounds: Surprisingly vocal — sharp kuk-kuk barks and chatters, plus a buzzy alarm call given with flicks of the tail to warn others of danger.
species.ecological_role: A keystone tree-planter: by burying and forgetting nuts, gray squirrels help oaks and other trees regenerate. They are also important prey for hawks, owls, and snakes, linking the treetops to the rest of the food web.
species.plant_connections: Oaks and other nut trees — the squirrel eats their acorns and nuts and, by burying and forgetting them, helps plant new trees.

size.length: 17 to 20 in (43 to 51 cm), including the tail
size.lifespan: often only a few years in the wild, but can exceed 10 years.

species.habitat: Forests and woodlands with nut-bearing trees, especially oaks, plus parks, campuses, and leafy neighborhoods. Needs trees for nesting and escape.
species.where_to_look: In and under the oaks and other large trees — on the ground foraging, racing up trunks, or leaping between branches. Active throughout the day.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident, active in every season and through most of the daylight hours.

danger.people: Harmless if left alone. Like any wild animal, a squirrel can bite if cornered or hand-fed, so it is best not to try to touch or feed them.
danger.pets: No real danger to pets, though dogs love to give chase. The squirrel is far quicker and usually well out of reach.

interaction.guidance: Enjoy them, but do not hand-feed. Fed squirrels lose their wariness, can become nippy, and a human diet is not good for them.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the eastern U.S. Not invasive here. (Notably, gray squirrels introduced to Europe have become invasive there — but in Florida they belong.)

conservation.status: Least Concern. Abundant and adaptable, and one of the most commonly seen mammals in the state.

species.internal_notes: The land-mammal crowd-pleaser, especially for kids.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: Florida's fox squirrel is much larger, often black-and-tan with a dark face and pale nose and ears; the gray squirrel is smaller and uniformly gray.

quick_hits: The mottled duck is Florida's own duck — a subspecies found almost nowhere else, living in the state's marshes and ponds all year long.
Unlike most ducks, it does not migrate; it is a true Florida resident, hatched, raised, and living out its life within the state.
Males and females look alike — warm mottled brown — so the easiest way to tell them apart is the bill: yellow-green on the male, orange-and-dark on the female.
Its biggest threat is an unexpected one: interbreeding with released domestic mallards, which is slowly diluting the wild Florida population.
species.range_and_origin: The Florida mottled duck is a non-migratory subspecies endemic to peninsular Florida — it lives only here, year-round. (A separate population of mottled ducks occurs along the Gulf Coast of Texas and Louisiana.)
more_information: Most dabbling ducks breed up north and only visit Florida in winter. The mottled duck flips that script: it stays put, nesting and raising young in Florida's fresh and brackish marshes from late winter into summer.

That homebody lifestyle is also its vulnerability. People release domestic and feral mallards onto Florida ponds — often with good intentions — and those mallards interbreed with mottled ducks. The hybrids (locally nicknamed muddled ducks) are fertile, so each generation blends the two further. Wildlife biologists rank this genetic mixing as the single biggest threat to the pure Florida mottled duck, and state law actually prohibits releasing mallards because of it.
The park's own records include a few of these mottled-duck-by-mallard hybrids, a small local window onto a statewide conservation problem.

blocks.label: Plumage
blocks.text: Both sexes are warm, mottled brown overall, with a slightly paler, plainer head and neck and a darker body.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Male's is olive-green to yellow; female's is orange with dark blotches.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: Shows a blue-purple wing patch (speculum), usually without the bold white borders a female mallard shows.

identification.what_to_look_for: A brown duck that looks a bit like a female mallard but with a plainer, lighter head. Check the bill color to tell the sexes apart. Birds showing odd patches of white, a curled tail feather, or a hint of green on the head are likely mallard hybrids, not pure mottled ducks.

species.diet: About 40 percent animal matter — insects, snails, small fish, and crayfish — and 60 percent plant matter, including the seeds, stems, and roots of marsh plants. Feeds by dabbling and tipping up in shallow water.
species.behavior: Active by day, usually in pairs or small groups rather than large flocks. Dabbles in shallow water and grazes marsh edges. Nests on the ground in dense vegetation near water, mainly February through July.
species.sounds: The female gives a mallard-like quack; the male a quieter, raspier call.
species.ecological_role: A native marsh duck that spreads plant seeds and helps cycle nutrients in wetlands. Its decline is a warning sign about both wetland loss and the effects of releasing non-native animals.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on the seeds, stems, and roots of marsh plants, alongside aquatic invertebrates.
species.habitat: Florida's fresh and brackish marshes, ponds, lakes, canals, ditches, and stormwater impoundments — wherever there is shallow water and marsh vegetation.
species.where_to_look: On and around the ponds, often in pairs. Look for a plain-headed brown duck; check the bill color and watch for hybrid traits.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident — one of the few ducks you can count on in Florida in any season. Most likely to be seen on the open ponds.

interaction.guidance: We love all ducks — but local ponds stay healthiest when domestic mallards don't mix with our wild, non-migratory mottled ducks. Please don't feed the ducks (bread and snacks harm their health and encourage the very mallard releases that threaten this species), and never release pet or feed-store mallards, which is both harmful and illegal in Florida.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida — in fact endemic to it. Not invasive. (The feral mallards it hybridizes with, however, are non-native to Florida.)

conservation.status: Not federally listed, but a species of real conservation concern in Florida. Hybridization with released mallards, plus wetland loss, have put the pure Florida mottled duck on conservation watch lists.

species.internal_notes: Count includes 3 Mallard × Mottled Duck hybrid obs + 1 genus-level Anas. Hybridization is a real FL conservation story worth telling.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: A female mallard shows white in the tail and a white-bordered blue wing patch; the mottled duck is darker and more uniform, with little to no white and an unmarked buff face.

quick_hits: The wood stork is the only stork that breeds in the United States — a large, quiet wading bird with a bare, scaly head and a heavy, down-curved bill.
It feeds by touch rather than sight: it sweeps its open bill through the water and snaps it shut the instant a fish brushes against it, in a matter of milliseconds.
That method only works where fish are concentrated, so wood storks depend on the shrinking pools of the dry season — which ties them closely to the health of wetlands like the Everglades.
After decades of recovery, the U.S. wood stork was removed from the endangered species list in 2026, though some scientists feel the comeback is still fragile and worth watching.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the Americas, from the southeastern U.S. through Central and South America. In the U.S., breeding is centered in Florida and has expanded north into Georgia and the Carolinas. The Everglades was historically its stronghold.
more_information: The wood stork's feeding method is genuinely unusual. It wades with its bill held open underwater, and the moment a fish touches the inside, the bill snaps shut — a reflex measured in milliseconds. Because it hunts by feel rather than sight, it needs water where fish are packed together, like the pools that shrink as wetlands dry down each season.

That dependence ties the wood stork closely to the health of its wetlands. When water management or drought disrupts the natural dry-down, fish don't gather, and storks may abandon their nests or fail to raise chicks — so how well storks nest tells biologists a great deal about how well the wetlands are working.

The bird's legal status has shifted over the years: listed as endangered in 1984 after steep declines, downlisted to threatened in 2014 as numbers improved, and removed from the U.S. endangered species list in early 2026. It is still protected under other laws, and groups such as Audubon have cautioned that south Florida's population remains vulnerable.

blocks.label: Body
blocks.text: Large and white with black flight feathers, obvious in flight.

blocks.label: Head and neck
blocks.text: Bare, dark gray, and scaly-looking — unfeathered in adults.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Thick, long, and curved downward.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: Neck and legs outstretched (unlike herons, which fold the neck).

identification.what_to_look_for: A big white wading bird with black wing edges and a dark, bald, almost reptilian head and heavy curved bill. If it is flying with its neck stretched straight out, that rules out the similar-sized herons and egrets.

species.diet: Mainly fish, caught by touch in shallow water, along with crayfish, amphibians, and other small aquatic animals concentrated in drying pools.
species.behavior: Active by day, often feeding in groups where fish are concentrated. Soars high on thermals between feeding and nesting sites. Nests in colonies in trees standing over or near water.
species.sounds: Adults are nearly silent — they lack the voice-box muscles for true calls — and communicate mostly by clattering their bills and hissing. Nestlings, by contrast, are noisy.
species.ecological_role: As a touch-feeder that needs concentrated prey, the wood stork is a key indicator of wetland health; thriving storks signal a functioning system. It also helps regulate fish populations in seasonal pools.
species.plant_connections: Nests in waterside trees such as cypress and mangroves.
species.habitat: Fresh and brackish wetlands — marshes, swamps, flooded fields, and pond edges — especially those that dry down seasonally to concentrate fish. Nests in trees, including cypress and mangroves.
species.where_to_look: Around the ponds and any shallow, fish-holding water, sometimes feeding alongside other wading birds. Also worth scanning the sky — wood storks often soar high overhead.
species.when_to_see: Present in Florida year-round, though numbers and movements shift with water levels and the breeding season. A less frequent park visitor than the herons and egrets.

interaction.guidance: Observe from a distance and do not feed it. Wood storks are sensitive to disturbance, especially when feeding or nesting.

conservation.status: Recently recovered: the U.S. wood stork was removed from the federal endangered species list in 2026 after rebounding from its 1984 endangered listing. It stays protected under other laws, and some conservationists consider south Florida's population still fragile and closely tied to Everglades restoration.

species.internal_notes: Federally listed history. Status verified 2026-06-19: ESA-delisted (Southeast U.S. DPS), effective March 2026, with a 10-year post-delisting monitoring plan; content reflects this.

seasonality.note: Numbers shift with water levels; a less frequent visitor than the herons and egrets.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The white pelican has an enormous orange bill and pouch; the wood stork is smaller-billed with a bare, scaly dark head and long legs that trail in flight.
The white ibis is much smaller with a curved red bill; the wood stork is far larger with black flight feathers and a bare dark head.

also_known_as: Great White Heron (white form, south Florida)
quick_hits: Standing about four feet tall with a sharp, prehistoric silhouette, the great blue heron is the largest heron in our wetlands.
Like the egret, its main tool is patience — it waits motionless, then unfolds its neck in a fast strike — but its diet is much broader.
It hunts fish in the shallows but is just as happy stalking open fields and grassy banks for frogs, snakes, and rodents, so you may spot one far from any water.
Travel south toward the Florida Keys and you may meet an all-white version of this same bird — long thought to be its own species, now treated simply as a different color form.
species.range_and_origin: Native across most of North and Central America. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident found at virtually any body of water, from wild marshes to suburban retention ponds.
more_information: For all its size, the great blue heron is built around patience. It will stand statue-still in shallow water, sometimes for many minutes, watching, before its coiled neck snaps forward to catch a fish.
It is also more versatile than most herons. Away from the water it will stalk fields and lawns for rodents, snakes, and large insects, which is why you might see one far from any pond.

South Florida is home to the striking great white heron, an all-white form once classified as its own species. Today scientists treat it as a white morph of the great blue heron — same bird, different wardrobe — most common in the Keys and Florida Bay.

blocks.label: Size
blocks.text: Very large — around four feet tall with a roughly six-foot wingspan.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Blue-gray body, a mostly white face with a black plume stripe over the eye, and a heavy yellowish bill.

blocks.label: In flight
blocks.text: Neck folded into an S, long legs trailing behind, slow deep wingbeats.

blocks.label: White morph (south Florida)
blocks.text: Entirely white, but with the same size, shape, and heavy bill.

identification.what_to_look_for: A tall blue-gray bird standing motionless or wading slowly in the shallows. In flight, the folded neck is the giveaway that separates herons from cranes and storks, which fly with necks outstretched.

species.diet: Mainly fish, but a true generalist — also frogs, snakes, turtles, large insects, and small mammals like rats and gophers, taken on land as well as in water.
species.behavior: Usually solitary while hunting, though it nests in colonies. Active mostly by day but will also hunt at dusk and after dark. Defends a feeding area and either stalks slowly or stands and waits, depending on the prey.
species.sounds: Mostly silent, but when alarmed or flushed it lets out a deep, harsh, far-carrying squawk.
species.ecological_role: A top predator of shallow waters and wetland edges, helping keep fish, amphibian, and rodent numbers in balance across a wide range of habitats.
species.habitat: Almost any wetland or water edge — marshes, ponds, lakes, rivers, coastlines — plus nearby fields and lawns where it hunts small animals.
species.where_to_look: Along the pond banks and shallows, standing still or wading slowly. Sometimes seen well away from water, stalking the lawns for prey.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Visible throughout the day, usually hunting alone.

danger.people: Harmless to visitors. As with all big herons, a frightened bird can strike toward the face with its bill, so injured ones should be left to trained rehabbers.
danger.pets: Not a danger under normal circumstances; just do not let a dog harass or corner one.

interaction.guidance: Keep your distance and do not feed it. A hunting heron needs quiet and space; getting too close sends it flying off hungry.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Common and widespread, and comfortable around people and human-made ponds.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The great egret is all white, slimmer, and a bit smaller, with a yellow bill and black legs; the great blue is gray-blue and larger.

quick_hits: The monarch is a familiar sight in Florida's open spaces and gardens — bright orange wings laced with heavy black veins.
Its caterpillars eat only milkweed, a plant whose toxins they store in their bodies, making both caterpillar and adult unpalatable, even poisonous, to predators.
Each fall, monarchs from the eastern U.S. and Canada fly thousands of miles to spend the winter in the mountains of central Mexico.
No single butterfly makes the whole round trip — the journey is a relay across several generations, with one long-lived late-summer generation doing the marathon south.
species.range_and_origin: Native and widespread across North America, and now found in many warm regions worldwide. In Florida monarchs are present much of the year; the state hosts both migrating monarchs and a resident, year-round breeding population, especially in the south.
more_information: The monarch's life revolves around two things: milkweed and migration. Females lay their eggs only on milkweed, and the caterpillars eat nothing else. In doing so they take in the plant's bitter, heart-affecting toxins and become distasteful to birds and other predators — the bright orange is essentially a warning label.

The migration is what the monarch is best known for. Each fall, monarchs from the eastern half of the continent funnel down to a handful of mountain forests in central Mexico, where they gather in enormous numbers. The trip is really a relay: most monarchs live only a few weeks, but the generation that emerges in late summer is different, living six months or more to complete the long journey south and begin the way back.

Monarch numbers have fallen sharply in recent decades, mostly from the loss of milkweed and habitat. The species is listed as Vulnerable internationally, and U.S. wildlife officials proposed protecting it under the Endangered Species Act in 2024 — a decision that, as of early 2026, has been repeatedly delayed. It is a small part of why the park keeps native milkweed and nectar flowers growing: a rest stop and nursery along the way.

blocks.label: Wings
blocks.text: Bright orange with heavy black veins and a black border dotted with white spots.

blocks.label: Male vs. female
blocks.text: Males have a small black scent spot on a hindwing vein and slightly thinner veins; females have thicker veins and no spot.

blocks.label: Caterpillar
blocks.text: Boldly banded in yellow, black, and white, found on milkweed.

blocks.label: Look-alike
blocks.text: The viceroy butterfly mimics the monarch but is smaller and has an extra black line across the hindwing.

identification.what_to_look_for: A large orange-and-black butterfly nectaring on flowers or drifting through the garden. To be sure it is a monarch and not a viceroy, check the hindwing — a viceroy has a curved black line crossing it that the monarch lacks.

species.diet: Caterpillars eat only milkweed leaves. Adults sip nectar from a wide range of flowers and also take water and minerals from damp soil.
species.behavior: Active by day, especially in warm sunshine. Adults flutter and glide between nectar sources; females search out milkweed on which to lay eggs. Migrating monarchs travel by day and roost in clusters at night. The full life cycle runs egg, caterpillar, chrysalis, adult.
species.sounds: Silent. Like other butterflies, monarchs make no calls.
species.ecological_role: A pollinator that moves pollen between flowers as it feeds, and a textbook example of a plant-insect partnership through its reliance on milkweed. It is also a flagship species for pollinator and habitat conservation.
species.plant_connections: Milkweed (Asclepias spp.) is the only food of its caterpillars and the anchor of its life cycle; adults also nectar at many flowers.

size.weight: about half a gram — extremely light
size.lifespan: most adults live 2 to 6 weeks; the migratory fall generation lives 6 to 9 months.

species.habitat: Open, sunny places with flowers and milkweed — meadows, gardens, roadsides, marsh edges, and parks. Breeding depends entirely on the presence of milkweed.
species.where_to_look: Around the flower beds and any milkweed plantings, especially on warm, sunny days. Check nectar-rich blooms for feeding adults and milkweed leaves for caterpillars.
species.when_to_see: Present much of the year in Florida, with both migratory and resident breeding monarchs. Most numerous during spring and fall movements and in warm, flowery months.

danger.people: Completely harmless to people. The toxins that protect monarchs only matter to animals that try to eat them.
danger.pets: Not a concern. A pet that ate one might find it bitter or get a mild stomach upset, but monarchs pose no real danger.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. Avoid handling butterflies, whose wings are delicate, and the best help you can offer is planting native milkweed and nectar flowers.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and North America. Not invasive.

conservation.status: A species of growing concern. The migratory monarch is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN, and U.S. officials have proposed listing it as Threatened under the Endangered Species Act; as of early 2026 that decision remains delayed. Habitat and milkweed loss are the main drivers of its decline.

species.internal_notes: Ties to milkweed plantings (host plant) and the park's Monarch Waystation story.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The viceroy is smaller and has an extra black line arcing across the hindwing that the monarch lacks.
The queen is a darker chestnut with less black veining than the monarch.
The gulf fritillary has narrower wings and brilliant silver spangles underneath; the monarch is broad-winged with heavy black veins and no silver.

quick_hits: The great egret hunts through sheer stillness, waiting motionless in the shallows until a fish drifts close, then uncoiling its neck in a split-second strike.
Standing over three feet tall and entirely snow white, it is hard to miss at the water's edge.
A little over a century ago it was nearly wiped out — hunted in breeding season for the lacy plumes on its back, which were prized for women's hats.
The outrage over that loss helped spark the early conservation movement, and the great egret became the symbol of the National Audubon Society.
species.range_and_origin: Found on every continent except Antarctica. In North America it is widespread, and in Florida it is a common, year-round resident of marshes, ponds, and shorelines wherever there are fish to catch.
more_information: In the late 1800s, fashion nearly cost the great egret its existence. During breeding season the bird grows long, lacy plumes called aigrettes, and those feathers became must-have decorations on stylish hats. Plume hunters raided nesting colonies, often killing adults and leaving the chicks to die.

The public backlash was a turning point. Early Audubon societies formed in protest, and landmark laws — the Lacey Act of 1900 and the Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918 — eventually shut down the feather trade.

The great egret recovered so fully that the National Audubon Society adopted it as its emblem. Its abundance in the park today is a gentle reminder that landscapes can heal when given a collective chance — and that protections, once won, still have to be maintained.

blocks.label: Plumage
blocks.text: Entirely white.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Long, dagger-shaped, and yellow.

blocks.label: Legs and feet
blocks.text: Black, top to bottom.

blocks.label: Breeding adults
blocks.text: Grow long, wispy plumes on the back and develop a greenish patch of skin on the face.

identification.what_to_look_for: A large, all-white wading bird with a yellow bill and black legs, standing motionless or stalking slowly through shallow water. The yellow bill plus black legs separate it from the smaller snowy egret, which has a black bill and bright yellow feet.

species.diet: Mostly fish, plus frogs, crayfish, insects, small reptiles, and the occasional small mammal — caught with a fast jab of that dagger bill.
species.behavior: A patient, visual hunter. It wades slowly or stands frozen in the shallows, watching, then strikes in an instant. Active by day, it nests in colonies, often mixed with other wading birds, and flies with slow, deep wingbeats and its neck folded into an S.
species.sounds: Usually quiet away from the colony. When disturbed it gives a low, hoarse croak; nesting birds are noisier.
species.ecological_role: A top shallow-water predator that helps regulate fish and amphibian populations. As a colony nester sensitive to wetland health, it is also a living indicator of how well an ecosystem like the Everglades is doing.

size.wingspan: up to about 5.5 ft (52 to 67 in)

species.habitat: Shallow fresh and salt water — marshes, ponds, lake edges, mudflats, and coastal shorelines — with open water for hunting and trees or reeds for nesting.
species.where_to_look: At the edges of the ponds, standing still in the shallows or stalking slowly along the bank. A tall white bird holding a statue-like pose over the water is almost always this species.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Often most active hunting in the cooler hours of morning and late afternoon.

danger.people: Harmless to visitors. The one caveat is for wildlife rehabbers: a frightened heron or egret can jab its sharp bill toward the eyes, so injured birds should only be handled by trained people.
danger.pets: No real danger to pets, though it is best to keep dogs from chasing or cornering any wading bird.

interaction.guidance: Admire from a distance and never feed it. Crowding a hunting egret ruins its chance at a meal and stresses the bird.

conservation.status: Least Concern today — a remarkable recovery from near-extinction in the early 1900s, thanks to the bird-protection laws its plight helped create.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: A rare all-white great blue heron is bulkier with pale legs and a heavier bill; the great egret is slim, with black legs and feet and a yellow bill.
The snowy egret is much smaller with a black bill and bright yellow feet; the great egret is tall with a yellow bill and all-black legs and feet.

quick_hits: Floating along the marshy edges of the ponds is a slate-gray bird with a red shield on its forehead — a bit like a swimming chicken.
It is often taken for a duck, but a look at its feet gives it away: it belongs to the rail family and has no webbing at all.
Instead it has long, widely splayed toes that work like snowshoes, spreading its weight so it can walk across lily pads and floating plants without sinking.
It is also one of the more talkative residents of the wetland, filling the quiet with an unexpected mix of clucks, squawks, and odd, laughing chatter.
species.range_and_origin: Native across the Americas, from the United States south through South America. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident of freshwater marshes, ponds, and any quiet water with thick edge vegetation.
more_information: The common gallinule is often mistaken for a duck because of how it swims, pumping its head as it goes, but it belongs to the rail family — and a look at its feet gives it away.

Where a duck has webbing, the gallinule has long, splayed toes. They spread its weight out so it can clamber over lily pads and matted vegetation, picking at food along the way without breaking through.
Until 2011 this bird was lumped with a close Old World relative under the name Common Moorhen. Scientists then split them into separate species, and the New World bird became the Common Gallinule.

blocks.label: Adults
blocks.text: Slate-gray body, brownish back, a bold white stripe along the flanks, and a red frontal shield with a yellow-tipped red bill.

blocks.label: Legs and feet
blocks.text: Yellow-green with very long, unwebbed toes.

blocks.label: Young
blocks.text: Duller and browner, lacking the bright red bill.

identification.what_to_look_for: A dark, hen-sized water bird swimming with a head-bobbing motion, showing a vivid red-and-yellow bill and a white side stripe. If it climbs onto floating plants on long, skinny toes rather than webbed feet, you have confirmed a gallinule, not a duck.

species.diet: An opportunist — seeds, leaves, and stems of water plants, plus insects, snails, and other small aquatic creatures, gathered while swimming, dabbling, or walking on vegetation.
species.behavior: Active by day, usually near cover at the water's edge. Swims, walks on floating plants, and ducks into the reeds when alarmed. Often seen in ones and twos, and can be quite vocal.
species.sounds: Loud and varied for its size: sharp clucks, squawks, and a whinnying, laughing series of notes.
species.ecological_role: Eats and spreads wetland plants and helps keep aquatic insect and snail numbers in check. A dependable presence in healthy freshwater marshes.
species.plant_connections: Feeds on and moves through marsh plants — cattails, reeds, and floating vegetation such as lily pads.
species.habitat: Freshwater marshes, ponds, lakes, and slow canals with plenty of cattails, reeds, and floating vegetation along the edges.
species.where_to_look: Along the vegetated margins of the ponds — watch where plants meet open water, and listen for its loud calls from the reeds.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Most easily found near pond edges with thick plant cover.

interaction.guidance: Watch from a distance and do not feed it. Gallinules are shy by nature and slip into cover when crowded.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the Americas. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Widespread and common in suitable wetland habitat.

species.internal_notes: Count includes 2 subspecies-level observations folded in.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The American coot has a white bill and forehead shield and lobed toes; the gallinule has a red shield, a yellow-tipped red bill, and a white stripe along the flank.

quick_hits: If you spot a flock of bright white birds probing a wet lawn with long, curved red bills, those are white ibises.
They do not start out white. Juveniles are a mottled, unassuming brown, and only grow into their clean white plumage — with black wingtips — as they mature.
That curved bill is a sensitive probe, so the ibis does not need clear water to feed; it sweeps through mud and shallows by feel, snapping up hidden crayfish, crabs, and insects.
Local folklore holds that the ibis is the last bird to take shelter before a storm and the first to venture back out afterward — which is why it is sometimes seen as a quiet symbol of resilience.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the southeastern United States, the Caribbean, and parts of Central and South America. In Florida it is a common, year-round resident of marshes, wet lawns, and shorelines, and one of the most abundant wading birds in the state.
more_information: White ibises are social birds, usually seen in flocks feeding, flying in long lines, and roosting together in trees at night, often alongside herons and egrets.

Their curved bill is essentially a sensitive probe. Rather than hunting by sight, an ibis sweeps it through mud and shallow water, feeling for prey and snapping shut the instant it makes contact, which lets it feed even in murky water.

The University of Miami connection is real: the school's mascot, Sebastian the Ibis, was chosen because folklore holds that the ibis is the last wildlife to leave before a hurricane and the first to return — a fitting emblem of riding out the storm.

blocks.label: Adults
blocks.text: All white with black wingtips (usually visible only in flight) and a long, down-curved bill, with red bill, face, and legs.

blocks.label: Juveniles
blocks.text: Mostly brown above with a white belly, gradually molting to white over their first couple of years.

blocks.label: Feeding style
blocks.text: Head down, bill probing the mud and shallows.

identification.what_to_look_for: A medium-sized white bird with a long, downward-curving red-pink bill, walking through wet grass or shallows with its head down. Black wingtips flash when it flies. A brown-and-white bird with the same curved bill is a young ibis, not a different species.

species.diet: Crayfish and crabs are favorites, along with insects, small fish, snails, and other small aquatic animals found by probing mud and shallow water.
species.behavior: Active by day and highly social, feeding and roosting in flocks. Often seen probing wet lawns and pond edges, sometimes in surprising numbers. Flies in lines or V-formations between feeding and roosting sites.
species.sounds: Generally quiet. Gives soft grunts and honking calls, and is more vocal at nesting and roosting colonies.
species.ecological_role: By feeding heavily on crayfish and other invertebrates, ibises help shape wetland food webs. They are a classic sign of healthy, productive marsh and shoreline habitat.
species.habitat: Shallow wetlands of all kinds — marshes, mudflats, flooded fields, pond edges, and coastal shorelines — plus well-watered lawns and parks.
species.where_to_look: On the lawns and grassy edges near the ponds, especially after rain, often in small flocks probing the wet ground with their curved bills.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Frequently seen in flocks, and often most active feeding on damp ground in the morning.

interaction.guidance: Please do not feed them. Ibises do best foraging naturally, and handouts (especially bread) are not good for them. Watch and enjoy from a comfortable distance.

conservation.status: Least Concern. White ibises are abundant and widespread, and their numbers are considered secure.

quick_hits: Watch the water for a slim black bird swimming with only its snakelike neck above the surface — that is the anhinga, and the pose earns it the nickname snakebird.
Unlike ducks, the anhinga's feathers are not waterproof, and that turns out to be the key to how it hunts.
Waterlogged feathers make the bird less buoyant, so it can sink low and stalk fish beneath the surface instead of bobbing on top.
Because it gets soaked to the skin, you will often see one afterward perched with its wings spread wide like a cape, drying out in the sun.
species.range_and_origin: Native to the warm wetlands of the Americas, from the southeastern United States through Central and South America. In the U.S. it is a year-round resident across Florida and the Gulf Coast, favoring fresh and brackish water with plenty of fish and perches.
more_information: The anhinga earns its snakebird nickname honestly. As it swims, its body rides below the surface and only the long, thin neck shows, giving it a serpentine look that has fooled many a passerby.

Most water birds keep their feathers waterproof with oils from a special gland. The anhinga does not — its feathers wet through, and combined with unusually dense bones, that lets it settle to near-neutral buoyancy and slip quietly underwater to stalk fish, rather than floating high like a duck.

When it strikes, the anhinga's neck does something remarkable. A hinge-like joint between the eighth and ninth neck bones acts like a coiled spring, snapping the head forward to spear a fish on its sharp bill. Larger fish are speared; smaller ones are gripped in the finely serrated bill. The bird then surfaces, tosses the catch into the air, and swallows it head-first.

Because its feathers soak through, the anhinga loses body heat in the water and cannot fly well while wet — which is why it spends so much time perched with wings spread, drying and warming in the sun.

blocks.label: Bill
blocks.text: Long, straight, and sharply pointed — built for spearing, not grabbing.

blocks.label: Neck
blocks.text: Very long and thin, often the only part visible while swimming.

blocks.label: Plumage
blocks.text: Males are glossy black with silver-white streaks on the wings; females and young have a pale tan head and neck.

blocks.label: Tail
blocks.text: Long and fan-shaped, longer than a cormorant's.

identification.what_to_look_for: A long-necked black bird either swimming low with just its head and neck showing, or perched with wings fully spread to dry. Pale-necked birds are females and juveniles. Compared with the similar cormorant, the anhinga has a straight, pointed bill (the cormorant's is hooked) and a longer tail.

species.diet: Mainly fish — larger ones speared on its sharp bill, smaller ones gripped in the serrated edges — plus the occasional aquatic invertebrate or small amphibian.
species.behavior: Active by day. Hunts by swimming submerged and ambushing fish, then perches in the open to dry its wings and warm up. A strong soaring flier, it often rides thermals high overhead. Nests in colonies, frequently alongside herons and egrets.
species.sounds: Usually silent. Around nesting colonies it makes clicking, rattling, and croaking sounds.
species.ecological_role: A specialized fish predator that helps keep fish populations in balance. By nesting in mixed colonies with herons, egrets, and ibises, it is part of the larger wading-bird community that depends on healthy wetlands.
species.habitat: Calm fresh and brackish water — ponds, lakes, slow rivers, marshes, and mangrove edges — with fish to hunt and branches or snags for perching and drying.
species.where_to_look: On and around the ponds. Scan the open water for a swimming snakebird, and check waterside branches, posts, and snags for one drying its wings in the sun.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Often easiest to spot in the morning when birds are perched and drying their wings.

danger.people: Harmless to people. That spear-like bill is for fish; anhingas keep their distance and pose no threat to visitors.

interaction.guidance: Enjoy from a distance and please do not feed it or any park wildlife. Give perched, wing-drying birds space — pushing one off its sunning spot makes it harder for the bird to warm up.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida and the Americas. Not invasive.

conservation.status: Least Concern. Anhingas are widespread and stable across their range.

species.internal_notes: Observed by 9 different people — broadest community appeal in the dataset.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The cormorant has a hook-tipped bill and a shorter tail; the anhinga's bill is straight and dagger-like, its tail long and fan-shaped, and it shows silvery patches on the wings.

quick_hits: If you see a small brown lizard doing push-ups on a fence post or flashing a reddish-orange throat fan, that is a brown anole — almost certainly the most common lizard you will meet anywhere in Florida.
It is not from here. Brown anoles arrived from Cuba and the Bahamas in the late 1800s and have since spread into nearly every county in the state.
Their success came at a cost to Florida's native green anole, which now spends more time high in the trees to stay out of the newcomer's way.
Males stake out territory with a stretchy throat flap called a dewlap and a bobbing push-up display — body language that says this spot is taken.
species.range_and_origin: Native to Cuba, the Bahamas, and nearby Caribbean islands. Brown anoles first turned up in the Florida Keys around 1887, likely hitching rides in cargo and on nursery plants, and have spread through nearly the entire state. Today they are considered one of Florida's most abundant land vertebrates.
more_information: The brown anole's arrival reshaped life for Florida's only native anole, the green anole. The two lizards want the same things — similar food, similar perches, similar sunny spots — so where browns move in, greens tend to move higher, shifting up into shrubs and trees. Brown anoles will also eat green anole hatchlings and eggs, which adds to the pressure.

There is a fascinating wrinkle to the story. Scientists have watched green anoles evolve larger, stickier toepads over just a few generations — an adaptation that helps them grip the higher, smoother perches they have been pushed onto. It is one of the clearest real-world examples of rapid evolution happening right under our noses.

The brown anole isn't doing anything wrong; it is simply very good at being a lizard in Florida. But its quiet, ongoing story is a nice reminder of how an introduced species can reshape life for the natives already here — unfolding right in our own garden beds.

blocks.label: Color
blocks.text: Shades of brown, tan, or gray, often with a pattern of triangles, diamonds, or a light stripe down the back. Unlike the green anole, it cannot turn green.

blocks.label: Throat fan (dewlap)
blocks.text: Males flash a reddish-orange dewlap edged in yellow during displays.

blocks.label: Body
blocks.text: Stocky, with a fairly short pointed snout and ridged (keeled) scales that give the back a slightly rough look.

blocks.label: Behavior cue
blocks.text: Usually seen low down — on the ground, fences, walls, and the base of trees — doing push-up displays.

identification.what_to_look_for: A small brown lizard perched head-down on a post or trunk, bobbing up and down. If it flashes an orange throat fan, it is a displaying male. The one thing it will never do is turn bright green — that is the native green anole, which tends to stay higher up.

species.diet: Insects and other small invertebrates — beetles, ants, flies, spiders, and just about anything it can catch and fit in its mouth. It will also eat the eggs and young of other lizards, including the native green anole.
species.behavior: Active by day and almost always low to the ground. Males are territorial and constantly signal with head-bobbing push-ups and dewlap flashes. When threatened, an anole can drop its tail to escape a predator, then slowly regrow it. They bask to warm up and are most visible on warm, sunny days.
species.sounds: Essentially silent. Anoles communicate with body language — push-ups and the colorful dewlap — rather than sound.
species.ecological_role: A major link in the local food chain. Brown anoles eat enormous numbers of insects and are in turn eaten by birds, snakes, larger lizards, and cats. Their sheer abundance makes them an important food source, but that same abundance is what pressures the native green anole.

size.length: 5 to 8 in (13 to 20 cm), including the tail; males are larger than females.
size.lifespan: usually 2 to 4 years in the wild.

species.habitat: Sunny, open spots low to the ground — tree trunks, fences, walls, garden edges, leaf litter, and shrubbery. Thrives in yards, parks, and other human-altered landscapes.
species.where_to_look: Almost everywhere on warm days — fence rails, tree trunks, sidewalk edges, and sunny garden borders. Watch low surfaces for a brown lizard doing push-ups.
species.when_to_see: Year-round resident. Most active and visible on warm, sunny days; scarce during cold snaps.

danger.people: Harmless. Brown anoles have no venom and will not hurt you. A cornered one might nip if picked up, but it cannot break skin in any meaningful way.
danger.pets: Not dangerous to pets. Cats and dogs often chase them; the lizard is far more at risk than the pet.

interaction.guidance: Just watch and enjoy. No need to feed or handle them — they are doing fine on their own.

invasive.notes: Introduced and invasive in Florida. Native to Cuba and the Bahamas, established in the state since the late 1800s, and now one of its most abundant lizards. Its spread is linked to the decline of the native green anole through competition and predation on green anole young.

conservation.status: Not a conservation concern — brown anoles are extremely abundant. If anything, the concern runs the other way, toward the native green anole they outcompete.

species.internal_notes: Most-observed animal in the export (23 obs). Strong 'introduced species' interpretive angle. Framing resolved — treated as invasive (invasive=Red), tied to the documented decline of the native green anole.

seasonality.note: Most active on warm, sunny days; scarce in cold snaps.

similar_species.how_to_tell_apart: The green anole can turn bright green and has a pink throat fan and climbs higher; the brown anole never turns green, flashes a reddish-orange fan, and stays low.
